title: KEWISH v IOM INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS SCHOOL LTD
text:
Proceedings




[1] By Statement of Claim brought against the Isle of Man International Business School Limited (the Defendant) , Karen Lorraine Kewish (the Plaintiff) seeks damages for personal injury, pain, suffering, loss of amenity and losses suffered as a result thereof. She claims that such injury was caused by the Defendantâs negligence.




Representation




[2] Mr. K. Goldie appeared for the Plaintiff, and Mrs C. Weatherill for the Defendant.




General Background 




[3] The Plaintiff is employed as a management reporting team leader by Clerical Medical Insurance. On 22nd January 2002, together with nine other colleagues she attended at the Isle of Man International Business School (the Premises), which is owned, and occupied by the Defendant, for a three day course under the tuition of Mr. Geoffrey Brown of Interact Development Training. The Plaintiff had not previously visited the Premises. The course was held in room LG24, which is situate on the lower ground floor. 




[4] Once assembled in room LG24, the course attendees were informed that on the last day of the course they would be required to give Powerpoint presentations to their managers, and Mr. Brown. Since room LG24 was considered too small for such presentations, they were informed that another room (room LG5) would be made available. It was indicated that they would visit such room at the start of the scheduled morning coffee break at 10.30am.




[5] A floor plan of the lower ground floor of the Premises shows that to access room LG5 from room LG24 requires a person to walk along a corridor, descend three steps (the subject stairs) to a lower level corridor at the end of which is situate the entrance to room LG5. For my purposes, I shall describe the top of the subject stairs as level 1, the going of the first step as level 2, the going of the second step as level 3, and the lower corridor level as level 4.




[6] The Plaintiff alleges that, whilst descending the subject stairs en route to room LG5, she fell sustaining fracture dislocation of her right ankle being a fracture of the distal fibula , and medial malleolus with a small flake fracture of the posterior malleolus . The Plaintiff claims that such injuries were caused by the negligence of the Defendant, its servants, or agents.




[7] The Defendant denies that it, its servants, or agents were negligent, and pleads that it took such care as in all the circumstances was reasonable to ensure that the Plaintiff was reasonably safe attending the Premises. In the alternative, if primary liability is found to rest with the Defendant, the latter pleads that the Plaintiff was contributorily negligent.




The Primary Issue




[8] The first issue to determine is whether the Defendant, its servants, or agents were negligent. If negligence is found, then I shall consider whether the Plaintiff was contributorily negligent. 




The Plaintiffâs evidence




[9] I turn to the Plaintiffâs evidence to the point when she was removed to hospital by ambulance.




[10] According to the Plaintiff, at the commencement of the coffee break, Mr. Brown together with seven students left room LG24. They were then followed by Deborah Julie Stephens, and Lorraine Quayle with the Plaintiff leaving the room last. As she walked along the corridor towards the subject stairs, Mrs. Stephens and Lorraine Quayle were some feet in front. They were talking with each other, but the Plaintiff could not hear what they were saying. The Plaintiff recalled making a favourable comment about the course towards Mrs. Stephens and Lorraine Quayle . At the time, the Plaintiff stated that she wore flat orthopaedic shoes for comfort, that she walked along the corridor towards the subject stairs at a standard walking pace, and that she was not carrying anything.




[11] The Plaintiff stated that she became aware of the subject stairs, because she saw Mrs. Stephens and Lorraine Quayle at the bottom. According to the Plaintiff, by that time, there was not much distance between herself, and her two colleagues. The Plaintiff stated that she was not talking to Mrs. Stephens or Lorraine Quayle as she descended the subject stairs. She thought she descended towards the right hand side of the same. However, she could not be certain, because her recollection of the incident was not good. In her witness statement, the Plaintiff stated that she is now not clear as to what is her actual memory of the accident and what she has been told since the accident. The Plaintiff stated that she believed that she was stepping from level 3 to level 4 when her right foot ârolledâ . She further believed that she had not experienced any problems descending from level 1 to 2, or level 2 to 3. She stated that all steps were steep, and uneven with each step steeper than the last: she could not adopt her natural gait. Whilst descending, the Plaintiff stated that she was looking where she was going. Although she could not recall precisely where she was looking, it was her custom to look a few feet ahead. The Plaintiff recalled that she put her right foot down towards level 4, but she could not find anything. Her foot rolled to the right, she reached out to grab something, the nearest thing was Mrs. Stephens, then she fell to the floor.




[12] The Plaintiff described the incident as being over in a flash. She believed that the incident was caused by the subject stairs being too steep, and uneven. She further believed that each step was deeper than the last.




[13] Mr. Brown attended the scene, together with some staff members from adjoining rooms. One such member attempted first aid by trying to straighten the Plaintiffâs foot, which had twisted behind her. It proved too painful to move. Accompanied by Mrs. Stephens, the Plaintiff was transported by ambulance to Nobleâs Hospital Accident and Emergency Department. 




[14] During her evidence, the Plaintiff was shown some photographs taken by Adrian Mooney on 25th January 2002. Such photographs show the subject stairs, part of the corridor leading from room LG24, and part of the lower corridor leading to room LG5. The Plaintiff identified the subject stairs, and indicated that in material terms the area as shown in the photographs was the same as at the time of the incident.




[15] Mrs. Stephens reported to the Plaintiff that, on her return to the Premises at lunchtime that day, signs had been erected warning of the presence of the subject stairs. In her witness statement, the Plaintiff stated that since there was no handrail to grab onto, she flailed forward and fell onto her colleague Deborah Stephens. Following the incident handrails have been fitted each side of the subject stairs. 




[16] In her witness statement, the Plaintiff stated that following the incident she later learned that the subject stairs were uneven in height, and depth. During her evidence the Plaintiff was referred to an expert report by Ian Copley , which report recorded Mr. Copleyâs measurements of the subject stairs. Such measurements indicate that level 1/2 rise measures 205mm, level 2/3 rise 200mm, and level 3/4 rise 198mm, and that level 2/3 going measures 276mm with level 3/4 307mm. The report also indicates that the subject stairs width is 1450mm average, with a pitch of 33 to 36.6 degrees. Whilst the Plaintiff accepted such measurements, she confirmed that at the time the subject stairs felt steep.




Deborah Julie Stephensâ evidence




[17] At the time of the incident, three people were on or within the immediate vicinity of the subject stairs: the Plaintiff, Lorraine Quayle and Deborah Julie Stephens. Mrs. Stephens gave evidence. At the time she was a work colleague of the Plaintiff, and was attending the same three day course. 




[18] In her witness statement, Mrs. Stephens described that part of the Premises in which the subject stairs are situate as âold and not renovatedâ . In evidence she stated that at the time of the incident, there were no handrails , or warning signs relevant to the subject stairs. She described the floor as uneven. She could not remember if the corridors, and subject stairs were carpeted. 




[19] According to Mrs. Stephens, she was the last person to leave room LG24. She was not sure as to the Plaintiffâs exact whereabouts when she left the room. However, she recollected that as she walked towards the subject stairs, she was positioned to the left of the corridor, just behind her and to her right was Lorraine Quayle with the Plaintiff between Lorraine Quayle and Mrs. Stephens, the latter being slightly in front. She could not recollect whether the Plaintiff was side by side with Lorraine Quayle . Mrs. Stephens described the Plaintiff and Lorraine Quayle as having a âgeneral chit chatâ . She stated that she was not really talking with the Plaintiff or Lorraine Quayle . She described the subject stairs that they might have been steeper than modern steps; they were old steps made of stone; they might have been carpeted. The steps were âpossibly unevenâ , she was not sure if they were uneven or not. 




[20] In response to Mrs. Weatherill , Mrs. Stephens stated that she did not fall down the subject stairs. She could see them, and had seen others descend them: she was aware of the location of the subject stairs. She stated that the Plaintiff was behind her talking with Lorraine Quayle , they were the last two of the group walking towards room LG5. They were fairly close behind her. Mrs. Stephens was not able to state the Plaintiffâs precise position on the subject stairs when the Plaintiff fell. She did not see her fall. Mrs. Stephens said that she might have been then stood on level 4 or level 3. However, she was certain that she had descended two steps. She stated that the Plaintiff collided with the rear of her right shoulder, pushing her forward. Mrs. Stephens steadied herself against the wall. In cross examination, she stated that she was certain that she had at all times walked and descended the subject stairs on the left hand side and that she had steadied herself against the left hand side wall. The Plaintiff had pushed her, and tried to grab her. Mrs. Stephens stated that she thought the Plaintiff was wearing fairly flat slip on court shoes with a 1â heel. Neither the Plaintiff nor Mrs. Stephens were carrying anything in their hands. 




[21] As to her recollection of the incident, Mrs. Stephens stated that whilst she could not remember the particular subject stairs, nor clearly what shoes the Plaintiff wore, her recollection of what had happened was good.




The Plaintiff, and Mrs Stephens evidence.




[22] The Plaintiff and Mrs. Stephens gave their respective evidence fairly and frankly. In her oral evidence, and in her witness statement, which she confirmed, the Plaintiff stated that she was now unclear as to what was her memory of the incident and what she had been told after the incident. In her witness statement, she stated that she believed the incident occurred as she stepped down to level 4, which she described as steeper than she had expected in comparison to her natural gait, and that she had learnt later that the steps were of uneven height and depth. She had felt her ankle give and her foot roll. 




[23] Notwithstanding that when shown Mr. Mooneyâs photographs of the corridor, and subject stairs, Mrs. Stephens stated that she was not certain that such stairs were in fact the stairs where the incident occurred, but that they looked like the subject stairs, I prefer her recollection of events immediately prior to, and at the time of the incident to that of the Plaintiff. As a finding of fact, I find that as the Plaintiff, Lorraine Quayle and Mrs. Stephens approached the subject stairs, Mrs. Stephens was walking to the left side of the corridor, slightly behind her and to the right hand side of the corridor was Lorraine Quayle with the Plaintiff towards the middle, and slightly behind Mrs. Stephens. I accept the Plaintiffâs evidence, and find that she was stepping from level 3 to level 4 immediately before she fell. I find that as she fell she tried to grab Mrs. Stephens, who received a push to the rear of her right shoulder, but was able to steady herself on the left hand side wall at level 4. The Plaintiff then fell to the floor. Thus, the Plaintiff was not positioned as she thought towards the right hand side of the subject stairs leading from room LG24, but at or very near the centre of level 3 descending to level 4 when she fell.




[24] The Plaintiff, and Mrs Stephens were the only witnesses, who gave evidence as to the incident. The Plaintiff called Mr Crerand to give medical evidence. The latter was the only witness to give such evidence. In its defence, the Defendant called Martin John Gershman , Mr. Mooney and Mr. Copley .




Martin John Gershmanâs evidence




[25] From 1st January 2000 until 13th August 2004, Mr. Gershman was employed as Registrar at the International Business School. As such, he was responsible for administration and support, the Premises, and health and safety issues. Mr. Gershman confirmed that from 1st January 2000 until the school opened in July that year, he was engaged in preparatory work. With reference to the subject stairs, and corridors, Mr. Gershman stated that following an inspection by the Fire and Rescue Authority, a new false ceiling was constructed. He further stated that âthe electricsâ were replaced, new lighting installed to the requisite standard for a public building, new carpet was laid, and non-slip nosings fitted to the steps of the subject stairs.




[26] Mr. Gershman did not witness the incident. His then office was situate on the right hand side of the corridor immediately before the commencement of the subject stairs. Following the incident, he carried out a visual inspection of the subject stairs. He stated that the non-slip nosings were in good condition, there was nothing wrong with the carpet, and there was no sign of any spillage , or anything else, which could have caused the incident. Mr. Gershmanâs observations were reflected in his Health and Safety at Work Inspectorate Report, and his Isle of Man Government Accident Report. 




[27] Such Health and Safety at Work Inspectorate Report indicates that it was completed at 10.45 a.m. on 22nd January 2002. It records that the Plaintiff informed Mr. Gershman that she âdid notice the subject steps but does not know how she missed her footingâ .Under Part F âAction Takenâ , Mr. Gershman records:-




âThe safety signage in the building is recent and has been inspected to confirm that it complies with statutory requirements. No requirement had been identified to signpost the stairs in question. However, to try to avoid similar incidents in the future, temporary warning signs have already been erected and an order has been placed for the installation of handrails on both walls on either side of the corridor to draw attention to the steps.â




[28] Mr. Gershman stated that there had been a dual purpose behind the installation of the handrails : they would draw attention to the subject stairs, and assist a person using the stairs. He further stated that the black painted skirting board then, and now drew attention to the location of the subject stairs.




[29] Mr. Gershman confirmed that the photographs taken by Mr. Mooney recorded the carpeted subject stairs with the nosings and part of the corridors leading thereto, and therefrom at the time of the incident.




[30] Mr. Gershman confirmed that there had not been any incident relevant to the subject stairs prior to the Plaintiffâs fall. He speculated that during the period from July 2000 until 22nd January 2002, staff, students and visitors must have descended or ascended the subject stairs in aggregate 20,000 times without incident. He confirmed that he descended, and ascended the stairs seven, or eight times each working day over a 4½ year period. He had not considered them to be hazardous, or dangerous. If he had done so, he would have ensured that the requisite work would have been undertaken. He stated that he had caused the handrails to be constructed after the incident to indicate that the school took health and safety issues seriously.




Adrian Mooneyâs evidence




[31] Mr. Mooney, a civil servant with the Department of Education, and a member of the Institute of Occupational Safety and Health, confirmed that he visited the Premises on 25th January 2002 to view the subject stairs, and the incident location. He paid a further visit in June 2004, and on that occasion, using a Robin OM210 digital light meter, recorded the Lux levels on the subject stairs ranging between 212, and 280 Lux . In evidence, he stated that the lights had not been changed since the incident, and that at the time of the incident the relevant Lux level would have ranged between approximately 260 to 330 Lux . He confirmed that the Lux level for corridors âwhere the characteristic of the activity is occasional visiting but visual tasks confined to movement and casual seeing calling for limited perception of detailâ recommended by the Chartered Institute of British Service Engineers Guide is 100 Lux . Thus, he confirmed that his readings taken in June 2004 and the calculated standard illuminant in January 2002 exceeded the recommended Lux level. 




[32] Mr. Mooney stated that he had not taken any measurements of the subject stairs: it was not necessary for his purposes. He visited the Premises to ascertain whether the subject stairs were in an unsafe condition: he therefore looked for any tears in the carpet, hazards and/or obstructions, and to take the illuminant level. He considered that the subject stairs would be categorised as low risk. Mr Mooney defined âsafeâ in health and safety terms as meaning that all risks are reduced to as low as reasonably practical and fulfil any health and safety legal obligations. He considered that the subject stairs were safe, and did not present a significant risk. He categorised low risk as being that no further action was necessary at the time: the subject stairs had good carpet, were well-maintained, and appropriately lit. He stated that he would not have advised the installation of handrails .




[33] Although Mr Mooney was called as a witness of fact by the Defendant, Mr Goldie accepted that in the sphere of health and safety Mr Mooneyâs evidence could be given due weight.




Ian Copleyâs evidence




[34] Ian Copley , a chartered structural engineer and civil engineer, with in excess of twenty years experience as a construction engineer, was called by the Defendant to give expert evidence. He produced and confirmed his report of 24th November 2004. The relevant parts of such report read:-




âWe have inspected the lower ground floor corridor stairs at the IOM Business School on 27th October 2004, and can report on our findings â¦


â¦


The stair construction comprises three risers and two treads with carpet surfacing and non-slip nosings . All carpet and nosings appeared secure, with a nominal nosing overlap of about 5mm. 




A timber handrail was present to both sides of the stairs positioned approximately 800mm above the stair pitch.




Stair measurements recorded on site are noted below.




Average Stair Going 307mm (lower) 276mm (upper)


Average Stair Rise 198mm (lower) 200mm (middle) 205mm (upper)




Flight Width 1450mm average


Pitch 33° ---> 36.6°




The treads surfaces appeared to be level within + 4mm across the stair tread width and depth.




The corridor floor surface immediately adjacent the lowest riser has a slight dip of the order of 5mm.




Illumination to the corridor in the area of the staircase appeared satisfactory.




â¦




We have traversed the stair several times travelling along the corridor in the direction of [Plaintiffâs ] route, as noted in the accident report, (we had not previously walked down this corridor/stairs).




Although no warning signs were evident on travelling towards the [subject stairs] the stairs was readily apparent in our view, as the change in level of the black skirting board to the corridor highlighted the presence of the stair.




The IOM Business School was formerly a private dwelling, The Nunnery , before a change of use.




The stair construction appears to reflect the original stair construction of The Nunnery and with the exception of the handrails , does not appear to have been materially changed as part of the IOM Business School refurbishment .




There is no requirement for the existing Nunnery construction to comply with current Building Regulations, nor we understand does a change of use require upgrading of the stair to Building Regulations current at the time of change of use.




However for comparison purposes, the Building Regulations 199[3], which were current at the time of the accident, requires handrails to each side of a stair wider than 1.0m, a maximum rise of 180mm and minimum going of 280mm for institutional buildings and maximum rise of 220mm and minimum going of 220mm for private stairs.




The stair would therefore not comply with the Building Regulation handrail requirement at the time of the accident, but would meet the going, rise and pitch requirements for a private stair, but not those for an institutional stair.




Unevenness to the stair in the form of the dimensional variation in rise and goings of 7mm and 31mm respectively is also not recommended, but having traversed the stairs ourselves, the variation could not be âfeltâ .




The nosing/overlap dimension to the stairs is also less than the 16mm recommended under Building Regulations but again this was not apparent as adversely affecting the stair use.




The absence of the handrail at the time of the accident would reduce the guidance and support to users of the staircase.â




[35] In examination, Mr. Copley confirmed that the lowest rise occurred between levels 3 and 4 and that level 3 had the greatest going. He described âgoingâ as being the measurement from the going nose end of level 2 to the nose end of level 3. He stated that in his opinion the goings were not relevant to the Plaintiffâs fall.




[36] As to the actual variation in risers, Mr. Copley stated that on average the rise differential measured 7mm which was a very small differential. In his opinion a person would be unable to detect the differential in the risings when descending the subject stairs: the differential in risings was not a material aspect to the Plaintiffâs fall. The goings were less significant to the fall than the differential in risers. Mr. Copley stated that there was a variation of 31mm in the goings between level 2 and level 3. He could not detect the same and considered that such differential was not a material aspect to the causation of the Plaintiffâs fall. With reference to the width of the subject stairs, Mr. Copley stated that he did not consider such width to be a cause of the Plaintiffâs fall. As to the lack of handrail, Mr. Copley stated that if the handrail had been present, it would have assisted a user such as the Plaintiff to descend the subject stairs. However, there are only two goings to the subject stairs. In his opinion, the effect of a handrail would be less if the fall took place, which it had, from level 3 because a handrail would not extend normally beyond a lower step, in this case level 3. If a handrail had been present and the Plaintiff fell whilst descending from level 3 to level 4, Mr. Copley considered that the handrail would have had limited effect to prevent the fall. Mr. Copley stated that each step had nosings , he did not consider that the nosings had any detrimental effect, they were of the non-slip type. When requested to describe the subject stairs, he stated that he would class them as adequate for the premises as they stood.




[37] In cross-examination, Mr. Goldie referred to that part of Mr. Copleyâs report in which he opines that having traversed the subject stairs, the variation in rise and goings could not be felt. Mr. Copley agreed that such opinion was subjective based on his measurements and practical examination. Mr. Copley stated that the British Standard in tolerance under BS5606 related to new constructions and that under such standard the variation in risers was + 6mm. Relevant to the subject stairs, the variation + had a maximum of 5mm. As to the differential in going being less critical than rise, Mr. Copley stated that the differential in rise would affect a personâs descent of a staircase.




[38] Mr. Goldie referred Mr. Copley to the Building Regulations 1993, to the Building (Amendment) Regulations 2000 and in particular to âapproved document Kâ . Mr. Copley stated that there was no requirement for the Premises to comply with such Building Regulations or any subordinate legislation under such Regulations, since the Premises was an existing building even though the use of the same had changed in 2000. In response to Mr. Goldieâs reference to that part of Mr. Copleyâs report commencing âHowever for comparison purposes, the Building Regulations 199[3]â to âand minimum going of 220mm for private stairsâ , Mr. Copley responded that the Building Regulations did not contain such dimensions, they simply provided that stairs should be safe; however, he accepted that Document K contained dimensions referred to in his report; and that the Premises do not comply with the maximum rise and minimum going requirements for institutional buildings. With reference to the minimum recommended going of 280mm, Mr. Copley agreed that level 2 going was 4mm less than that recommended for a public building. He further agreed that under Document K, a handrail was recommended for each side of stairs which exceed 1m in width as in the case of the subject stairs. Mr. Copley confirmed that the Premises did and do not comply with Document K. However, he emphasised that the Building Regulations and Document K apply to new constructions. Mr. Copley confirmed that he anticipated the person who undertook refurbishment of the Premises would be aware of such Building Regulations. In carrying out such refurbishment , Mr. Copley considered that such person would have considered whether the subject stairs were safe to use. Whilst he accepted that Document K provided guidance, he stated that such guidance is for new constructions. There is no obligation to adopt such guidance for existing constructions.




[39] In re-examination, Mr. Copley confirmed that he did not consider the subject stairs to be unsafe. He did not consider that the risers and goings, being at variance with those prescribed under the Building Regulations, caused the Plaintiffâs fall. Whilst accepting that stairs are more hazardous than corridors, Mr. Copley considered that the subject stairs presented no real risk.






Mr. Goldieâs arguments and submissions




[40] Mr. Goldie submitted that bearing in mind the nature of the Defendantâs business and the use of the Premises by students and the public, it was foreseeable that someone would fall down the subject stairs as the Plaintiff had done. The absence of a prior incident was not itself determinative: the fact that there had been no prior incident did not mean that a hazard did not exist. The Plaintiffâs case is that the Premises contained a hazard in the form of uneven steps.




[41] Mr. Goldie submitted that the Defendant took no action to rectify the hazard, or to warn of its existence, or to provide a means by which the risk the subject stairs created could be minimised. He referred to the lack of handrails , the placing of which would have been proportionate, cheap and would have reduced the risk of injury to users of the subject stairs. As to the uneven steps, he maintained that the Defendant ought to have ensured that the same were not too steep or uneven. Whilst accepting that to achieve the latter would have required the Defendant to expend more monies and undertake more work than the installation of handrails , Mr. Goldie submitted that the Defendant had undertook refurbishment of the Premises to accommodate future use of the same by students and lawful visitors: such work and such expenditure would not have been unreasonable.




[42] Even in the absence of unevenness, the failure to provide a handrail meant that any person falling down the steps, which Mr. Goldie maintained was a foreseeable risk, had no means to save or right themselves and accordingly the potential injury sustained was much greater than would otherwise have been the case.




[43] Whilst Mr. Goldie recognised that the Defendant was not under a specific statutory duty to correct the uneven nature of the steps, nor to provide a handrail, he referred to the Building Regulations 1993, and in particular, those parts which read:-




âLimitation on requirements


8. Parts A to K and N of Schedule 1 to these regulations shall not require anything to be done except for the purpose of securing reasonable standards of health and safety for persons in or about buildings (and any others who may be affected by buildings, or matters connected with buildings).




â¦


PART K STAIRS, RAMPS AND GUARDS


Stairs and ramps


K1. Stairs, ladders and ramps shall offer safety to users moving between levels of the building.â 




[44] Mr. Goldie also referred to approved Document K and in particular those parts which read:-




âThe Approved Documents are intended to provide guidance for some of the more common building situations. However, there may well be alternative ways of achieving compliance with the requirements. Thus there is no obligation to adopt any particular solution contained in an Approved Document if you prefer to meet the relevant requirement in some other way.




Other requirements


The guidance contained in an Approved Document relates only to the particular requirements of the Regulations which that document addresses. 


â¦




LIMITATION ON REQUIREMENTS


In accordance with regulation 8, the requirements in Parts A to K and N of Schedule 1 to the Building Regulations do not require anything to be done except for the purpose of securing reasonable standards of health and safety for persons in or about the building.


â¦




Technical specifications


Building regulations are made for specific purposes; health and safety, energy conservation and the welfare and convenience of disabled people.


â¦




Mixed use development


In mixed use developments part of a building may be used as a dwelling while another part has a non-domestic use. In such cases, if the requirements of this Part of the Regulations for dwellings and non-domestic use differ, the requirements for non-domestic use should apply in any shared parts of the building.


â¦




Requirement


Stairs, ladders and ramps


K1. Stairs, ladders and ramps shall be so designed, constructed and installed as to be safe for people moving between different levels in or about the building.â




[45] Whilst Mr. Goldie accepted that the Building Regulations did not impose an obligation upon the Defendant relevant to the subject stairs, he submitted that the obligation imposed by such Regulations was equivalent to the common law duty of care because compliance with the Regulations secured reasonable health and safety of users of the Premises moving between levels. Document K provided practical guidance to meet the regulatory requirements. If the Court accepted that the guidance provided by Document K ensures safety for users, then if the subject stairs did not meet such guidelines they were not safe and the Defendant had breached its duty of care. Mr. Goldie submitted that the risers of the subject stairs exceeded the guidelines; the going of level 2 was below the minimum recommended in Document K. The latter document recommended that all risers should be of even height, steps of even goings, and stairs in excess of 1m width should be fitted with a handrail on each side. Mr. Goldie referred to Mr. Copleyâs confirmation that the subject stairs did not comply with Document K. Mr. Goldie argued that when the Defendant undertook refurbishment of the Premises in contemplation of change of use and future public admittance thereto, it was not unreasonable to expect the Defendant to take guidance from Document K to make the subject stairs safe rather than merely rely upon the lack of obligation to comply with the Building Regulations. Mr. Goldie submitted that the absence of the specific statutory duty does not mean that there is no duty of care to those using the Premises. He submitted that the Defendant owed the Plaintiff a duty of care, was in breach of such duty by failing to take sufficient measures to prevent or reduce the risk of injury to the Plaintiff by either ensuring that the steps were even or by warning of the uneven nature of the steps or by providing a handrail. In the absence of such measures, the Defendant is liable for the injuries sustained and the losses suffered by the Plaintiff.




[46] Mr. Goldie submitted that the Plaintiff had fallen whilst descending from level 3 to level 4 of the subject stairs: she perceived that the rise was steeper than the last rise. The unevenness of the steps had affected the Plaintiffâs natural gait and could have led to such perception. Whilst Mr. Goldie accepted that Mr. Copley gave expert evidence, he submitted that Mr. Copleyâs perception as to whether the steps felt uneven was subjective and therefore that part of his evidence ought to be treated accordingly.




Conclusions as to whether Defendant, its servants or agents were negligent




[47] The Plaintiff claims that the injury, which she sustained, occurred during her attendance as a lawful visitor at the Premises, and that the same was caused by the negligence and/or breach of statutory duty of the Defendant, its servants, and/or agents.




[48] The Defendant does not dispute that the Plaintiff was a lawful visitor to the Premises on 22nd January 2002, and that whilst there she fell, and sustained injury. However, the Defendant denies liability, and, in the alternative, if primary liability is found, pleads that the Plaintiff was contributorily negligent. 






[49] Although the Plaintiff pleads breach of statutory duty in her Statement of Claim, she does not so plead in her Statement of Case but pleads that the accident occurred as a result of the negligence of the Defendant, and/or its servants or agents. Mrs. Weatherill submitted that the Defendant is thus only required to respond to the pleaded case, viz. common law negligence. I agree, and in support cite Lewis v Catchpole 1978-80 MLR 419 (SGD ) and that part of the judgment of the court at 422 L37-423 L10 which reads:-




âThe purpose of pleadings is not purely a technical one. The purpose of pleadings is to ensure a fair trial of the issues between the parties and, of course, to save the litigant expense. It has a solid advantage in practical terms for litigants. If there were no pleadings at all litigants, ordinary citizens, would be brought before the court not knowing what was being alleged against them, not knowing what evidence to bring, and not knowing what documents to produce. The purpose of legal pleadings is not a technical matter to satisfy the lawyers or the judges, it is something which is of great practical advantage to the people who actually pay to have their cases fought, and therefore they are important documents; if they do not express clearly the issues between the parties, it is the parties in the end who are going to lose out in terms of either losing money because further expense will be incurred, or in not having their case properly fought.â




[50] I proceed to determine the case as pleaded: common law negligence. 




[51] I refer to Halsburyâs Laws of England Volume 33 paragraphs 601-605, and 621-622. Negligence is a specific tort, and in any given circumstance is the failure to exercise that care, which the circumstances demand. What amounts to negligence depends on the facts of the particular case: it may be the omission of something which ought to have been done, or the doing of something which ought to have been done in a different manner, or not at all. However, unless a defendant owes a duty of care to the particular plaintiff, no matter how strong the facts of such plaintiffâs particular claim, it will fail. Once a notional duty has been accepted, or found the question is then whether, on the facts of the case, the plaintiff comes within the scope of that duty so as to render the damage actionable at his suit: the question is one of factual duty. A factual duty is owed only to those persons, who are in the area of objectively foreseeable danger.




[52] The Defendant admitted that it owed the Plaintiff a duty of care to take reasonable care for her safety, she being a lawful visitor. Thus the issue presented is whether the Defendant breached such duty. 




[53] It is a question of fact whether a defendant has failed to show reasonable care in the particular circumstance. The court will apply the legal standard of care to its findings of fact so as to decide whether a defendant has attained that standard. The legal standard is objective; it is not that of the defendant himself, but that which might be expected from a person of ordinary prudence, or a person of ordinary skill, and care engaged in the type of activity in which the defendant was engaged. A person must have regard both to the probability of harm resulting from his acts, or omissions, and the probable seriousness of the harm. His knowledge of the probabilities must be judged in the light of the evidence available at the time and not with benefit of hindsight. He may weigh the cost and difficulty of the precautions necessary to prevent the harm resulting: the actual resources available to a defendant are not generally relevant. The courts may take into account the importance of the activity in which a defendant is engaged. A person is not entitled to assume that others will take reasonable care to look out for themselves, or will take reasonable steps to avoid common risks when experience shows that negligence is common. What is habitually done in the same circumstances usually furnishes a test of reasonable care, but a person cannot excuse an obvious failure to make some inquiry or take some precaution merely by showing that his failure to do so is in accordance with the established practice in a particular business. 




[54] It was common ground, supported by Mr. Copleyâs evidence, that there was no requirement for the Premises to comply with the Building Regulations 1993 or any subordinate legislation thereunder. I reject Mr. Goldieâs submissions that non-compliance with the Regulations, or the guidance provided by Document K equates to a breach of duty of care. The common thread of such Regulations and Document K is to ensure that, inter alia, stairs offer safety to users moving between levels of a building, to which such legislation applies, and to secure reasonable standards of health and safety for persons in or about such building. Whilst an existing building, which is not governed by such Regulations or subordinate legislation, might not precisely accord with the same or the guidance provided thereby, it does not necessarily follow that such building does not offer the required standards of health and safety, or fails to offer safety to stair users. As to the Premises, Mr. Copley did not consider the subject stairs to be unsafe, and Mr. Mooney considered the same to be safe.




[55] As to the submissions that the Premises contained a hazard in the form of uneven steps and that the Defendant ought to have ensured that the same were not too steep or uneven, I reject the same. In support of such rejections, I refer to Mr. Copleyâs evidence that the stepsâ goings were not relevant to the Plaintiffâs fall; that the differential in the risings was not a material aspect to the Plaintiffâs fall; and that he did not consider that the risers, or goings, although at variance with those prescribed under the subordinate legislation, caused the Plaintiffâs fall. Further, Mr. Copley referred to the British Standard in tolerance under BS5606 applicable to new constructions, which standard permitted a variation in risers of+ 6mm, and to the fact that the variations in the risers of the subject stairs were within such tolerance being + 5mm. 




[56] As to the width of the subject stairs, I accept Mr. Copleyâs evidence that he did not consider the stair width to be a cause of the Plaintiffâs fall. 




[57] The Plaintiff pleads that the Defendant was negligent in that, inter alia, it had failed to provide a banister or handrails . I have found that as the Plaintiff approached the subject stairs, Mrs. Stephens was walking to the left of the corridor, slightly behind the latter and to the right hand side of the corridor was Lorraine Quayle with the Plaintiff towards the middle and slightly behind Mrs. Stephens. I have further found that the Plaintiff was stepping from level 3 to level 4 immediately before she fell and that as she fell she tried to grab Mrs. Stephens who received a push to the rear of her right shoulder but was able to steady herself on the left hand side wall at level 4. 




[58] Mr Copley expressed the view that, in this case, the effect of a handrail would be less, and considered that if a handrail had been present that the handrail would have had limited effect to prevent the fall. I accept Mr. Copleyâs evidence that a handrail would not normally extend beyond a lower step, in this case level 3. I accept that the absence of a handrail would reduce the guidance and support to a user of the subject stairs. However, in this case, one must not overlook the positions of the Plaintiff, Mrs Stephens, and Lorraine Quayle immediately before the fall; that there are only two goings to the subject stairs; that the Plaintiff was stepping from level 3 to level 4 immediately before she fell; and that as the Plaintiff fell she tried to grab Mrs. Stephens, who received a push to the rear of her right shoulder. The latter two aspects indicate forward movement of the Plaintiff as she was falling. 




[59] Also, one should not overlook that the Plaintiff was aware of the subject stairs, see for example the entry at 10.45 a.m. on 22nd January 2002 in the Health and Safety at Work Inspectorate Report. Further, I accept Mr. Mooneyâs evidence that he would not have advised the installation of handrails , and that he considered the subject stairs to be low risk, and safe. Mr. Copley did not consider the stairs to be unsafe.




[60] In the circumstance of this case, I am not satisfied that the lack of a handrail was a cause, not necessarily the sole, or dominant cause, of the Plaintiffâs fall, or that the injuries sustained by the Plaintiff were greater than would have otherwise been sustained if handrails had been provided. I do not consider that the lack of handrails caused, or contributed to the Plaintiffâs fall, or the injures she sustained. 




[61] In further support but ancillary, I refer to Mr. Gershmanâs evidence that there had not been any incident relevant to the subject stairs prior to the Plaintiffâs fall and, albeit speculative, but bearing in mind Mr. Gershmanâs responsibilities at that time, the volume of traffic which he speculated had used the subject stairs from July 2000 until 22nd January 2002 without incident. What is habitually done in the same circumstances usually furnishes a test of reasonable care




[62] I do not consider, on the evidence adduced, and the facts of this case, that the Defendant, its servants, and/or agents can be held to have been negligent, or to have failed to exercise that care which the circumstances demanded on the basis that there were no handrails , or that the lack of handrails caused the Plaintiff to sustain injuries greater than would have otherwise been sustained. Further, the fact that the Defendant installed handrails after the incident is not of itself evidence of negligence â for support see Coates âv - Jaguar Cars Limited (2004) All ER (D) 87 at paragraph 11.




[63] In her evidence the Plaintiff accepted that she was aware of the subject stairs before she reached the same: I agree with Mrs. Weatherill that in such circumstance, the allegation that the Defendant was negligent in failing to signpost the subject stairs or highlight or bring the same to the Plaintiffâs attention, cannot be supported. The Plaintiff was aware of the subject stairs. Further, I accept Mr. Copleyâs view that the subject stairs were readily apparent due to the change in level of the black skirting board to the corridor highlighting the presence of the same. Also, the subject stairs were well-maintained, appropriately lit, and the step ends clearly defined with non-slip nosings .




[64] I reject the Plaintiffâs assertion that the Defendant, its servants, and/or agents failed to take due and reasonable care to ensure that the subject stairs were fit and safe for use. I refer to Mr Gershmanâs inspection of the subject stairs immediately following the incident, and to Mr. Mooneyâs inspection, some three days after the incident, which led him to conclude that the subject stairs had good carpet, were well-maintained and appropriately lit. Mr Mooney considered that the subject stairs were safe, and categorised the same as low risk, that is that no further action was necessary. He stated that he would not have advised the installation of handrails . Mr. Copley did not consider the subject stairs to be unsafe, and whilst accepting that stairs are more hazardous than corridors, he considered that the subject stairs presented no real risk. 




[65] Having considered the evidence, particularly that of Mr. Mooney and Mr. Copley , I conclude that the subject stairs were safe; and that they posed no real or foreseeable risk. 




[66] This Court is not required to ascertain the cause of the accident, or the injuries sustained by the Plaintiff. In addressing the Primary Issue, it is merely required to determine whether the Defendant, its servants, or agents were negligent. I am satisfied that the Plaintiff adduced no evidence, which would support a finding that the Defendant itself, or by its servants and/or agents failed to exercise that care, which the circumstances demanded, or breached its duty of care to the Plaintiff. Whilst this Court has sympathy for the Plaintiff, her fall, and the injuries she sustained were not due to any fault, or omission on the part of the Defendant, its servants, and/or agents.




[67] Having found that the Defendant is not liable in negligence, it follows that the Plaintiffâs action must be dismissed. Consequently, I need not and ought not to address the Plaintiffâs claims for general and special damages, or the Defendantâs assertion that if it was found to be primarily liable in negligence, the Plaintiff ought to be found contributorily negligent.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
party_two: ISLE OF MAN INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS SCHOOL

title: BANK OF IRELAND HOLDINGS (IOM) LIMITED
text:
Definitions




[1] In this judgment, the following words, and phrases shall have the following meanings:




Bank Bank of Ireland (Isle of Man) Limited, a company incorporated in the Isle of Man, and wholly owned subsidiary of Holdings




Bank of Ireland the Governor and Company of the Bank of Ireland Plc of Baggot Street, Dublin, Republic of Ireland




Directors the Directors of the Bank






Disclosure Resolution Special Resolution passed on15th February 2006 by the members of the Bank, the full text of which is recited at paragraph [9]




English 1970 Act Taxes Management Act 1970 (of the Westminster Parliament)




FSC Isle of Man Financial Supervision Commission




Holdings Bank of Ireland Holdings (I.O.M.) Limited, a company incorporated in the Isle of Man, and wholly owned subsidiary of Bank of Ireland




Information the information sought by the Irish Revenue at paragraphs numbered 1 to 4 of Mr. Walshâs letter dated 26th January 2006 to the Taxation Department of the Bank of Ireland, parts of which letter, including such numbered paragraphs, are recited at paragraph [7]




Irish 1997 Act Irish Taxes Consolidations Act 1997 (as amended) (of the Republic of Ireland) 




Preliminary Issues The issues summarised at paragraph [12]






Mr. Walsh Paul Walsh, a Principal Officer with the Investigations, and Prosecution Division of the Irish Revenue




Representations




[2] At the hearings of the Preliminary Issues, Mr. S.F. Caine appeared for the Petitioner, Mr. J.J. Callin for the Bank, and the Directors, and the Attorney General assisted by Miss M.A. Norman for himself, and the FSC.




Background




[3] The Bank is licensed to carry on banking business by the FSC. By a written Special Resolution passed on 20th December 2005 the Bankâs Articles of Association were amended by the introduction of a new Article 85, which reads:




âSubject to the provisions of the (Companies Acts), the Memorandum and Articles, and to any directions given by special resolution the business of the company shall be managed by the Directors who may exercise all the powers of the company. No alteration of the Memorandum or Articles and no such direction shall invalidate any prior act of the Directors which would have been valid if that alteration had not been made or that direction had not been given. The powers given by this regulation shall not be limited by any special power given to the Directors by these Articles.â




[4] A materially similar Article is contained in the Articles of Association of Holdings by virtue of its incorporation, inter alia, of Article 70 of Table A of the Companies (Memorandum and Articles of Association) Regulations 1988. Thus, pursuant to such Articles, Holdings, and the Bankâs respective shareholders may give directions to their directors requiring them to take certain action provided that such action is not illegal, ultra vires or improper.




[5] On 20th December 2005, the Bank of Ireland, as sole shareholder of Holdings, passed the following resolution as a Special Resolution:




âPursuant to Article 70 of Table A of the Companies (Memorandum and Articles of Association) Regulations 1988, as incorporated into the companyâs Articles of Association by Article 1 thereof, the current and future Directors of the Company are directed immediately to procure that its subsidiary, [the Bank], fully complies within fourteen days of the date hereof with the provisions of a draft Order of the Irish Court to be served on Bank of Ireland Plc (sic) pursuant to section 908 of the Irish Taxes Consolidations Act 1997, as substituted by section 207(i) of the Irish Finance Act 1999 (the âOrderâ), a copy of which is attached hereto and which forms part of this Special Resolution. In particular, but without limitation, the current and future Directors of the Company are hereby directed immediately to procure that [the Bank] will make available for inspection by the Irish Revenue, within fourteen days of the date hereof, the books, records, documents and information requested in the Schedule to the Order.â




[6] Holdings, by its Directors, desired to comply with such Special Resolution and together with the Bankâs other member passed on 20th December 2005 the following membersâ written Special Resolution pursuant to Article 85 of the Bankâs Articles of Association:




âPursuant to Article 85 of the Companyâs Articles of Association, the current and future Directors of the Company are directed to fully comply within fourteen days of the date hereof with the provisions of a draft order of the Irish High Court to be served on Bank of Ireland Plc (sic) pursuant to section 908 of the Irish Taxes Consolidations Act 1997, as substituted by section 207(i) of the Irish Finance Act 1999 (the âOrderâ), a copy of which is attached hereto and which forms part of this Special Resolution. In particular, but without limitation, the current and future Directors of the Company are hereby directed to make available for inspection by the Irish Revenue, within fourteen days of the date hereof, the books, records, documents and information requested in the Schedule to the Order.â




[7] Subsequently, but before any action consequent upon the latter Special Resolution had been taken, by letter dated 26th January 2006 from Mr. Walsh to the Taxation Department of the Bank of Ireland, the Irish Revenue reduced the amount of information which it required the Bank of Ireland to provide about Irish residents, who held accounts with the Bank. The relevant parts of such letter read:




ââ¦ The primary objective of the Revenue application to the High Court is to seek to ascertain the identity of those customers of the Bank of Ireland Group who failed to avail of the voluntary disclosure programme and who hold or have held a bank account outside the State that gives rise to a taxation liability in this State.




I understand that the scope and extent of the Order sought will require a considerable deployment of manpower in servicing the Order. However, I would propose that the terms of the Order sought could be amended so that compliance with the Order should be ameliorated. This proposal is made on the basis that the following information in relation to Isle of Man accounts of customers with an address in the State can be made available to Revenue:




1. The name and address of the owner(s) of the account;


2. The date the account was opened and the opening balance;


3. The maximum amount in the account over the life of the account and 


4. The date of closure of the account if applicable.â




[8] On 3rd February 2006, the Bank of Ireland, as sole shareholder of Holdings, passed a Special Resolution, the relevant part of which reads:




âPursuant to Article 70 of Table A of the Companies (Memorandum and Articles of Association) Regulations 1988, as incorporated into the Companyâs Articles of Association by Article 1 thereof, the current and future Directors of the Company are directed immediately to procure that its subsidiary, [the Bank] â¦ provides to the Revenue Commissioners of Ireland the information required at paragraphs 1 to 4 of the letter dated 26 January 2006 from Paul Walsh, Principal Officer with the Investigations and Prosecutions Division of the Irish Revenue, to the Taxation Department of the Governor and Company Bank of Ireland, a copy of which is attached hereto and which forms part of this Special Resolution.â




[9] Holdings, by its directors, two of whom are also directors of the Bank, determined to comply with such Special Resolution. Consequently, on 15th February 2006, the Disclosure Resolution was passed, such resolution reads:




âPursuant to Article 85 of the Companyâs Articles of Association, the current and future Directors of the Company are directed to disregard the written special resolution of the Company dated 20 December 2005 and instead to provide to the Revenue Commissioners of Ireland the information required at paragraphs 1 to 4 of the letter dated 26th January 2006 from Paul Walsh, principal officer with the Investigations and Prosecutions Division of the Irish Bank to the Taxation Department of the Governor and Bank of Ireland, a copy of which is attached hereto and which forms part of this Special Resolution.â




[10] The Directors have declined to comply with the Disclosure Resolution. By Petition dated 16th February 2006 brought under the provisions of section 42 High Court Act 1991, and with notice to the Bank, Holdings seeks a mandatory injunction requiring the Directors to comply.




[11] By orders made on 27th February 2006, at their request, the Directors were joined as parties to the Petition with notice also being given to the Attorney General and to the FSC. On 5th May 2006, it was ordered that certain issues be dealt with by way of preliminary issues with the remainder of the Petition being stayed pending determination of the same.




Preliminary Issues




[12] The Preliminary Issues may be summarised thus. Is it against public policy for the Directors to be compelled to disclose the Information to the Irish Revenue, (1) if all of the Information is held by the Bank of Ireland in Dublin in any event but in unwieldy form; (2) if some of the Information is held by Bank of Ireland in Dublin in any event, but in unwieldy form; and (3) if none of the Information is held by Bank of Ireland in Dublin?




Hearings




[13] The hearing of the Preliminary Issues commenced on 3rd August 2006, but was adjourned part heard to 4th October 2006 at Mr. Caineâs request to enable further legal arguments, and submissions to be raised, and made.






Arguments, and Submissions




[14] Mr. Caine indicated that he would argue the Preliminary Issues on the basis that none of the Information is held by Bank of Ireland in Dublin.




[15] He argued that it would not be against public policy for the Directors to be compelled to disclose the Information to the Irish Revenue, because there are no statutory secrecy laws in the Isle of Man. The only duties of confidentiality imposed upon banks in the Isle of Man are contained in the common law, as set out in Tournier v National Provincial and Union Bank of England [1923] All ER Rep 550, and those duties are not absolute. The Isle of Man as a finance and banking centre has become ever more transparent in relation to the provision of bank account details to foreign jurisdictions, for example, disclosure, including disclosure to foreign revenues can be made under certain statutory provisions. Indeed, Mr. Caine argued that it is positively in the public interest for such disclosures to be made, inter alia, to foreign jurisdictions, including foreign revenues, in order to protect the Islandâs reputation as a responsible banking and finance sector. Further, the disclosure of details of bank accounts to foreign revenue does not amount to enforcement of foreign tax debts. Thus, Mr. Caine submitted it cannot be said that it would be against public policy per se for the Information to be provided to the Irish Revenue.




[16] Mr. Caine further submitted that if it cannot be said that it would be against public policy per se for details of bank accounts to be provided to foreign revenues, then the question of whether the provision of such information to a foreign revenue is against public policy must be considered on a case-by-case basis, and that the only real test to be applied is one of proportionality.




[17] Mr. Caine referred to paragraphs 4, and 5 of Mr. Walshâs affidavit sworn on 1st February 2006, which paragraphs read:




â4. Irish Revenue has good grounds to believe that there has been significant and systematic use of accounts held with [the Bank] by Irish residents for evasion of tax due to the Irish Revenue. In 2004, Irish Revenue launched a scheme which invited Irish residents holding funds outside the State to make a voluntary disclosure of all taxes, interest and penalties owing. The benefits of the scheme included reduced penalties, non-publication of the defaulterâs name in Iris Oifigiuil, the official Irish State gazette, and non-prosecution.




5. Over 15,000 taxpayers made voluntary disclosures under this scheme. Among their number were over 1,700 customers of [the Bank], who paid Irish Revenue in excess of â¬237m to cover tax, interest and penalties due. I am satisfied both from the scale of the payments made by [Bank] customers and the number of customers who came forward that there was a significant and systematic use of accounts held with [the Bank] by Irish residents for the purposes of tax evasion.â




[18] Mr. Caine argued that over 1,700 account holders with the Bank had disclosed details of their accounts to the Irish Revenue pursuant to the scheme. Such account holders represented a very large proportion of the account holders with the Bank. In the circumstances, he submitted that it would be entirely proportionate for this Court to order the Directors to provide the Information to the Irish Revenue, and that such an order would not be against public policy. In support Mr. Caine referred to the Decision of Special Commissioner Dr John F Avery-Jones CBE dated 28th November 2005.




[19] Mr. Caine submitted that it would be wholly incongruous for those account holders, who have not made voluntary disclosures to the Irish Revenue and who may, on the basis of the âcredible evidenceâ from the Irish Revenue, as set out in Mr. Walshâs affidavit, be using accounts with the Bank for purposes of tax evasion, to be able to avoid their obligations to pay tax by hiding behind the Bankâs duty of confidentiality, thereby damaging the Islandâs reputation as a responsible banking centre. He argued that cases such as Re X AG and others v A Bank [1983] 2 All ER at 464 have been overtaken by greater transparency, and mutual assistance relative to finance centres, and that more recent case law has emphasised public interest in ensuring that disclosure of confidential documents relating to alleged fraud outweighed the public interest in preserving the confidentiality of those documents. In support, Mr. Caine cited Pharaon âv- BCCI [1998] 4 All ER 455, Pricewaterhouse


-v- BCCI (Luxembourg) SA [1992] BCLC 583,and Acturus Properties Limited âv- Attorney General 3 ITELR 360 (Royal Court (Samedi Division) Jersey).




[20] Mr. Caine submitted also that, applying the test of proportionality, the Irish Revenue is not engaged in a fishing expedition, the provision of the Information would not be against public policy, no prejudice would be caused to the account holders if the Information is provided because (i) the Irish Revenue has confirmed that the Information will not be shared with any other jurisdiction; (ii) monies in the accounts of the account holders should have been disclosed to the Irish Revenue, if it has been no prejudice is suffered, if it has not then an account holder has used his account to evade tax: any prejudice suffered would result from that evasion, and not from disclosure of the Information by the Bank.




[21] Mr. Caine argued that, if disclosure is made, and an account holder suffers prejudice thereby, he could sue the Bank for breach of its duty of confidentiality, and in defence the Bank would have to rely upon one, or more of the exceptions to the rule in Tournier. In this case, he submitted that the Bank could rely upon two of the exceptions, namely the interests of the Bank require disclosure, and there is a duty to the public, both in the Isle of Man and elsewhere.




[22] Further, Mr. Caine argued that, if this Court grants a mandatory injunction, and a disgruntled account holder sues the Bank, it could not rely on the exception of compulsion by law. Mr. Caine submitted that if the Directors had complied with the Disclosure Resolution then there would have been no need for the Petition, and no question of the Bank providing the Information under compulsion. He argued that there is a clear distinction between the Directors being compelled by an order of mandamus to comply with a special resolution, and the Bank disclosing the Information to the Irish Revenue under compulsion of an order of Court. He submitted that the crucial point is that the Bank, by its shareholders, has already decided to voluntarily disclose the Information to the Irish Revenue, and is now seeking to compel the Directors to action that decision. In such circumstances, if an aggrieved account holder sues the Bank, the latter cannot seek to argue that the Information was provided under compulsion of an order of Court, since the order of Court will simply have compelled the Directors to comply with the Disclosure Resolution, which was voluntarily passed by the Bankâs shareholders.




Conclusions




[23] In the absence of statutory secrecy laws, it cannot be against public policy per se for a bank to make disclosures relevant to customersâ accounts. However, I agree with the Attorney General, and Mr. Callin that there is an expectation of confidentiality on the part of banksâ customers in this jurisdiction in the same way as would be expected in other financial centres, including the United Kingdom and the Republic of Ireland. Such expectation is supported by a bankâs contractual duty of confidentiality, or secrecy as set out in Tournier, which confidentiality obligation is reflected in the banking licences issued by the FSC. Whilst such duty should be regarded as qualified, not absolute, it can be taken as being overridden only in cases within one of the four classifications, or exceptions identified in Tournier by Bankes LJ., namely: (a) where disclosure is under compulsion by law; (b) where there is a duty to the public to disclose; (c) where the interests of the bank require disclosure; or (d) where the disclosure is made by the express or implied consent of the customer. The principles set down in Tournier continue to be approved, and applied by the courts: they are considered not to be open to dispute - see Christofi âv- Barclays Bank PLC [2000] 1 WLR 937 at 946 F.




[24] Whilst I cannot take issue with Mr. Caine that provision of information to a foreign revenue authority relevant to bank accounts does not necessarily amount to enforcement of foreign tax debts, whether any such disclosure is appropriate must depend on the particular circumstances of the case, and the application of relevant principles. Although in support of this argument, Mr. Caine cited Re McCoy[1996-98] MLR 327, and Re Frederiiksen[1996-98] MLR 26, I do not consider that either such case assists Mr. Caine: each such case can be clearly distinguished on its facts.




[25] In this case, it is noted that the request for the Information made to the Bank of Ireland is based on a draft court order: compliance with such draft order can be regarded as purely voluntary. Although an authorised officer of the Irish Revenue may apply under section 908 of the Irish 1997 Act to the Irish High Court for an order requiring the disclosure of records relating to a taxpayer, no application for such an order has been made. In any event, as maintained by the Attorney General, there is no reference in section 908 to an order under that section having any extra-territorial effect. Indeed, in paragraph 8 of his affidavit, Mr. Walsh confirms that, as an authorised officer, he may make an application to the Irish High Court seeking access to accounts, and that âIn the event of such application being made, and should the Irish High Court see fit to make such an Order compelling Bank of Ireland to provide such information as is available in Ireland, (my emphasis) it is my understanding that the Bank of Ireland could furnish this informationâ.




[26] In considering whether the disclosure request amounts to a fishing expedition, I cannot accept that the test to be applied in this case is one of proportionality. The case of the Decision of Special Commissioner Dr John F Avery-Jones CBE concerned the interpretation of section 20(8A) of the English 1970 Act, which contains similar provisions to section 908 of the Irish 1997 Act and which enables a Special Commissioner to consent to a notice, in which the taxpayer(s) is not specifically identified, requiring the disclosure of information regarding such target taxpayer(s) being served by an inspector on a person, including a financial institution. I agree with the Attorney General that the scope of section 20 and section 908 permits disclosure of information held within the domestic jurisdiction of the United Kingdom, or the Republic of Ireland in circumstances which go far beyond those provided for in the mechanisms, which govern the exchange of confidential information between jurisdictions pursuant to specific statutory provisions, or in accordance with the OECD principles. Although the proportionality test may be relevant when considering whether an application made under the English 1970 Act, or the Irish 1997 Act amounts to a fishing expedition, it is not relevant in determining whether the request for disclosure of the Information would be considered to be a fishing expedition, if such request is made to the appropriate authorities in the Isle of Man by the Irish Revenue under statute or the OECD principles. 




[27] In relation to the Information, disclosure is required of details of all accounts held with the Bank by persons with Irish addresses. No further identification information has been provided. I accept the Attorney Generalâs submissions that, if such a request is made to the Isle of Man under an Information Exchange Agreement based on the OECD model agreement, it would be considered to be a fishing expedition as information sufficient to identify a particular individual would not have been provided. Further, should such a request be made under the legislative provisions governing intra-jurisdictional mutual assistance, it would also be considered as a fishing expedition since any such request is required to be precise and focused in relation to named individuals who are already the subject of investigation in the requesting jurisdiction.




[28] Re X AG and others v A Bank underlines the importance, which the courts have placed, upon upholding the duty of confidentiality owed by banks to their customers in determining the balance of convenience. The fact that statutory mechanisms now exist, which were not available when such case was decided cannot detract from the importance of upholding that duty. Further support as to the importance of the duty of confidentiality may be found in the judgment of Lord Denning MR in R v Grossman [1981-83] MLR 20. The cases of Pharon v BCCI and Pricewaterhouse v BCCI (Luxembourg) SA can be distinguished on their own facts from this case. As to the case of Acturus Properties Limited v Attorney General, such case concerned the Attorney General of Jerseyâs exercise of powers equivalent to those in section 24 Criminal Justice Act 1990, and provides no support to an argument for the court to permit disclosure in circumstances where it would not be permissible under section 24 Criminal Justice Act 1990, or any of the other statutory mechanisms, or under any Agreement for the Exchange of Information.




[29] I accept that a bank can voluntarily determine to make disclosures, which may lead to challenge or other proceedings by customers, which require the bank to seek to rely upon one of the exceptions in Tournier. In the event that the Bank determines voluntarily to provide all, or any of the Information, I would not wish to prejudice the Bank, or any customer. As to Tournier, I shall therefore restrict myself to addressing Mr. Caineâs arguments that, if this Court grants a mandatory injunction, the Bank could not rely upon the exception of compulsion by law. 




[30] Whilst it was common ground that at law a company is a different person from the subscribers to the Memorandum â see Salomon âv- Salomon & Co. (1895-99) All ER Rep. 33 per Lord MacNaughton at 48, Mr. Caine submitted that so long as the company is solvent, the shareholders are in substance the company, and a decision, which is binding upon the company, can be made by its members. In support, Mr. Caine referred to Multi National Gas -v- Multi National Services (1983) BCLC 461.




[31] Mr. Caine accepted that directors do not have to comply with a membersâ resolution, if the same is improper, illegal or ultra vires. However, subject thereto, he reiterated that the relationship between a company and its shareholders is such that the shareholders are in substance the company, and can bind the company to a particular course of action.




[32] I accept that, in certain circumstances, a decision of the members of a company can bind the company, see for example, Multi National Gas. However, I agree with Mr. Callin that to accept that the shareholders are in substance the company does not reflect the true status of the company as a distinct and separate entity.




[33] Mr. Caine argued that as a matter of general law, and except in special circumstances, a director owes his fiduciary duties to the company only, not to any shareholder, and as in this case there are no concerns as to insolvency, the Directors owe no duty to present, or future creditors, see Colin Gwyer & Associates Limited & Another âv- London Wharf (Limehouse) Limited and Others, Eaton Bray Limited and Another âv- Palmer and Others (2002) EWHC 2748, at paragraphs 72 and 74 . I accept such arguments by way of general statements. 




[34] Mr. Caine submitted that the Disclosure Resolution, which was voluntarily passed unanimously by the Bankâs members, binds the Bank to a particular course of action that is disclosure of the Information to the Irish Revenue unless the Court in the face of non-compliance by the Directors determines that such resolution is illegal, ultra vires, or improper. He maintained that, absent any such ground, there are no public policy issues. Further, if the Court grants the requested injunction, and the Information is disclosed, the Bank will be unable to argue that such disclosure was made under compulsion by law, because the Disclosure Resolution was a voluntary decision made by its members, which decision binds the Bank, and which decision was made prior to the filing of the Petition.




[35] The Directors have determined not to comply with the Disclosure Resolution for reasons indicated in the affidavit sworn on 19th April 2006 of Michael McKay, one of the Directors. If the Directors decide to comply then there will be no need for these proceedings. Absent such voluntary change of mind, the granting of a mandatory injunction requiring the Directors to comply with the Disclosure Resolution must be considered as compulsion by law, and thus there would be no distinction to be drawn between disclosure, which is a direct, and inescapable consequence of such an injunction, and disclosure, which is directly ordered by a court.




[36] Bearing in mind the exceptions to the rule in Tournier, it cannot be said to be against public policy per se for a bank to make voluntary disclosure in apparent breach of its duty of confidentiality. However, in determining to make such voluntary disclosure, a director of a bank licensed in this jurisdiction must not permit his duties to the bank to be unduly influenced, or his powers to be unduly curtailed by shareholders.




[37] Whilst the Isle of Man is willing to play its part in disclosure of information, this jurisdiction supports the fundamental principle of bank confidentiality. The banking industry is important to the Island, and its economic well-being: any application for court orders to set aside the duty of confidentiality must be considered important, and of interest to the Islandâs public. 




[38] By filing its Petition, Holdings seeks that this Court grants a mandatory injunction requiring the Directors to comply with the Disclosure Resolution. I agree with the Attorney General that the public policy issue is whether it is appropriate for this Court in the circumstances of this case to exercise its discretionary jurisdiction under section 42 High Court Act 1991, and grant the relief sought, and not whether shareholders can bind directors to a course of action.




[39] Whilst I agree with Mr. Caine that disclosure of bank account details can be made to foreign jurisdictions under certain legislative provisions, such as sections 17, and 24 Criminal Justice Act 1990, section 21 Criminal Justice Act 1991, section 52 Drug Trafficking Act 1996, and the Anti-Terrorism & Crime Act 2003, and that some of such disclosures may be in relation to investigations undertaken by foreign revenues, the fact that mechanisms have been developed to provide for disclosure in specific circumstances cannot be taken as implying that, absent the exceptions to the rule in Tournier, there is a public interest in disclosure being permitted in circumstances outwith such legislative mechanisms. To do so would seriously undermine customer confidence in the Islandâs banking sector. In the context of these proceedings any reference to public interest means the interest of the public of the Isle of Man, and not, as argued by Mr. Caine, of the Isle of Man and elsewhere.




[40] The Isle of Man must be vigilant to protect its reputation as a responsible banking and finance sector. The Island has in relatively recent times undergone reviews such as those by OECD, FATF, IMF and the Edwards Commission. The Isle of Man participated in the development of the Model Agreement on Exchange of Information on Tax Matters by the OECDâs Global Forum Working Group on Effective Exchange of Information, which was published in 2002. The standard reflected in the Model Agreement preserves appropriate procedural rights, and safeguards, see Articles 1 and 5.5, and the accompanying commentary. Article 5.5(a) requires that, in order to demonstrate the foreseeable relevance of the information to the request, the competent authority of the requesting party must provide, inter alia, the identity of the person under examination or investigation. Article 5.5(a) must be interpreted liberally so as not to frustrate effective exchange of information; thus where the identity of the account holder is unknown, the requirement may be satisfied by supplying the account number or similar identifying information. Further, the Isle of Man has entered into Information Exchange Agreements based on the Model Agreement with the United States of America, and the Netherlands.




[41] As to Mr. Caineâs submission that it is positively in the public interest for bank account details to be given to foreign jurisdictions, including foreign revenues, to protect the Islandâs reputation as a responsible banking, and finance sector, it may be considered that the Islandâs reputation is protected by information being exchanged in accordance with the principles formulated by the OECD, or disclosed under legislative provisions. I agree with the Attorney General that, again absent the exceptions to the rule in Tournier, there is no public interest in making disclosures in circumstances, which fall outwith the relevant legislation, such as the Criminal Justice Act 1990, or the principles formulated by the OECD. 




[42] Other than disclosure permissible under the exceptions to the rule in Tournier, public policy requires that any information held in the Isle of Man, which is not available in the requesting jurisdiction should not be disclosed unless the circumstances are such that disclosure of the information would be justified in accordance with relevant statutory provisions, or applicable international agreements. In this case, the request for the Information would be considered to be a fishing expedition under such provisions, or agreements.
