Further, the request for the Information in this instance carries with it no compulsion since no order has been made by the Irish High Court. If such an order had been made, there is no public interest in the Isle of Man in enforcing judgments of foreign courts unless a statutory mechanism is available for such enforcement.




[43] I turn to the Preliminary Issues. In the circumstances of this case, it would be against public policy for this Court to exercise its discretion to compel the Directors to comply with the Disclosure Resolution, in each of the circumstances envisaged by the Preliminary Issues. If all, or part of the Information is held by the Bank of Ireland in Ireland then no authority has been produced that mere inconvenience on the part of the Bank of Ireland should be capable of outweighing the duty of confidentiality attaching to the accounts in the Isle of Man. If all, or part of the Information is held in the Isle of Man, then public policy dictates that it should not be disclosed for the reasons set out in paragraph [42] above.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN

title: R v HALLIGAN
text:
Introduction




1. Joseph Shaun Halligan ("the Defendant") is due to stand trial by jury on 16th January 2006 on a count of causing grievous bodily harm with intent contrary to section 33 of the Criminal Code 1872 as amended. The particulars of the offence are that "on the 8th day of January 2005 (he) unlawfully and maliciously caused grievous bodily harm to David Jon Clarke with intent to do him grievous bodily harm".




2. The alleged assault occurred during the early hours of Saturday 8th January 2005 in Castle Street, Douglas. The defendant was arrested shortly afterwards in Pharaoh's Kebab Shop, also in Castle Street. When asked prior to his arrest why he had blood on his hands and his clothing, the Defendant replied that he had had a nose bleed. At Police Headquarters, after being told by the Custody Sergeant that his detention had been authorised, the Defendant made the comment "That blood there is not my blood. It's the bloke that was on the floor who I comforted". When interviewed that afternoon in the presence of the duty Advocate Mr Cordwell the Defendant chose to make "no comment" answers.




3. At the conclusion of the interview the Defendant was bailed pending further Police enquiries. Those enquiries included the recovery of CCTV footage of the alleged assault and of surrounding events. The Defendant was re-interviewed on Wednesday 23rd February 2005 in the presence of his Advocate Mr. Taubitz. Prior to the interview the Defendant was able to view the CCTV footage. Again, when interviewed, the Defendant chose to make "no comment" answers. However, the following prepared statement was handed to the Police: "I wish to help the Police with enquiries in connection to an alleged assault of a Mr. David Clarke. I recall the evening in question. I went to the Outback Pub. I was with Andy Poole & Anthony Darcy. I went outside at about 1.50 a.m. â 2.00 a.m. & I flagged down a taxi for Andy Poole and arranged for him to be taken to the Caledonian. Darcy said that he was hungry and was going for something to eat. I said I would get a taxi. I accept I had been drinking that evening. I have reviewed the CCTV evidence and I accept it was me involved in the incident. The Police have seized my clothing. I believe that the person (Mr. Clarke) on the video hit me first and I acted in self defence. I also believe Mr Clarke lunged at me later outside the bookshop. I understand that the aggrieved party Mr. Clarke has sustained serious injuries. I did not intend for him to be seriously injured. I feel sorry that he has sustained these injuries. I wish I had never gone out that night. There is nothing I can add to the CCTV evidence".




4. At 6.36 p.m. on 23rd February 2005 the Defendant was charged with the offence in question and on 24th May 2005 he was committed to this Court for trial.




5. By way of letter dated 28th June 2005 the Prosecution served the statement of Police Constable 120 Egan Harley Seamus Loader as additional evidence in this case. It reads as follows:-




"I am police constable 120 and currently work in the force's training dept. I am responsible for training the force in defensive skills, these include handcuffing, using batons and incapacitant sprays. A large part of the training involves being able to recognise a person's body language, or non verbal communications (NVC). Being able to read a person's NVC's can be an invaluable skill, as people will naturally exhibit certain signals, especially when preparing themselves to attack or defend. I have been training these subjects for over four years and have attended court to assist in proceedings during that time.




I have viewed a number of CCTV clips from a disc bearing the exhibit no. NO/01. I have studied the journey of David Clarke and Joseph Halligan, as they walked from Strand Street, Douglas, past the Strand Shopping Centre and onto the Castle Street. There is no sound on any of the footage and the image quality varies.




David Clarke is wearing what appears to be a dark suit and white shirt. He is seen walking in the centre of the roadway with Halligan to his left hand side. Clarke travels in a fairly straight line, following the centre drain. His hands are to his sides, free from his pockets as he is walking. They do not rise at any time, as I would expect them to if he was animated or expecting to attack or defend. He does not appear to turn towards Halligan or treat him as a potential threat, right upto the second that Halligan first pushes him over, outside O'Donnells public house. I would expect someone who has even the smallest inkling that someone poses a potential threat, to treat them with a little more caution. This would be shown by them putting distance between themselves and the other, or turning slightly in their direction, or even raising an arm in defence as the initial push came. Clarke appears to do none of these which would indicate that he did not treat Halligan as a threat and expected no attack from him. The quality of the footage is not that clear at the distance of the initial push but it is evident that Clarke continues to walk after Halligan's first push. This is short lived as he is soon pushed to the floor. 




Joseph Halligan, wearing what looks like a dark sweatshirt with writing on the front and jeans, appears more animated than Clarke. On a number of occasions he is seen to raise his hand and point at Clarke's face. He can be seen with head turned facing Clarke, who does not react accordingly, continuing to look forward. Halligan travels alongside Clarke but his path is more erratic, compared to Clarke's straight line of travel. This could be due to Halligan being drunk but could also be a cause of adrenalin being released into his body. This could make a person more 'jumpy' or 'alive'. If Halligan was being aggressive towards Clarke, or was planning to attack him, adrenalin would certainly be released into his body. Prior, to Halligan first pushing Clarke to the floor, it is very clear that Halligan steers himself away from Clarke, giving him a 'run-up'. This indicates that it is a premeditated act, rather than an act of defence.




Having viewed these short clips of footage, it is my opinion that Clarke is submissive throughout towards Halligan. Halilgan is clearly the aggressor and appears to be goading Clarke whilst in Strand Street. As they pass Granville Street and onto Castle Street, Clarke walks looking straight ahead whilst Halligan continues to point at him. Clarke does not try and defend himself and does not pre-empt the attack. It appears to me that Clarke is ignoring Halligan in the hope of him going away".




6. The Defendant now makes application to the Court that the statement of evidence of Police Constable Loader should be excluded on the basis that it is inadmissible.




7. The Defendant was represented by Mr. Taubitz at the hearing of this application on 21st December 2005 and the Attorney General by Mr. Wannenburgh. Prior to the hearing I had the benefit of skeleton arguments from both parties. Since the hearing I have also had the considerable benefit of viewing the CCTV footage. For the record, neither party considered it necessary for Police Constable Loader to attend/give evidence at the hearing. 




Submissions




8. Mr. Taubitz made a number of submissions:-




(i) The duty of an expert is to furnish the judge or jury with the necessary scientific criteria for testing the accuracy of his or its conclusions, so as to enable him or it to form his or its own independent judgment by the application of those criteria to the facts proved in evidence: see Davie v. Magistrates of Edinburgh (1953) S.C. 34 at page 40. Thus the opinions of experts are admissible where they are given to assist the jury in determining an issue comprising a subject of which knowledge can be acquired only by training or expertise.




(ii) Expert opinion evidence may only be received on the subject calling for expertise which a lay person could not be expected to possess to a degree sufficient to understand the evidence in the case unaided. If the tribunal of fact can form its own opinion without the assistance of an expert, i.e., the matter being within its own experience and knowledge, expert evidence is inadmissible because it is unnecessary: see R. v. Turner (Terence) (1975) Q.B. 834 at page 841. 




(iii) The opinions expressed by Police Constable Loader in his statement of evidence are not those of an expert but are ordinary lay person opinions. The opinions expressed by the Officer concern matters well within ordinary human experience and do not fall within any specialised field of expertise. The jury would have not difficulty in viewing the CCTV footage for themselves and forming their own opinions. "At its highest, the evidence in the present case was an opinion on which â¦ the jury were in as good a position as the psychiatrist to assess and without the need for expert guidance" : R. v. Loughran (1999) Crim. L.R. 404.




(iv) The danger of admitting Police Constable Loader's bare opinions is that given his position the jury may accord his opinions some special weight or significance and treat his observations as being the definitive position.




9. In response, Mr Wannenburgh made the following submissions:-




(i) The subjects upon which expert evidence has been received are varied and diverse; there is no definitive list of topics and nor should there be. "It would be entirely wrong to deny to the law of evidence the advantages to be gained from new techniques and new advances in science": R. v. Clarke (1995) 2 Cr. App. R. 425 at page 430.




(ii) There are circumstances where the jury should be warned not to draw their own unaided conclusions. Thus, e.g., where it is anticipated that there will be a dispute as to handwriting, the Prosecution should call expert evidence: see R. v. O'Sullivan (1969) 53 Cr. App. R. 274.




(iii) An expert is now permitted to give his opinion on what has been called the "ultimate issue". However, the judge should make plain that such opinion is not binding upon the jury and that the issue is for them to decide: see R. v. Stockwell (1993) 97 Cr.App. R. 260. Furthermore, the trial process itself guards against the jury paying too much regard to Police Constable Loader's opinions.




(iv) Given the Defendant's admission that it is he who is recorded in the CCTV footage with the complainant, the only issues before the jury are whether or not the Defendant acted in self defence and whether he intended to cause the complainant grievous bodily harm. Police Constable Loader's evidence is intended as no more than an aid to the jury in determining such issues, as the jury is unlikely to have the Officer's experience/expertise as to the recognition and interpretation of body language/non verbal communications. Leave has been obtained to adduce the evidence of a Police Officer, who had closely and analytically studied video films of a brief fracas between rival football supporters in a town centre after a match, identifying persons as those committing acts of violence: see R. v. Clare and Peach (1995) 2 Cr. App. R. 333. 




(v) Even if Police Constable Loader's opinions on the body language/non verbal communications of the Defendant and the complainant are excluded, those parts of the Officer's statement which detail their actions should be allowed into evidence. 




Law




10. As a general rule, parol evidence is not admissible with regard to anything not immediately within the knowledge of the witness; he must speak of facts which happened in his presence or within his hearing. This rule excludes both hearsay and the expression of opinion or belief. 




11. A recognised exception to this rule is expert opinion evidence. At trial, the judge acts as gatekeeper to the admissibility of such evidence. There are three vital pre-requisites to the admissibility of expert testimony.




12. Firstly, is it something outside the competence of the jury (or does it fall within ordinary human experience and so not require an expert's assistance)?: R.v. Turner (Terence).




13. Secondly, is the tendered witness an expert within his field?: R v Silverlock (1894) 2 Q.B. 766.




14. Thirdly and finally, does the evidence relate to what is recognised by the Courts to be a legitimate field of expertise (even if the proposed field is obviously outside the jury's competence and the tendered witness is clearly a specialist within it)?: see R. v. Robb (1991) 93 Cr. App. R. 161 at page 164 where Bingham L.J. pointed out that the Courts would not receive the evidence of an astrologer or a soothsayer (even if they had spent decades mastering their discipline).








Determination




15. In his statement of evidence, Police Constable Loader indicates that he has studied the journey of the Defendant and the complainant as they have walked from Strand Street past the Strand Shopping Centre and on to Castle Street. The Officer then outlines and interprets the parties' respective body language/non verbal communications before concluding "It is my opinion that Clarke is submissive throughout towards Halligan. Halligan is clearly the aggressor and appears to be goading Clarke whilst in Strand Street. As they pass Granville Street onto Castle Street, Clarke walks looking straight ahead whilst Halligan continues to point at him. Clarke does not try and defend himself and does not pre-empt the attack. It appears to me that Clarke is ignoring Halligan in the hope of him going away". 




16. Mr. Wannenburghsubmitted that the evidence of Police Constable Loader is intended as an aid to the jury in determining the issues in this case, i.e., self defence and intent to cause grievous bodily harm. In fact, it is clear that the Officer's evidence relates solely to the issue of self defence. In his statement he does not attempt to outline, or express any opinions on, the physical contact that takes place between the parties other than to indicate that the Defendant pushes the complainant.




17. The CCTV footage shows only the Defendant and the complainant. They can be seen, without difficulty, walking along Strand Street and Castle Street. Physical contact then occurs between them. I consider that the jury will not only be quite capable of observing the parties' respective actions from the footage but also that it will be equally capable of forming its own opinion on the issue of self defence without expert assistance, such matter clearly falling within ordinary human experience. In my view the Officer's opinion would be no more helpful than that of the jury itself but if admitted there would be a very real danger, given his position that the jury might think it was.




18. Consequently, I find the statement of evidence of Police Constable Loader to be inadmissible. In these circumstances I am not required, nor do I believe it necessary, to consider the remaining pre-requisites to the admissibility of expert testimony.




19. I will,however, comment on Mr. Wannenburgh's assertion that the evidence of Police Constable Loader was comparable to the Police Officer's evidence admitted in R. v. Clare and Peach. I disagree with that assertion. The circumstances of that case, the nature of that Officer's evidence and the reasons for the admission of his evidence are far removed from the present case. There, because the acts relied upon were very brief and because there were many supporters/other members of the public milling about creating a confused scene, what was actually being done and who was doing it could only be discerned by close study. The Police Officer had studied the video films closely and analytically. He it was who, together with a colleague, had filmed supporters arriving at the football ground before the match, filmed them whilst they were in the stadium and filmed them as they left. Those colour films were of good quality. The video films made in the street were recorded in black and white. The Police Officer had viewed the recording of the incident about forty times. He had been able to examine it in slow motion, frame by frame, rewinding and playing as frequently as he needed. By studying it in this way, he was able to follow the movements of individuals and see what actions they took. By comparing the individuals performing violent acts with the colour pictures taken before, at and after the match, he claimed to be able to identify not only the violent acts in the street but who was committing them. It was held that the Police Officer had special knowledge that the Court did not possess having acquired such by lengthy and studious application, and that to afford the jury the time and facilities to conduct the same research would be utterly impracticable.
party_one: THE ATTORNEY GENERAL ON BEHALF OF THE QUEEN

title: R v McSTAY
text:
Introduction






1. James Ian McStay ("the Defendant") is due to stand trial by jury on 23rd January 2006 on a count of inflicting grievous bodily harm contrary to section 35 of the Criminal Code 1872 as amended. The particulars of the offence are that "on the 28th day of November 2004, in Douglas, (he) unlawfully and maliciously inflicted grievous bodily harm upon Sean Michael Kelly".




2. Medical examination of the complainant revealed generalised swelling of the left side of his face with abrasions up and below his left eye, the left side of his nose and his upper lip. He also sustained a blow-out fracture of his left orbital floor (eye socket). In the opinion of Mr. Roger M. Godfrey, Consultant Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon at Nobles Hospital, the complainant "sustained multiple blows to his face including a heavy blow to his left eye resulting in an orbital blow-out fracture".




3. The alleged assault occurred during the early hours of Sunday 28th November 2004 in the toilet area of the Venue Nightclub on Central Promenade, Douglas. The Defendant was arrested shortly afterwards outside the Nightclub and, following caution, he replied: "I was hit by someone so hit a male who was in front of me, I hit him". The Defendant was then conveyed to Police Headquarters.




4. At 3.05 a.m. that same morning, at the request of the Police, the Defendant was examined by Dr. Iain Scott Kewley. He is a General Medical Practitioner and also a Police Surgeon to the Isle of Man Constabulary. His statement, which was made on 1st December 2004, reads as follows:-




"On 28th October (November?) 2004, I was asked to examine James McStay (d.o.b. 25.1.87). I was told that he had been involved in a fight in a nightclub. I saw him at 03.05 a.m. He confirmed that he had been involved in a fight and said that he had been punched on the mouth.




Examination showed the following injuries:




1. Damage to the inside of both the upper and lower lips on the left hand side, which appeared to be as the result of impact between the lip and his teeth. There was some localised swelling visible but the injuries were not severe enough to require any direct treatment.




2. A small superficial abrasion on the right thumb, at the base of the nail, at the medial (inside) edge. This was approximately 0.5 cm. in length and had been bleeding. The bleeding had stopped by the time of the examination. He was unable to account for the mechanism of this injury.




There were no other injuries apparent, and in particular no further injuries visible on the hands. Neither of the injuries required any treatment at that time.




In my opinion, the injury described above in (1) is compatible with the story of a blow to the mouth. The injury described in (2) was not accounted for but was sufficiently minor to have been acquired with minimal force."




5. When interviewed that afternoon in the presence of the Duty Advocate the Defendant maintained that his actions had all been in self defence. However, whilst accepting that he may have hit the complainant, he stated that he was "pretty sure pretty certain I never hit anyone cos I would have felt it".




6. In these circumstances, the Police clearly considered that identification was disputed and an identification parade was subsequently held on 10th March 2005. The Defendant was positively identified by two out of three witnesses. When re-interviewed on 27th April 2005, again in the presence of the Duty Advocate, the Defendant indicated that if he was responsible for the injuries to the complainant then he had been acting in self defence.




7. At 9.53 a.m. on 23rd May 2005 the Defendant was charged with the offence in question and on 4th July 2005 he was committed to this Court for trial. The Defendant on that date was represented by his present Advocate Mrs. Jones and included within the committal papers was the statement of Dr. Kewley.




8. The matter first appeared before this Court on 5th September 2005 when a "not guilty" plea was entered. On 19th September 2005 the matter was adjourned for trial by jury to commence on 23rd January 2006 and it was ordered inter alia that all prosecution witnesses, i.e., all those witnesses whose statements were contained in the committal papers including that of Dr. Kewley, be duly summoned. 




9. On 12th October 2005 Mrs. Jones wrote to the Attorney General's Chambers in the following terms:-




"Thank you for your fax of 7th October 2005. We apologise if you believe that we have not complied with the Court Order however there are no witness statements we could agree and we do require all witnesses to attend to give evidence. We enclose herewith a list which was attached to the committal papers. As you are aware we only took over this matter at a late stage. Perhaps you could confirm that this is the extent of the witnesses that the Crown will be calling in relation to the case.




So far as your letter of 16th September is concerned, our client has clearly raised the defence of self defence. We do not see how we could possibly advise him to plead guilty on the basis of your final paragraph of that letter."




No response was received from the Prosecution.




10. By way of letter dated 16th December 2005 Miss Braidwood on behalf of the Attorney General notified the Defence that it was "no longer the intention of the Prosecution to call Dr. Kewley due to your indication that the defence will be self defence".




11. Mrs. Jones, who had been on leave from 14th December 2005 until 3rd January 2006, made the following response on her return:-




"Further to your letter of 16th December 2005 we note that you do not intend to call Dr. Kewley. All witnesses contained within the committal bundles are conditionally bound over to attend trial. If you believe that our only defence is self defence then you are mistaken, this is one of many. For the record we expect Dr. Kewley to be called by the Crown to give evidence. In the circumstances we look forward to hearing from you with confirmation that he will be called."




The Prosecution replied in the negative.




Application




12. The Defendant now makes application to the Court that the Prosecution be ordered to call Dr. Kewley and examine him as part of its case or, at the least, call and then tender him for the purposes of cross-examination.




13. The Defendant was represented by Mrs. Jones at the hearing of this application on 10th January 2006 and the Attorney General by Mr. Neale. Prior to the hearing I also had the benefit of a skeleton argument from the Prosecution.




Law




14. The extent of the Prosecution obligation to call witnesses was considered in R. v. Russell-Jones (1995) 1 Cr.App.R. 538. At page 542 the Court of Appeal set out the following principles which emerge from the authorities and from rules of practice:-




(1) Generally speaking the Prosecution must have at Court all the witnesses whose statements have been served as witnesses on whom the Prosecution intend to rely, if the Defence want those witnesses to attend. In deciding which statements to serve, the Prosecution have an unfettered discretion, but must normally disclose material statements not served.




(2) The Prosecution enjoy a discretion whether to call, or tender, any witness they require to attend, but the discretion is not unfettered.




(3) The first principle which limits this discretion is that it must be exercised in the interests of justice, so as to promote a fair trial. Per Fullagar J. in Ziems v. The Prothonotary of the Supreme Court of New South Wales (1957) 97 C.L.R. 279, 292: "The present case, however, seems to me to call for a reminder that the discretion should be exercised with due regard to traditional considerations of fairness".




The dictum of Lord Thankerton in Adel Muhammed El Dabbah v. The Attorney-General for Palestine (1944) A.C. 156 that the Court will only interfere if the Prosecutor has been influenced by some oblique motive does not mean that the Court will only interfere if the Prosecutor has acted out of malice. It means that the Prosecutor must direct his mind to his overall duty of fairness, as a minister of justice. Were he not to do so, he would have been moved by a consideration not relevant to his proper task â in that sense, an oblique motive.




(4) The next principle is that the Prosecution ought normally to call or offer to call all the witnesses who give direct evidence of the primary facts of the case, unless for good reason, in any instance, they regard the witness' evidence as unworthy of belief. In most cases, the jury should have available all of that evidence as to what actually happened, which the Prosecution, when serving statements, considered to be material, even if there are inconsistencies between one witness and another. The Defence cannot always be expected to call for themselves witnesses of the primary facts whom the Prosecution have discarded. For example, the evidence they may give, albeit at variance with other evidence called by the Crown, may well be detrimental to the Defence case. If what a witness of the primary facts has to say is properly regarded by the Prosecution as being incapable of belief, or as some of the authorities say "incredible", then his evidence cannot help the jury assess the overall picture of the crucial events; hence, it is not unfair that he should not be called.




(5) It is for the Prosecution to decide which witnesses give direct evidence of the primary facts of the case. A Prosecutor may reasonably take the view that what a witness has to say is at best marginal.




(6) The Prosecutor is also the primary judge of whether or not a witness to the material events is incredible, or unworthy of belief. It goes without saying that he could not properly condemn a witness as incredible merely because, e.g., he gives an account at variance with that of a larger number of witnesses, and one that is less favourable to the Prosecution case than that of the others.




(7) A Prosecutor properly exercising his discretion will not therefore be obliged to proffer a witness merely in order to give the Defence material with which to attack the credit of other witnesses on whom the Prosecution rely. To hold otherwise would, in truth, be to assert that the Prosecution are obliged to call a witness for no purpose other than to assist the Defence in its endeavour to destroy the Crown's own case. No sensible rule of justice could require such a stance to be taken.




The Court added that these principles should not be regarded as a lexicon or rule book to cover all cases. There may be special situations that have not been adverted to, and in every case it is important to emphasise the judgment to be made is primarily that of the Prosecutor, and in general the Court will only interfere with it if he has gone wrong in principle.




15.R. v. Russell-Jones was applied in R. v. Brown (D.) and Brown (M.) (1997) 1 Cr.App.R. 112. At page 113 Schiemann L.J., reading the judgment of the Court of Appeal, stated the following: "We consider that nowadays the primary time when a decision should be made as to whether the Crown should call a witness who, to a greater or lesser degree, may give evidence favourable to the Defence is at the plea and directions hearing. Of course circumstances may change thereafter, in which case any decision would have to be reconsidered".




Submissions




16. Mrs. Jones made a number of submissions, which can be summarised as follows:-




(1) It was the Police who brought in Dr. Kewley to examine the Defendant; specifically to look at his hands.




(2) Dr. Kewley is employed as a Police Surgeon by the Isle of Man Constabulary and "all he will do is tell the truth".




(3) The Prosecution chose to include Dr. Kewley's statement of evidence in the committal papers.




(4) There has been no change in circumstances since the serving of the committal papers; "identification" and "self defence" were live issues then and now.




(5) Dr. Kewley's evidence is central to these issues which the jury will need to decide and the Prosecution is under a duty to call all such evidence.




(6) Irrespective of the above, to be notified at such a late stage places the Defence in a very difficult position and "in the interests of a fair trial" the Prosecution should be ordered to call Dr. Kewley.




17. The following submissions were made in response:-




(1) The Prosecution will ensure that the witness summons in respect of Dr. Kewley stands and that he attends Court; in these circumstances he will be available for calling by the Defence.




(2) The Defence has not shown that it will suffer prejudice by having to call Dr. Kewley; indeed, the Defence as a result will be able to obtain its own proof of evidence from him prior to the trial.




(3) Dr. Kewley does not give direct evidence of the primary facts of the case and therefore a limitation which may otherwise have applied does not apply.




(4) The statement of Dr. Kewley was included within the committal papers as it referred to an injury on the Defendant's hand and therefore was evidence supportive of him having been involved in an incident such that he sustained injury to his hand. The indication from the Defence that self defence would be put forward meant that Dr. Kewley's evidence was no longer necessary to the Prosecution case.




(5) The Defence has indicated that Dr. Kewley is important to it in that he confirms there were no injuries on the Defendant's hands of an attack nature and it would therefore be appropriate for the Defence to use this in cross-examination of the Prosecution witnesses who say that his attack was more sustained than his own admissions. "The law does not insist that the Prosecution are obliged to call a witness for no purpose other than to assist the Defence in its endeavours to destroy the Crown's case. Such a course would merely serve to confuse a jury. The Crown's obligation is to make such witnesses available to the Defence so that the Defence can call them if they choose to do so. The jury will then be clear that evidence is led by the party who wishes to rely upon it and can be tested by cross-examination by the other party, if that party wishes to challenge the evidence": per Schiemann L.J. in R. v. Brown (D.) and Brown (M.) (1997) 1 Cr.App.R. 112, 114.




Determination




18. In R. v. Russell-Jones it was stated that the principles relating to the extent of the Prosecution obligation to call witnesses "should not be regarded as a lexicon or rule book to cover all cases in which a Prosecutor is called upon to exercise his discretion. There may be situations to which we have not adverted". I consider this to be one of those cases which turns upon its own very individual facts.




19. I find the following to be of special significance:-




(1) Unlike the vast majority of cases in which the Defence is seeking an Order directing the Prosecution to call or tender a witness whose statement has been included in the committal bundle, the witness in this case is not someone who is unreliable or unworthy of belief. Dr. Kewley is a Police Surgeon to the Isle of Man Constabulary and one whose integrity is not in doubt.




(2) In the Prosecution's Opening Note it is stated that "The Prosecution say that the Defendant's version of events cannot be right. The evidence is that Sean Kelly was hit with a deliberate and purposeful punch or punches not a glancing blow from someone flailing their arms about. Especially from someone who can't even be sure that they connected with anyone whilst they were 'throwing' their arms around".




(3) The Police brought in Dr. Kewley to examine the Defendant, specifically to look at his hands, very shortly after his arrival at Police Headquarters. The Police clearly believed it to be of utmost importance, in view of the allegations, that his hands in particular be examined. Had such examination revealed knuckle damage, I have little doubt that Dr. Kewley would be being called by the Prosecution to give evidence.




(4) Dr. Kewley's evidence is, on any reasonable view, material to the jury's deliberations as to whether it was the Defendant who assaulted the complainant and, if so, whether or not he was acting in self defence.




20. In these circumstances, I consider that the calling of Dr. Kewley or the tendering of him as a witness by the Prosecution would further the interests of justice and promote a fair trial.




21. At the hearing neither Counsel was able to assist on the question of whether this Court has the power to make the Order sought by the Defence. In Archbold 2005 at paragraph 4-276 it is indicated that some authorities suggest the trial judge has the power to order the Prosecution to call a witness but that there are observations to the contrary in other authorities. If, in view of my findings, the Prosecution is prepared to give an undertaking that it will call or tender Dr. Kewley as a witness then I need consider the matter no further. Otherwise, I shall require further submissions.
party_one: THE ATTORNEY GENERAL ON BEHALF OF THE QUEEN

title: R v MILLER
text:
IN THE MATTER of the Drug Trafficking Act 1996
and
IN THE MATTER of JOHN MILLER
and
IN THE MATTER of the Petition dated 15th day of January 2003 
of William John Howarth Corlett Her Majestyâs Attorney General for the 
appointment of a Receiver



Petition




[1] By Petition dated 15th January 2003 brought under the Drug Trafficking Act 1996 (the Act), Her Majestyâs Attorney General seeks that this Court appoints a receiver over the assets of John Miller, and makes consequential variations to an order (Restraint Order) made on 15th September 1999 as varied, by which Mr. Millerâs assets are restrained. 




[2] Mr. Miller, and Shirley Kathleen Faragher were given notice of the Petition, which was called for first day on 11th February 2003. 




[3] The Petition was supported by an affidavit sworn on 15th January 2003 of Neil Stuart Mackie, and subsequently further supported by an affidavit sworn on 23rd January 2006 of Lindsey Jane Bermingham. 




Representation




[4] Mr. D. Griffin appeared for the Attorney General, and Miss E. Rattigan for Mr. Miller. At the hearing on 22nd May 2006, Mr. I. Kermode appeared for Ms. Faragher.












General Background




[5] On 15th September 2000, following trial by jury, Mr. Miller was sentenced to a term of custody for offences, inter alia, of drug trafficking. Mr. Miller was released from such custody on 28th February 2005. 




[6] During the course of the criminal proceedings, the Attorney General made application under section 2 of the Act. On 15th June 2001, following a contested hearing, at which, inter alia, Mr. Miller, and Ms. Faragher gave evidence, I found that Mr. Miller had benefited from drug trafficking, but adjourned determination of Mr. Millerâs realisable property, as defined in section 6 of the Act, pending further information relevant to Mr. Millerâs late fatherâs estate. On 27th July 2001, I certified that Mr. Miller had benefited from drug trafficking in the sum of £34,349.26, and made a confiscation order (Confiscation Order) in such amount.




[7] By orders made by consent on 14th October 2003, and 16th February 2005, the Restraint Order was varied to permit Mr. Miller to purchase a pick-up vehicle, and to expend an amount not exceeding £3,000 on the purchase of garden implements, and equipment to enable him to pursue self-employment as a gardener upon his release from custody. Such latter order also provided that the Confiscation Order was to be satisfied by 30th June 2005. On that day, I made a further order by consent that the Confiscation Order should be satisfied by 30th January 2007, and that Mr. Miller should pay £500.00 on 26th day of each month commencing on 26th July 2005.




[8] As at 22nd May 2006, Mr. Miller had paid £25,422,82, leaving the principal amount of £8,426.44 with interest thereon in the sum of £2,360.22. I record that the Attorney General does not seek interest from 14th October 2003. I further record that the majority of the monies paid towards satisfying the Confiscation Order have derived from Mr. Millerâs late fatherâs estate, and that since the Order of 30th June 2005, Mr. Miller has paid £1,500.00 by three trenches of £500.00 on 12th August 2005, 9th March 2006, and 2nd May 2006.




Evidence




[9] Whilst Mr. Miller had not filed an Answer he had filed his affidavit sworn on 20th March 2006. At the hearing on 7th April 2006, Mr. Miller confirmed the contents of his affidavit, in which he deposed that, since his release from custody, he had had gainful self employment, but that he had experienced more difficulty obtaining work than he had anticipated. He estimated that his total income for the period from his release from custody to the end of January 2006 was approximately £8,000. He stated that he had purchased a pickup vehicle, gardening equipment, and tools during April/May 2005. During the period May to August 2005, he had had sufficient work to enable him to save £2,500 towards the cost of advertising in the âYellow Pagesâ. However, from August 2005 to February 2006 he had had little, or no work. During February 2006, he had begun to receive work enquiries. Mr. Miller expanded upon statements in his affidavit as to his earnings, and his anticipated income with specific examples of work undertaken, and breakdown of gross, and net receipts therefrom. For the then current month of April, Mr. Miller stated that he had only one dayâs work certain. He was unable to give any evidence of meaningful projected income. However, he felt confident that he would be able to satisfy the Confiscation Order, but was unable to support such confidence by any evidence whatsoever. He stated that his assets consisted of the pickup, and the tools, and equipment used in his business.




[10] At the end of Mr. Millerâs evidence, being mindful of section 29(5), and (6) of the Act, I adjourned the hearing to afford Ms. Faragher the opportunity to take legal advice, and to make representations. I also granted Mr. Miller, and Ms. Faragher leave to file affidavits no later than 15th May 2006. 




[11] Prior to the adjourned hearing on 22nd May 2006, Mr. Miller had not filed a further affidavit. Ms. Faragher had filed her affidavit sworn on 15th May 2006, in which she deposed as to her former relationship with Mr. Miller, to whom she has two children, both now adults. I refer to paragraphs 7 to 11, and 16 of such affidavit, which paragraphs read:




â7. During my relationship with Mr Miller I have always worked. I have always paid the mortgage. I was the person who was responsible for earning, paying the mortgage and all outgoings and supporting out [sic] two children. Mr Miller contributed virtually nothing over the years. There was a brief period when Mr Miller operated a gardening business when he did have earnings and for that brief period he did contribute. Basically all expenses were paid by me. Mr Miller has spent many years in prison leaving me to provide for and raise our two children.




8. On 10 May 1999 I received a cheque in the sum of £13,400 from the Advocates who were dealing with the Estate of Mr Millerâs father Eric Kelly of which Mr Miller was a beneficiary. I believe that this cheque was an advance payment of what was to be due to Mr Miller. My understanding of the advancement was that Mr Miller considered this to be his contribution to the financial responsibilities he failed to meet throughout our relationship. I honestly considered it to be payment for what was owing to me and our children. I had supported both myself, Mr Miller and the children for many years.




9. Of the £13,400 on 17 May 1999 I gave Mr Miller £3,000 in cash from the monies received. Mr Miller needed money and we had agreed that I would give him £3,000 and the remainder belonged to me for the reasons I have detailed above.




10. The money has long been spent on maintaining my family and the household. There is none of it left.




11. I am presently in receipt of Job Seekers Allowance of £158.20 per fortnight. This is my only income.


â¦




16. The monies Mr Miller gave me in 1999 were monies that were more than due and owing to me in support of our children. Further, these monies have been long spent.â




Ms. Faragher then continued by disclosing her monthly expenditure, which exceeds her income.




[12] Other than her affidavit, Ms. Faragher did not give evidence. She was, however, represented by Mr. Kermode at such adjourned hearing. 




[13] I record that Ms. Faragherâs evidence in these proceedings was similar, but appears not to be identical, to oral evidence given during the hearing, which led to my judgment of 15th June 2001. At paragraphs 35 to 45 of that judgment, I referred in some detail to Ms. Faragherâs evidence. I refer to paragraphs 100 to104 of such judgment, which paragraphs read:




â100. As to the amount of £13,400 paid by Dickinson Cruickshank & Co from the estate of [Mr. Millerâs] late father to Ms Faragher at the direction of [Mr. Miller], [Mr. Millerâs] evidence and indeed that of Ms Faragher was that such sum was paid by [Mr. Miller] as a contribution towards monies expended by Ms Faragher in the upbringing of their children Barnaby and Jade. Both Ms Faragher and [Mr. Miller] stated that there had been no calculation as to how such sum was made up. Ms Faragher stated that she anticipated that [Mr. Miller] would have contributed to such expenditure when he âfelt he could do soâ and that the monies from his late fatherâs estate in the sum of £13400 was the first opportunity [Mr. Miller] had had to make such a contribution.




101. When he was arrested in 1999 [Mr. Miller] had upon his person the sum of £2340.76. When he was arrested in 1995 again for drug offences, [Mr. Miller] had in excess of £3000 in cash upon his person. [Mr. Miller], however, had made no contribution towards such referred to expenditure from any such monies. [Mr. Miller] would lead the Court to believe that the notebooks and diaries evidenced money lending transactions effected by him for which he might or might not have charged interest. Yet according to [Mr. Miller] and Ms Faragher he was not able until May 1999 to make any contribution to the upbringing of his children, Barnaby or Jade. I remind myself that during her evidence Ms Faragher stated that there was no order of court requiring [Mr. Miller] to pay any periodical or other payments to Ms Faragher relevant to Barnaby or Jade.




102 I refer to the previously cited copy correspondence telephone notes and file notes from the file of Dickinson & Cruickshank & Co. I accept that [Mr. Millerâs] initial enquiry relevant to the progress of the winding up of his late fatherâs estate took place prior to his arrest on 24th April 1999. However, by 5th May 1999 [Mr Miller] had been arrested for drug trafficking offences. I refer particularly to the above cited memorandum of the telephone conversations of 5th May 1999 between [Mr. Miller] and Dickinson & Cruickshank & Co. I pause to comment that [Mr. Miller] did not challenge directly or indirectly the accuracy of such memorandum. The same records that [Mr. Miller] sought payment of the partial distribution of the residuary estate to him in cash. When he was informed that a cash payment was not possible and was requested to name a person who could facilitate the payment of the distribution to him, he suggested Ms Faragher.




103 I remind myself that [Mr. Miller] had previously been convicted of drug trafficking offences and had been the subject of a confiscation order. As at 5th May 1999 [Mr. Miller] had been arrested for drug trafficking offences the subject matter drug being Methylenedioxymethylamphetamine a controlled drug of Class A.




104 I find that the sum of £13400 caused by [Mr. Miller] to be paid to Ms Faragher was not paid under any obligation by [Mr. Miller]. I reject without hesitation [Mr. Millerâs] and Ms Faragherâs evidence that such payment was by way of contribution to Ms Faragher for monies expended by her relevant to Barnaby and Jade or indeed any other expenditure. I find that such monies were a gift by [Mr. Miller] to Ms Faragher and are therefore realisable property.â




Conclusions




[14] Although neither Mr. Miller nor Ms. Faragher sought to argue delay on the part of the Attorney General seeking to enforce the Confiscation Order, Mr. Griffin raised the matter, and submitted that there had been no delay. Since the Petition was filed on 15th January 2003, there have been vacations of hearing dates, and adjournments at Mr. Millerâs request. Further, in the interim, there have been voluntary payments by Mr. Miller. Mr. Griffin further submitted that as soon as it became clear that Mr. Miller was not complying with the 30th June 2005 Order, the Attorney General sought a hearing of the Petition. In support of such submissions, Mr. Griffin referred to Leonard Lloyd v Bow Street Magistrates Court [2003] EWHC 2294 (Admin), and in particular that part of the judgment of Dyson LJ at paragraph 27, which part reads:




âIt follows that, in deciding what is a reasonable time, regard should be had to the efforts made to extract the money by other methods, for example (as in the present case) the appointment of a receiverâ¦â¦ Likewise, if the defendant has been evasive and has avoided diligent attempts to extract the money from him, he will be unable to rely on the resultant delay in support of an argument that his right to a determination within a reasonable time has been violatedâ




Mr Griffin argued that delay in appointing a receiver could lead to criticism of the Attorney General. He further referred to Attorney Generalâs Reference(No 2 of 2001) [2004]1 Cr App R 25; and Crowther v The United Kingdom 53741/00 [2005] ECHR 45. I accept such submissions, and find that any delayhas been at the request, and for the benefit of Mr. Miller.




[15] It was common ground that the statutory requirements under section 29(1) of the Act had been met, namely the Confiscation Order had been made, it has not been satisfied, and it is not subject to appeal. It was further common ground that the Court has a discretion whether to appoint a receiver in respect of Mr. Millerâs realisable property.




[16] Miss Rattigan submitted that a significant proportion of the Confiscation Order has been paid, that Mr. Miller has gainful self-employment, and is confident that he will be able to satisfy such order, that in such circumstances the appointment of a receiver would be unnecessarily burdensome, and add considerably to the expense of the matter generally. Miss Rattigan suggested that, if the Court is minded to appoint a receiver, then it would be preferable to afford Mr. Miller the opportunity to prove that he could satisfy the Confiscation Order by making an unless order by which a receiver would be appointed if Mr. Miller failed to meet any future instalment.




[17] Mr. Kermode submitted that if a receiver is appointed, he will be entitled to pursue all Mr. Millerâs realisable property, which might mean that Ms. Faragher looses her home: it would be unfair upon Ms. Faragher to appoint a receiver. 




[18] At the conclusion of the hearing on 22nd May 2006, I rejected Miss Rattiganâs and Mr. Kermodeâs submissions, and determined to appoint a receiver. 




[19] I refer to section 31 of the Act, and, in particular those provisions which read:




â31 Exercise by High Court or receiver of powers for the realisation of property




(1) The following provisions apply to the powers conferred â




(a) on the High Court by sections 26 to 30; â¦.




(2) Subject to subsections (3) to (6), the powers shall be exercised with a view to making available for satisfying the confiscation order or, as the case may be, any confiscation order that may be made in the defendant's case, the value for the time being of realisable property held by any person, by means of the realisation of such property.




(3) In the case of realisable property held by a person to whom the defendant has directly or indirectly made a gift caught by this Act, the powers shall be exercised with a view to realising no more than the value for the time being of the gift.




(4) The powers shall be exercised with a view to allowing any person other than the defendant or the recipient of any such gift to retain or recover the value of any property held by him.




(5) In exercising the powers, no account shall be taken of any obligations of the defendant or of the recipient of any such gift which conflict with the obligation to satisfy the confiscation order.




â¦â¦â¦â¦â




[20] I agree with Mr. Griffin that the Court when exercising its discretion should be guided by the legislative steer provided by section 31, which steer includes those given by subsections (2), and (5). Mr. Miller has been given every opportunity to satisfy the Confiscation Order. He has sought, and obtained extensions of time. He has made proposals for instalment payments, for example his proposals which led to the 30th June 2005 Order, subsequent to which instead of paying in the aggregate £5,000, he has paid £1,500 by 22nd May 2006. Mr. Miller has not applied to vary such Order. He has not applied for a certificate of inadequacy under section 17 of the Act. Whilst Mr. Miller professed confidence that he will satisfy the Confiscation Order, he was unable to provide any meaningful evidence of projected earnings, or to suggest any realistic proposals to reduce the arrears of instalments under the 30th June 2005 Order, and/or to maintain regular payments. 




[21] I refer to those parts of section 29 of the Act, which read:


â29 Realisation of property


â¦â¦..


(2) The court may appoint a receiver in respect of realisable property.


(3) The court may empower a receiver appointed under subsection (2) under section 26 or in pursuance of a charging order -




â¦â¦â¦â¦




(b) in relation to any realisable property â¦â¦â¦to take possession of the property subject to such conditions or exceptions as may be specified by the court.




(4) The court may order any person having possession of realisable property to give possession of it to any such receiver.




(5) The court may empower any such receiver to realise any realisable property in such manner as the court may direct.




(6) The court may -




(a) order any person holding an interest in realisable property to make to the receiver such payment as it may direct in respect of any beneficial interest held by the defendant or, as the case may be, the recipient of a gift caught by this Act; and




(b) on the payment being made, by order transfer, grant or extinguish any interest in the property.




.â¦â¦..




(8) The court shall not in respect of any property exercise the powers conferred by subsection (3)(a), (5) or (6) unless a reasonable opportunity has been given for persons holding any interest in the property to make representations to the court.â




[22] This Court afforded Ms. Faragher a reasonable opportunity to make representations, in response she filed her affidavit. In such affidavit, Ms. Faragher deposes that âof the £13,400 on 17th May 1999 I gave Mr. Miller £3,000 in cash from the monies received. Mr. Miller needed the money and we agreed that I would give him £3,000 and the remainder belonged to meâ. In my judgment of 15th June 2001, I found that Mr. Miller had gifted £13,400 to Ms. Faragher. I record that I made no reference to Ms Faragher having asserted that she had given back £3,000 to Mr. Miller.




[23] If the best way of enforcing a Confiscation Order is the appointment of a receiver, then an appointment lies. In this case, having regard to all matters, I had no hesitation concluding that the best way to enforce the Confiscation Order is to appoint a receiver. Thus at the conclusion of the hearing, I gave my decision that I would appoint Mr. Roger Barrs of Howarth Clark Whitehall, Chartered Accountants as Receiver in respect of Mr. Millerâs realisable property. As to the terms, powers, and conditions of Mr. Barrsâ appointment, I look to assistance from Counsel.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
party_one: HER MAJESTY'S ATTORNEY GENERAL OF THE ISLE OF MAN

title: LEVENTHORPE v THE CHIEF CONSTABLE
text:
Background




1. On the 10th January 2006 Adrian Edward Digby Leventhorpe (the Appellant) having pleaded guilty to a charge of driving having consumed so much alcohol that the proportion of his breath exceeded the prescribed limit was sentenced to 42 days custody, licence endorsed and disqualified from driving for 7 years. He was also ordered to retake his test at the end of the period of disqualification. 




2. On the 11th January 2006 the Appellant lodged his appeal against sentence. The grounds of the appeal were stated to be as follows âThe learned High Bailiff failed to give adequate weight to the Psychiatric Report which detailed the Appellantâs bi polar disorder when considering a custodial sentence. The Appellant will submit that the High Bailiff should have ordered the custodial sentence to be suspendedâ. 




Facts




3. The facts in relation to this matter were as follows. On Tuesday the 22nd November 2005 at about 11 p.m. acting on information received PC 211 Gorry and PC 131 Footer attended the area of Central Promenade, Douglas. At that time they witnessed a green Daihatsu registration EMN 406T drive along the promenade towards Queens Promenade, Douglas. This vehicle was caused to stop and the driver, the Appellant, was subsequently spoken to. PC Gorry stated that he could smell intoxicants from the Appellantâs breath, his eyes were glazed and his speech was slurred. A roadside breath test was conducted which resulted in a positive reading. The Appellant was subsequently arrested and cautioned to which he made no reply. At Police Headquarters the Appellant gave two samples of breath on the lion intoxilyzer. The lowest reading being 146ugs. At 11.30 a.m. on Wednesday 23rd November 2005 at Police Headquarters the Appellant was interviewed on tape under caution. He stated that he had been drinking vodka and beer since midday and used the vehicle to drive to an off licence to purchase more drink. At 12.54 p.m. on Wednesday 23rd November 2005 at Police Headquarters the Appellant was charged with the offences for which he appeared before the High Bailiff. Under caution he made no reply. 




4. We also note the previous conviction in October 1996 for driving a motor car with excess alcohol. Some time ago now but a serious aggravating factor. 




5. We have full regard to the points raised in mitigation by Mr OâNeill in the court below and to the aggravating factors. 




Sentencing Remarks of the High Bailiff




6. We also have full regard to the sentencing remarks of His Worship the High Bailiff. 




7. We refer to the following extract from such remarks. The extract appears on page 5 of the transcript: 




âIâve had to have regard as to whether prison will be so detrimental to you that it would be oppressive for me to send you there, because the views of the courts are well known, this is the second time youâve been before the Court, once in England now before me, for an offence of this nature. Custody is the obvious thing the Court has in mind. I have to decide whether having looked at the reports, prison would be so detrimental as I say to be oppressive to you. Dr Haq makes no such observation. As regard to the history of this matter and to your previous history I think and I regret to say this, but I would be failing my duty if I werenât to mark this offence with a custodial sentence. Itâs one of the more serious matters that come before the Court. As I say you have a previous conviction and you were four times the limit. Iâm sure that the shame and disgrace will have an effect on you, and it may indeed make you in future behave and try and co-operate. But at the moment Iâm sorry that custody is the only sentence I thinkâs appropriate and I donât pass the sentence because I want to, itâs because I feel I have no alternative. I will reduce the normal sentence, a normal sentence would have been round about 60 days plus for this sort of offence Mr Leventhorpe. You will go to prison for 42 days, licence is endorsed. I was minded to say well you should be disqualified or, whether it should be open ended, but Iâm going to disqualify you in the circumstances for 7 years, youâll take your test again at the end of the period and your disqualification also applies in the United Kingdom. Thank you.â




8. Those were the closing words of the High Bailiffâs sentencing remarks in respect of this case. 




Reports




9. We have considered all the documents, evidence and submissions placed before the court including the psychiatric report of Dr Haq dated the 3rd January 2006 and the social enquiry report completed on the 22nd December 2005. 




10. We note from the social enquiry report at paragraph 2.4 that the Appellant accepts responsibility for his behaviour. He also accepts that he has to take the consequences for his actions. 




11. We have also had regard to the psychiatric report of Dr OâSullivan dated the 12th day of January 2006. We gave leave to the Appellant to adduce this further evidence on appeal. We have considered that report very carefully and we note in particular the reference that the doctor is concerned that a further period in prison will cause the Appellant to regress and to have a severe exacerbation in his mood disorder. The Respondent submits that such statement lacks further particularity. 




Submissions




12. Mr OâNeill for the Appellant accepts that the High Bailiff was correct to impose a custodial sentence in all the circumstances of this case. 




13. Mr OâNeill submits that in considering whether to suspend the sentence the High Bailiff had regard to the correct provisions but failed to give sufficient weight to the psychiatric evidence before the court in relation to the bi polar disorder and its relevance to the actual offence and its affect on the Appellant whilst in custody. 




14. Mr OâNeill also refers in detail to the report of Dr OâSullivan. Mr OâNeill submits that the High Bailiff should have suspended the sentence or imposed a suspended sentence supervision order. 




15. Mr OâNeill has raised every conceivable point on the Appellantâs behalf. 




16. Miss Braidwood for the Respondent submits that the sentence imposed by the High Bailiff on the information available to him was not excessive and was entirely appropriate and right in principle. 




Legal Principles




17. The relevant legal principles are well settled. This court would not normally interfere with a sentence imposed by a court below unless that sentence was shown to be manifestly excessive or wrong in principle (Perryv Clague 1961-1971 MLR 162). We note also the sentencing guidelines in Cooper v Oake 1987-89 MLR 390 and Thompson v Oake 1993-95 MLR 11. 




18. There is no suggestion that the High Bailiff did not have regard to the relevant sentencing guidelines. 




19. We have regard to the judgment of this court in Jameson delivered on the 29th April 2005 and the position in respect of suspended sentences. The circumstances and the medical evidence in Jameson were very different to the circumstances and medical evidence in the case before us this afternoon. 




Determination of the Appeal




20. In the case we are presently dealing with it is plain that the High Bailiff took full account of the condition the Appellant suffers from. There are references to this in the exchanges with counsel and in the High Bailiffâs sentencing remarks. 




21. Moreover it is plain that the High Bailiff, albeit without the benefit of the report from Dr OâSullivan of the 12th January 2006, had regard to the affect a custodial sentence would have on the Appellant in particular taking into account the medical condition of the Appellant. 




22. There were serious aggravating factors in this case. The Appellant had a previous conviction for a similar offence albeit some considerable time ago. Moreover we have regard to the very high reading of alcohol. The High Bailiff referred to the history of the lack of co-operation with respect to treatment. The Appellant made a conscious decision to drive motivated by the fact that he had run out of alcohol and wanted more. The High Bailiff took into account the medical condition of the Appellant. That is clear from his sentencing remarks and from the exchanges with counsel. It is for the prison authorities to deal with any medical issues in respect of those in custody. 




23. The High Bailiff took into account the Appellantâs medical condition and imposed a much reduced period of custody. 




24. The sentence imposed by the High Bailiff cannot be faulted. There are no grounds which would justify this court interfering with the sentence imposed by the High Bailiff. It was not manifestly excessive and it was not wrong in principle. It follows for those reasons that we dismiss this appeal.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
party_two: MICHAEL CULVERHOUSE - THE CHIEF CONSTABLE OF THE ISLE OF MAN

title: WIFE v HUSBAND
text:
1. The Parties married on the ***** 1990; they had been in a relationship for about ten years prior thereto. Their relationship was of a considerable duration, but it floundered in 2001 or 2002 and a final divorce Order was made on the *****2004. I shall continue to describe the Parties, for convenience, as the husband and the wife. 


2. There are three surviving children of the marriage; two daughters now aged 13½ and 11¾ years, and a son who is nearly 7½ years. The wife is a little over 41 years old and the husband is 46 years today.


3. Matters concerning residence of and contact with the children have been dealt with by a Consent Order of the 7th December 2005. The children will reside in England with their mother. The matter before the Court presently is the wife's application by affidavit of the 18th October 2004 for ancillary relief. There is substantial agreement, but one large stumbling block.


4. In respect of child maintenance there was an agreement, in theory a consent interim Order, but I believe it was never formalised, that the husband pay £100 per week in total for the children. It has not been suggested that that should change and I will make an Order that the husband pay such sums through the Court and through the wife to the children for the usual duration. For the sake of neatness the Order will be expressed as £33.34 per week for the eldest child and £33.33 per week for each of the others.


5. The former matrimonial home is in the wife's sole name, but it is agreed that it is a joint asset. There is no mortgage and the value is agreed as £200,000. There are two Royal London policies in the husband's name, again joint assets. They have a value of £22,857.09 now, but the parties agreed that they should share the value as at separation £17,715.15. That, says Mr Stanley, is that. An asset pool of just under £218,000.


6. The wife is in a settled relationship with a gentleman in England. He owns his house, subject to mortgage. The wife said that the intention was that his house will go into their joint names and that she will contribute to the property by injecting capital and in due course income.


It is the husband's wish, if he is able, to buy out the wife's interest in the joint assets. That is acceptable to the wife provided the price is right.


7. Miss Bolton for the wife puts the joint assets rather higher than Mr Stanley. She says that there is no issue over pensions nor over the contents of the former matrimonial home. Where they differ is in respect of the hotel and business where the husband works. He is a director of the company which runs the hotel and business. His mother and stepfather are the other directors. The wife believes that the husband has an interest in the business. The value of the business is £351,000 and the claim is that the husband has a one-third interest and that the wife has an entitlement to one-half of that interest. Thus, says Miss Bolton, the total asset pool is of the order of £335,000 and the husband should pay something approaching £170,000 to buy the wife out. Accordingly the Parties are about £60,000 apart. Alternatively, it is put, it is fair that the wife has some recognition for the work she herself put in in building up the business.


8. I shall review the evidence. I say at once, however, as I did at the hearing, that I am quite satisfied that the husband has no present interest in the business other than as an employee. I heard his evidence, I heard his mother's and step-father's evidence. I saw the relevant documents. The hotel is owned by his mother and her husband. The business is run by a limited company which they own. Prior to incorporation they ran it as a partnership. The husband, that is to say the Respondent husband has no financial interest in the hotel or the business.


9. The wife's written evidence, her statement of the 24th November 2005, does not refer to the hotel. Her affidavit of the 18th October 2004 said this:



"4. Prior to the purchase of (the former matrimonial home) I lived with the Respondent at the (hotel) which is owned and operated by the Respondent and his mother and step-father â¦ .


9. â¦ . The hotel business is owned by the Respondent and his family and I believe he has a considerable interest in it. I put the Respondent to proof as to the value of this interest but I believe the Respondent has now been appointed a Director by his mother and step-father and whether or not he has a defined shareholding it is reasonable to expect he shall have at some time in the near future even if he has not quantified it yet. I contend that I personally contributed to building up the business working in the hotel as manageress for about 15 years. I believe I am entitled to some compensation for this contribution."



As aforesaid, I reject the claim that the husband owns any part of the hotel or business. As will appear, he does derive benefits from it in addition to his wages, but any interest that he has is an expectancy.


10. The wife's oral evidence: the former matrimonial home was bought with joint monies; she had worked in the hotel and for about four years, outside as a ******* manager and manager ********** for the ********. All wages of both parties went into a joint account and savings. They lived and worked in the hotel, the house was not used for accommodation until separation. Their normal living expenses, the wife had a credit card which the husband paid. The house was bought for £37,000 in 1995 and they did not need a mortgage. The wife could not recall just how the purchase price was paid. Apart from the matters with which the Court is presently concerned marital assets were divided. The wife re-affirmed her belief that the husband has an interest in the hotel and business and said that he deals with everything at the hotel and has always lived and worked there. The wife had nothing to do with the financial side. The husband, "always said it was for our future." The husband has a flat at the hotel; the hotel, or part of it, is in the process of being turned into apartments and "there was talk of him having one of them."


11. The wife was cross-examined as to her belief about the hotel and had to concede that there was no documentary evidence to support her. She stressed the amount of work that he puts and has put into the business and said "it is his life. He puts into it for the future. If he was busy he would say he was working for us for the future."


12. The husband's affidavit is dated the 12th November 2004. He had not filed any further statement of his own. In as much as his affidavit needed updating he dealt with that orally. He confirmed that he lived and worked at the hotel. He has private quarters there and anticipates having the "use of one or two apartments within the aparthotel for future personal use." He confirmed the purchase of the former matrimonial home from joint savings free of mortgage. His recollection was that their savings had been augmented by the surrender of an insurance policy and that there was still a shortfall on the purchase price which was made up by his mother. That house was bought in a derelict and uninhabitable state. The original intention had been to refurbish and sell it and the husband said that from 1995 to 2000 he personally spent considerable time and money on the refurbishment. The wife did not agree that the husband had done a lot of the physical work himself, but agreed that he had done some, as, she said, she did too and agreed that he continued to do maintenance work on it. 


13. I have not commented on the wife's present income. She is not presently working, but hopes to in due course. She made it clear that this was not an income case, save for child maintenance. It is, however, necessary for me to consider the husband's income and the wife's income in earlier years. It is accepted that when the Parties were together their incomes, whether from the hotel or the wife's work outside the hotel, were pooled. The hotel, as the husband's mother put it, is and always has been a cash driven business. Wages, including the husband's, are paid in cash. His present wage is £245 per week. It has increased to that over the years. Not a lot, it is said, for the work he puts in. There is, however, more. He has produced his tax returns, assessments and correspondence. He is treated as receiving benefits in kind at the hotel, accommodation, food, and the tax authorities increased his taxable income by £40 per week on this basis. There is a loose or casual arrangement between him and his parents. He does not necessarily take his wage on a weekly basis. Nor does he necessarily take only his wage. He helps himself with his parents' consent to what he needs when he needs it. He has been taxed on such sums. Neither he nor his parents keep records of precisely what he has. His mother wrote to the Income Tax Division on the 4th July 1999, "I wish to confirm to you that at the moment, my son receives from me due resources each year and has done so for the past number of years while he has lived and worked at the (hotel). He receives these in addition to the set wage paid by the hotel. As this is from me personally, I have kept no record of the amounts involved each year, but estimate that these have ranged from £2,000 to £8,000 to date." The Treasury replied to the husband in November, 1999, that such monies would be regarded as taxable income. In evidence the husband estimated that such gifts are presently £2,000 to £3,000 per year. The husband has the use, if not exclusive, the vast majority use, of a car owned by the hotel. He pays the insurance â he has a maximum no claims bonus.


14. It is clear to me and not disguised by the husband that the hotel and business are worth more to him than his basic wage. All in I would estimate that it is presently worth not less than £20,000 per annum to him. Still not a king's ransom; dependent upon the goodwill of his parents; still on an income basis; a far cry from his having an interest in the hotel and business.


15. The husband has a brother, not involved in running the hotel; an architect; he has supplied plans in respect of the re-developments. The husband fully expects that one day he will have an interest in the hotel. So do his parents. I saw and heard from them. They are in their mid-sixties, and seem hale and hearty. They have another dwelling which is rented out. They have no immediate thoughts of retirement and even when they retire from active involvement in the business it by no means follows that they will retire from ownership of the hotel or as shareholders of the Company. 


I am in no doubt that the husband has and has earned a substantial expectancy, but that it may be many years yet before he comes into it.


16. The question is to what extent, if any, I should heed the benefits which the husband presently receives and the expectancy in dividing capital between him and the wife. 


I have rejected the initial approach ,that the husband has a current interest which should be regarded as a marital asset.


What of the wife's input to the business, her contribution? It may be said that any claim that she would have on that score would be a claim against the husband's parents. Realistically, however, she was paid for her work and prior to separation had the same bonus from benefits in kind and extra payments that the husband bad. She has already benefited from her contribution; to the extent that she and the husband, then 31 and 35 years old respectively, were able to acquire, mortgage free, albeit in need of modernisation a property the value of which they have increased between five and six-fold in ten years.


17. In closing, Miss Bolton moved away from the more precise claims of contribution and the husband's interest to the White principle of fairness and the yardstick of equality. There is no dispute as to the application of the yardstick of equality in respect of the martial assets.


Miss Bolton presented a small bundle of authorities. She did not refer to all of them. Al Khatib v Masry [2002] 2FCR 539 I consider has no application to the present case nor J v J [1955] 2 ALL E.R.85. As aforesaid, there was reference to the well known principles of White v White [2000] 3.FCR. 555. Miss Bolton referred too to Thomas v Thomas [1995] 2.FLR 668 and to extracts from Rayden on Divorce. Mr Stanley submitted extracts from Butterworth's Family Law Service and Sweet and Maxwell's Encyclopaedia. 


18.The Statutory checklist, section 32(2) of the Matrimonial Proceedings Act 2003 must be considered. In White it is said that the objective of the checklist is to achieve a fair outcome. The Courts often treat inherited wealth rather differently from other assets and are even more reluctant to take cognizance of any expectancy. My approach is to see whether it is necessary to have recourse to the expectancy to achieve fairness within the terms of the statutory checklist. 


To start at the end, I hold that it is not and I am satisfied that I should not adjust the wife's entitlements. An equal share in the agreed assets will, in round terms, give to the wife a sum of £110,000. I shall make an Order providing for the payment of such sum to the wife within a reasonable time. Should the husband be unable to buy the wife out within such time, the property must be marketed and upon sale the wife must receive the first £110,000. The husband to retain the balance and the two policies. There will also be the child maintenance orders. 


The wife is already rehoused with her new partner, whom she intends to marry. With £110,000 she is well placed to buy into his property if that is, as she described, her wish. The wife told me little of her partner's circumstances. She said that they considered their relationship, now of four years, permanent. That he owns, subject to the mortgage, his detached, four bedroomed house; that she did not know his income, but that his job in Health and Safety for a private company is secure; he does maintain his three children from a previous marriage. As aforesaid, the wife has made it clear that this is not an income case and I am satisfied that there are no considerations under s32 2(a) to influence me. Likewise under 2(b) I am satisfied that the needs of the parties are met by the Order which I make. I have considered the Parties' ages, their standard of living during the marriage and their respective contributions. Conduct I do not see as an issue. Each party raised matters of health, but not to the extent that I was invited to consider, nor do I consider such as bearing upon my determination. I was specifically advised that pensions are not an issue.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN

title: R v BELL
text:
Background




1. By application dated the 1st December 2004 the Defendant submits that his arrest at 13.00 hours on the 30th June 2004 was unlawful. Paragraph 13 of the application signed by Mrs Jones as advocate for and on behalf of the Defendant reads: 




âIt is submitted that as the arrest as [presumably that should read was] unlawful no charges should be preferred and as such these whole proceedings are invalidâ. 




2. There was no reference in the application to a request that the counts in the information before the court be dismissed or stayed and no specific relief is referred to in the application. 




3. When the matter came before the court for hearing on the 30th December 2004 Mrs Jones indicated that she was not putting forward an abuse argument and indicated that her submissions were not based on the fact that the review did not take place on time but on the fact that the Defendant was released from detention and then re-arrested. Mrs Jones submitted that the Defendant could not be validly charged under such re-arrest.
