Mrs Jones submitted that the re-arrest was unlawful and the charges and proceedings which followed invalid. 




4 The Defendant is facing serious counts or charges in the information. In the first count the statement of offence is possessing a controlled drug with intent to supply contrary to section 5(3) of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1976. The particulars of the offence are that Spencer Elliot Bell on the 29th June 2004 in the Isle of Man had in his possession a controlled drug of class A namely ecstasy with intent to supply it to another in contravention of section 4(1)(b) of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1976. In the second count the statement of offence is possessing a controlled drug contrary to section 5(2) of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1976 and the particulars of the offence are that Spencer Elliot Bell on the 29th day of June 2004 in the Isle of Man unlawfully had in his possession a controlled drug of class A namely ecstasy in contravention of section 5(1) of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1976. 




5. In the committal papers filed with the application there is a statement of police constable 111 John Henry Mitchell dated the 12th day of July 2004 and it indicates that on Tuesday the 29th June 2004 he spoke to the driver of a green coloured Citroen Saxo who he recognised as Spencer Bell (âMr Bellâ). He informed Mr Bell that he was being detained for a drug search. He asked Mr Bell if he had anything in the vehicle or on him. Mr Bell stated:




âThere are some pills under the front seat, a 1,000 pillsâ. 




6. Mr Bell was informed that he was under arrest on suspicion of possession of a Class A drug with intent to supply. He made no reply after caution. Mr Bell was searched and subsequently transported to Police Headquarters where the arrest was authorised. While in the custody suite P C Mitchell says he read out the notes he had made in his pocket note book in relation to Mr Bellâs comment regarding a 1,000 pills in his car and Mr Bell signed the pocket note book to indicate that the comments written down were a true and accurate account of what was said. The Saxo was searched. Various exhibits were seized including a Hulk packed lunch bag. 




7. In the statement of detective constable 54 Darren James Burns dated the 1st July 2004 DC Burns stated that various white tablets were recovered from the Saxo. One of the tablets was tested. It gave a positive indication for ecstasy. He estimated that there was 900 tablets in total with a âstreet valueâ of £5,400. 




8. In the statement of detective constable 107 Stephen Martyn Maddocks dated the 1st July 2004 DC Maddocks indicated that at 9.05 p.m. on Tuesday 29th June 2004 he searched 5 Ballure Cottages Maughold and seized various items. DC Maddocks stated:




âAt 1p.m. [that would have been on the 30th June 2004 although the statement does not say that] at Police Headquarters I further arrested Mr Bell on suspicion of possession of a Class A controlled drug namely Ecstasy and also on suspicion of supplying a Class A controlled drug namely Ecstasy. He made no reply after caution.â 




9. At 3.40 p.m. DC Maddocks charged Mr Bell with possession of a Class A drug namely ecstasy with intent to supply and possession of a Class A controlled drug namely ecstasy. DC Maddocks says Mr Bell made no reply after caution.




10. On the 29th November 2004 the Defence faxed to the court clerk copies of certain correspondence including a letter from Jones and Taubitz dated 5th October 2004 to the âHigh Bailiffâs departmentâ with a copy to Helen Mason prosecution department Police Headquarters. It stated:




âIt is our intention at Court on the 12th October to confirm that there is a case to answer against Mr Bell and that therefore a âPaper Committalâ should take place. We would however wish to state on the record that this is without prejudice to the Defences right to challenge the validity of the proceedings at a later dateâ. 




11. For the purposes of this application let me assume, without deciding or determining the matter, that the facts as put forward on behalf of the Defendant in relation to what happened just before and during the Defendantâs presence at Douglas Police Headquarters on the 29th and 30th June 2004 are correct. In any event there is little dispute on the material facts. 




12. The material facts appear to be as follows:




At 19.40 hours on the 29th June 2004 the Defendant was arrested on suspicion of possession of a Class A drug with intent to supply. He arrived at Police Headquarters at 20.05 hours and his detention was authorised at 20.14 hours. The Defence say that the reasonable grounds for the arrest and indeed the evidence in the case was the observations of two police officers. A search of the Defendantâs home address was concluded by 21.55 hours. The Defendantâs detention was authorised to âsecure/preserve evidence â interview.â The Defendant was sober and fit to be interviewed. The Defence say that under the Police Powers and Procedures Act 1998 a review must take place after 12 hours detention. The Defence say that the Defendant should have been reviewed by 8.05 hours. The Defendant was not reviewed by that time. The entry by Inspector Kinrade at 13.15 on the 30th June 2004 in the custody record reads as follows âSgt KINLEY spoke to me at home at 07.40 hours today. Review conducted over telephone after circumstances had been told to me. Stated that I would make every effort to be at DPHQ prior to 0815 to conduct review. Arrived at HQ at 0817 and conducted review with who I thought was Spencer BELL. MCDONALD the other prisoner who was spoken to by myself in the Charge Room answered to the name of Spencer and gave me no reason to suspect that he was not BELL. This custody record was filled in as I thought it and had no reason to doubt that it was not BELL I was reviewing. As soon as mistake noted D/Pâs advocate Mr Cordwell advised. Advice then taken from Linda WATTS at AGâs office and then above action taken. Reviewed at 12.57 hours by myself in presence of advocate. Representations made that detention was unlawful. At 12.59 hours I released D/P from detention. He was immediately re-arrested for the same offences by DC Maddocks. Sgt KINLEY accepted charges. Rights re-issuedâ. The entry at 15.40 indicates that the detained person was charged and bail was refused. The Defendant appeared before the court of summary jurisdiction on the 1st July 2004 and was released on bail that morning subject to conditions. The custody record indicates âtic 36 hours 27 minsâ. The Defence say that the Defendantâs detention after 08.05 on the 30th June 2004 was unlawful. Reviews are mandatory. The Defence say that at 13.00 on the 30th June 2004 the Defendant was arrested for the same two offences he had been released from detention for. There was no different or further evidence. The Defence submit that the arrest was unlawful. The Prosecution deny that the subsequent arrest was unlawful. The Defence refer to section 50 of the Police Powers and Procedures Act 1998 which they submit supports the proposition that a person can only be re-arrested if there is new evidence justifying a further arrest which has come to light since his release. The Defence submit that no such evidence existed as can be seen from the committal papers and the custody record. 




13. In her oral submissions before the court Mrs Jones on behalf of the Defendant focused attention on the unlawfulness of the arrest and submitted in effect that charges which follow an unlawful arrest are not valid charges and as such the proceedings are invalid. At the hearing on the 30th December 2004 I indicated to the Defence and the Prosecution that the court would require further assistance in connection with the legal consequences flowing from an unlawful arrest on the assumption that there was an unlawful arrest in this case. I adjourned the application to the 7th January 2005 to give the Defence and the Prosecution a further opportunity of filing written submissions and authorities on this point. The matter could not proceed on the 7th January 2005 as Mrs Jones was ill. The matter was adjourned to the 12th January 2005. The court received further written submissions and authorities and had the benefit of further oral argument on the 12th January 2005. 




14. The Prosecution admit that the review did not take place when it should have taken place. The Prosecution submit that the police did not act in bad faith. The Prosecution say that the procedure that was followed was proposed and approved by the advocate then representing the Defendant. I have no evidence on that point before me other than the references in the custody record and it is not admitted by the Defendant so I disregard it for the purposes of the application presently before me. It is recorded in the custody record that the advocate then representing the Defendant did make representations to the effect that the detention was unlawful. The Prosecution say there is no requirement to interview a suspect. No evidence was gathered by the police as a result of the review not taking place when it should have, submit the Prosecution. The Prosecution submit further that the evidence of the Defendant having committed the offences remains cogent and admissible. The Prosecution submit that the Defendant should not escape prosecution based on a procedural irregularity in the circumstances of this case. 




15. That there was a procedural irregularity (a failure to review within 12 hours pursuant to the Police Powers and Procedures Act 1998) seems to be common ground between the Prosecution and the Defence. What is not common ground is whether the subsequent arrest of the Defendant after the period of unlawful detention was an unlawful arrest. The Prosecution say it was a lawful arrest. The Defence say it was an unlawful arrest. Even assuming for the purposes of this application (without deciding or determining the issue) that the arrest was an unlawful arrest what are the consequences of such? 




The Issues




16. In considering this application I focus on the legality of the detention and the consequences of any unlawful detention and any subsequent unlawful arrest. I consider the consequences on the assumptions that the detention after the relevant review period was unlawful and on the assumption that the subsequent arrest was unlawful. I make it clear that I am making these assumptions for the purposes of considering this application but I have not made any determinations as to whether the subsequent arrest was unlawful or not. Although the application was not presented on the basis of a stay, in light of the additional authorities lodged recently by the Defence, I also consider the courtâs power to stop a prosecution and whether a stay should be granted in the circumstances of this case.




The consequences of an unlawful detention




17. Let me first consider the consequences of an unlawful detention. Under Section 37 of the Police Powers and Procedures Act 1998 (the Act) a person arrested for an offence shall not be kept in police detention except in accordance with the provisions of Part IV of the Act. Under section 43( 1) of the Act reviews of the detention of persons in police detention in connection with the investigation of an offence shall be carried out periodically in accordance with the provisions of the section. Section 43(3)(a)(i) of the Act provides that subject to sub section (4) the first review shall be, in the case of a person who has been arrested but not charged, not later than 12 hours after the detention was first authorised. Section 43(4) of the Act indicates the circumstances in which a review may be postponed. The Prosecution have not argued that a postponed review would have been justified in this case and it appears to be common ground that none of the postponed review provisions are applicable in this case. 




18. Section 50 of the Act concerns bail after arrest. Section 50(2) of the Act provides that nothing shall prevent re-arrest without warrant of a person released on bail subject to a duty to attend at a police station if new evidence justifying a further arrest has come to light since his release. In this case the police had refused bail. The police did not release the Defendant on bail. Mrs Jones submits that an individual who is detained and not released on bail or who is released unconditionally should be in no worse position as regards police powers of re-arrest than an individual who is released on bail. Section 50 of the Act does not however prevent the re-arrest of an individual, whether released on bail or not, where there is no new evidence. It simply provides that nothing shall prevent re-arrest if new evidence justifying a further arrest has come to light since the release of an individual. 




19. Nothing I say in this judgment should detract from the importance of complying strictly with the provisions of the Police Powers and Procedures Act 1998. As Clark L.J. stated in Roberts v Jones (Chief Constable of the Cheshire Constabulary) [1999] 2 Cr. App. R. 243 (the âRoberts caseâ) âThere are rules, the police must stick to themâ. That obviously is correct but what the court must consider in detail is the consequences of any breach of the rules and the court must respond in a fair and proportionate way to any breach.




20. Part IV of the Act exists in order to ensure that members of the public are not detained save in certain defined circumstances. In all other circumstances every member of the public is in principle entitled to his liberty unless there are other provisions authorising his detention. We are here dealing with an area of law where specific provisions have been laid down by Tynwald to protect the individual against the abuse of the powers of arrest and detention. In his useful book The Police and Criminal Evidence Act1984 4th edition Professor Michael Zander QC at page 135 paragraph 4-56 states : âthe failure to conduct a review at the proper time can be actionableâ and then goes on to refer to the Roberts case (a case attached to the bundle filed by Mrs Jones). The civil claims in the Roberts case were claims for compensatory damages in respect of false imprisonment. Professor Zander refers to the award of £500 damages made to Mr Roberts and states: 




âThe basis of such a claim is that failure to conduct a review at the time â at least if it was due to oversight or neglect â means that any subsequent detention is unlawful. It also follows that any confession or admission that emerges during that period of unlawful detention may be held to be inadmissible under section 78â. 




21. Professor Zander refers to section 47 of the Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 (Act of Parliament) at paragraph 4-127 on page 153 and under the section headed âBail after arrestâ states:




âThere is nothing wrong with a person being arrested for one offence and bailed and then charged later with a different offence arising out of those same factsâ. 




22. The point raised by Mrs Jones however is that the Defendant has not been charged with different offences. Professor Zander deals with the new evidence point at paragraph 4-132. 




23. Where there is a breach of the statutory review requirements a defendant may have a successful civil claim for false imprisonment as in the Roberts case. In that case it was held that where a review had not been carried out as required by statutory provisions in England a suspectâs detention was not in accordance with those provisions. It mattered not that the detention would have been lawful if the review had been carried out at the prescribed time or that there were grounds which would have justified continued detention. In the Roberts case the suspect would have been entitled to the benefit of a review by a certain time. In the absence of that review he was in principle entitled to his liberty. His further detention was therefore unlawful. In the Roberts case the consequence of the unlawful detention was that the plaintiff was entitled to damages for false imprisonment. 




24. What other legal consequences may flow from an unlawful detention? If a confession or an admission is obtained during the period of the unlawful detention it may be held to be inadmissible under section 13 of the Criminal Justice Act 1991.




25. The case of the Attorney General v Shillito 2001-03 MLR 356 gives a clear example of the consequences that can follow if the police commit significant and substantial breaches of the Police Powers and Procedures Code Order 1998. In that case evidence was excluded under section 13 of the Criminal Justice Act 1991 since it would have had an adverse effect on the fairness of the proceedings. My brother Deemster His Honour Deemster Kerruish at paragraph 28 of his judgment (page 366 of the report) stated:




ââ¦ I accept that a breach of Code C does not lead automatically to exclusion of the subject-matter evidence. When there is a breach, the judge has a discretion to exclude the evidence under section 13 of the Criminal Justice Act 1991 â¦ But the breach of the Code must be âsignificant and substantialâ. The more so, the more likely the judge is to exclude the evidence â¦.â 




There was no suggestion that the breach of the Code or the Act would lead to criminal proceedings being declared invalid and stopped in view of a procedural irregularity.




26. Deemster Kerruish also touched upon the general nature of the discretion under section 13 of the Criminal Justice Act 1991 in Re Ironside 1999-01 MLR 177. In considering fairness a balance has to be struck between that which is fair to the prosecution and that which is fair to the defence. Moreover if there has been a procedural irregularity the courtâs approach should be proportionate. It cannot seriously be submitted that in every case involving a procedural irregularity the subsequent criminal proceedings are invalid and must be stayed. 




The consequences of an unlawful arrest




27. Let me now refer to the consequences of an unlawful arrest. Even assuming in favour of the Defendant for the purposes of this application that his arrest was unlawful it does not follow from that assumption that the subsequent charge and subsequent proceedings are invalid as submitted by the Defence. 




28. When one looks at the authorities on unlawful arrest and the consequences flowing from an unlawful arrest one sees scattered throughout the law reports civil actions for damges in respect false imprisonment. One needs only to take the famous example of Christie v Leachinsky [1947] AC 573 (House of Lords) a case well known by all first year law students. That famous case was a civil action involving allegations of false imprisonment and unlawful arrest. Section 31 of the Act reflects the position at common law as set out in Christie v Leachinsky. See also Taylor vChief Constable of Thames Valley Police [2004] 1 WLR 3155. Under section 31(1) of the Act subject to subsection (5) an arrest is unlawful unless a person who is arrested is informed that he is under arrest as soon as is practicable after his arrest. Under sub section (3) no arrest is lawful unless the person arrested is informed of the ground for the arrest at the time of, or as soon as is practicable after, the arrest. The Privy Council case of Hussein v Chong Fook Kan [1970] A.C. 942 was a case concerning an unlawful arrest and false imprisonment and damages. Riley v DPP [1990] 91 Cr. App. R. 14 was a case concerning the validity of an arrest and an alleged assault on a police officer in the execution of his duty. 




29. Leaving aside the cases on abuse of process no authority has been put before the court which supports the proposition that one of the consequences of an unlawful arrest is that any subsequent charge and any subsequent criminal proceedings are thereby rendered invalid. The closing paragraph of the Defendantâs application dated 1st December 2004 states that as the arrest was unlawful no charges should be preferred and as such âthese whole proceedings are invalidâ. 




30. Section 34 of the Act refers to arrest for further offence. Where (a) a person (i) has been arrested for an offence and (ii) is at a police station in consequence of that arrest and (b) it appears to a constable that if he were released from that arrest he would be liable to arrest for some other offence he shall be arrested for that other offence. In R v Samuel [1988] QB 615 at 622 it was indicated that the reason for this rule (which is in section 31 of the English Act) is to prevent the release and immediate re-arrest of an alleged offender. 




31. The liberty of the subject is of vital importance in this country and the police need to act within the rule of law and justify arrests and detentions. The House of Lords in A(FC) and others v Secretary ofState for the Home Department [2004] UK HL 56 on the 16th December 2004 confirmed the importance of the liberty of the individual. The liberty of the subject has always had a special place in the jurisprudence of common law jurisdictions. DaltonâsCountryJustice reflects this when dealing with arrest and imprisonment it is stated at page 406 âThe liberty of a man is a thing specifically favoured by the common lawâ. The common law has also recognised that human beings make mistakes and a lot of the cases in the law reports focus on the consequences of mistakes made by human beings. At one extreme are serious mistakes with serious consequences. At the other extreme are minor mistakes with less serious consequences and in between is an infinite variety of mistakes of different degrees and different consequences. Nothing I say in this judgment should be taken as distracting from the importance of complying with the Act and the common law and the importance of ensuring that all arrests are lawful and that all periods of detention are lawful. The main points I have to consider in this case however are if there has been a breach of the review requirements of the Act in effect because of human error committed in good faith and if there has been an unlawful arrest what are the consequences of such breach and of such unlawful arrest? The legal authorities show that in certain circumstances evidence obtained during the wrongful or unlawful detention period may be excluded and moreover an individual may be entitled to damages for false imprisonment. 




Human Rights Act 2001




32. The Defence did not present arguments on the basis of the Human Rights Act 2001 but, again in fairness to the Defendant, I have felt it proper to consider the position under the Human Rights Act 2001.




33. The substantive provisions of the Human Rights Act 2001 are sadly not yet in force. Defendants on this Island however should not be disadvantaged due to the Governmentâs delay in bringing into force the substantive provisions of the Human Rights Act 2001. The sooner those provisions come into operation the better for all concerned. I refer to my judgment in CP 2004/50 in the IIM Limited case (reasons for judgment delivered at Douglas on the 29th November 2004). It is right and proper that this court has regard to the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. I have briefly considered the Convention and the relevant European jurisprudence on this point although I have not had the benefit of argument. Article 5 of the Convention refers to the right to liberty and security. Article 5(1) provides that everyone has the right to liberty and security of person. No one shall be deprived of his liberty save in certain specified cases and in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law. Under Article 5(2) everyone who is arrested shall be informed promptly in a language which he understands of the reasons for his arrest and of any charge against him. Article 5(4) provides that everyone who is deprived of his liberty by arrest or detention shall be entitled to take proceedings by which the lawfulness of his detention shall be decided speedily by a court and his release ordered if the detention is not lawful. Article 5(5) provides that everyone who has been the victim of arrest or detention in contravention of the provisions of the article shall have enforceable rights to compensation. Article 5 provides certain rights and it can be seen in relation to Article 5(5) that anyone who is a victim of arrest or detention in contravention of those rights should have an enforceable right to compensation. That again seems to support the proposition that if there is an unlawful arrest or an unlawful detention then the victimâs remedy lies with a civil action for compensation for false imprisonment rather than an order that any subsequent criminal proceedings against him be stopped. I have considered the Law of Human Rights by Richard Clayton and HughTomlinson the section on arrest commencing at paragraph 10.28 at page 458. Most of the cases refer to actions for false imprisonment. At paragraph 10.77 on page 476 under the heading âcompensation for unlawful detentionâ the learned authors indicate that there is no general right to compensation which arises where a person is unlawfully detained. It is indicated that damages are available where a person is falsely imprisoned and where he was in custody as a result of being maliciously prosecuted but not if an individual was negligently prosecuted. 




The power to stop a prosecution




34. The application of the Defendant in this case was not styled as an application for a stay on grounds of abuse of process and indeed Mrs Jones in her oral submissions on the 30th December 2004 made it quite plain that she was not applying to the court for a stay on grounds of abuse.




35. For the sake of completeness I should state however that I accept that this court has the jurisdiction to stop a prosecution proceeding in certain circumstances. Such jurisdictions is only exercised in exceptional cases. 




36. I accept that the court does have a discretionary power to in effect stop criminal proceedings continuing. See for example Falconer-Atlee 58 Cr. App. R. 348 at page 357 English Court of Appeal Criminal Division and R v Brown 1998 Crim. L. R. 196 regarding the power of the trial judge to withdraw a case from the jury. 




37. Moreover it is well established (see for example the judgment of the Staff of Government Division in the Lawrence appeal 2DS 2004/14 judgment delivered on the 28th July 2004) that the courts have power to stay criminal proceedings. The authorities indicate that a judge has power to intervene if the prosecution amounts to an abuse of the process of the court and is oppressive and vexatious (DPP v Humphreys [1977] AC 1 at 46). The authorities also make it crystal clear that the power to stop a prosecution arises only when it is an abuse of process of the court and it should only be exercised in the most exceptional circumstances. It may be an abuse if either (a) the prosecution have manipulated or misused the process of the court so as to deprive the defendant of the protection provided by the law or to take unfair advantage of a technicality or (b) on the balance of probability the Defendant has been or will be prejudiced in the preparation or conduct of his defence by delay on the part of the prosecution which is unjustifiable. A stay on the ground of delay should only be ordered in an exceptional case where a Defendant establishes, on a balance of probability, that the delay has caused him to suffer serious prejudice such that no fair trial is possible. The Defence do not complain of delay in this case. The Defence refer to procedural irregularities. If there was evidence of bad faith or a deliberate manipulation of the process to cause prejudice to a Defendant then no doubt the court would take such matters into account in deciding whether to stop the prosecution. 




38. In written submissions filed on the 5th January 2005 Mrs Jones refers to the cases of R v Horseferry Road Magistratesâ Court ex parte Bennett (HL) [1994] 1 AC 42 (the âBennett caseâ) and the case of Nicholas Robert Neil Mullen (CA) [1999] 2 Cr. App. R. 143 (the âMullenâ case). Mrs Jones draws an analogy between an unlawful arrest and abuse of extradition proceedings. Mrs Jones submits that in the case presently before the court the Crown has âfrom the very beginning of the process within this jurisdiction acted unlawfullyâ (paragraph 4). Mrs Jones however at paragraph 2 of the written submissions filed on the 5th January 2005 accepts that âthe original arrest was lawful. It is the second arrest which was unlawful and therefore these set of circumstances are exceptionalâ. Mrs Jones submits that âthese particular set of circumstances â¦ should attract an automatic bar to the proceedings as opposed to the exercise of discretionâ. 




39. Let us analyse the Bennett case and the Mullen case in more detail. Mrs Watts refers to them as extreme cases. 




40. In the Bennett case the defendant in a criminal matter had been brought back to the United Kingdom in disregard of available extradition process and in breach of international law and the laws of the state where the defendant had been found. It was held in the House of Lords that the courts in the United Kingdom should take cognisance of those circumstances and refuse to try the defendant. It was further held that the High Court in England, in the exercise of its supervisory jurisdiction, had powers to inquire into the circumstances by which a person had been brought within the jurisdiction and, if satisfied that there had been a disregard of extradition procedures, it might stay the prosecution as an abuse of process and order the release of the defendant. The High Court had a discretion. The defendant in that case said that he was placed on a flight from South Africa to the United Kingdom in defiance of an order of the Supreme Court of South Africa. The defendant was in effect forcibly abducted in violation of local and international law. It was a serious case of a deliberate abuse. The facts of the Bennett case before the House of Lords in London in 1993 are very far removed from the facts of the Bell case before the Court of General Gaol Delivery in Douglas in 2005. In stark language Lord Bridge in the Bennett case states at page 64 that the appeal raises an important point in principle â 




âWhen a person is arrested and charged with a criminal offence, is it a valid ground of objection to the exercise of the courtâs jurisdiction to try him that the prosecuting authority secured the prisonerâs presence within the territorial jurisdiction of the court by forcibly abducting him from within the jurisdiction of some other state, in violation of international law, in violation of the laws of the state from which he was abducted, in violation of whatever rights he enjoyed under the laws of that state and in disregard of available procedures to secure his lawful extradiction to this country from the state where he was residing?â 




Lord Griffiths reviewed the authorities on extradition and abuse of process and at page 61 stated:




âIf the court is to have the power to interfere with the prosecution in the present circumstances it must be because the judiciary accept a responsibility for the maintenance of the rule of law that embraces a willingness to oversee executive action and to refuse to counternance behaviour that threatens either basic human rights or the rule of law. My Lords, I have no doubt that the judiciary should accept this responsibilty in the field of criminal law â¦ if it comes to the attention of the court that there has been a serious abuse of power it should, in my view, express its disapproval by refusing to act upon it â¦ The courts, of course, have no power to apply direct discipline to the police or the prosecuting authorities, but they can refuse to allow them to take advantage of abuse of power by regarding their behaviour as an abuse of power and thus preventing a prosecutionâ. 




Lord Bridge at page 67




âTo hold that the court may turn a blind eye to executive lawlessness beyond the frontiers of its own jurisdiction is, to my mind, an insular and unacceptable view. Having then taken cognisance of the lawlessness it would again appear to me to be a wholly inadequate response for the court to hold that the only remedy lies in civil proceedings at the suit of the defendant or in disciplinary or criminal proceedings against the individual officers of the law enforcement agency who were concerned in the illegal action taken. Since the prosecution could never have been brought if the defendant had not been illegally abducted, the whole proceeding is taintedâ.






Lord Lowry at page 74




ââ¦ I consider that a court has a discretion to stay any criminal proceedings on the ground that to try those proceedings will amount to an abuse of its own process either (1) because it will be impossible (usually by reason of delay) to give the accused a fair trial or (2) because it offends the courtâs sense of justice and propriety to be asked to try the accused in the circumstances of a particular case. I agree that prima facie it is the duty of a court to try a person who is charged before it with an offence which the court has power to try and therefore that the jurisdiction to stay must be exercised carefully and sparingly and only for very compelling reasons. The discretion to stay is not a disciplinary jurisdiction and ought not to be exercised in order to express the courtâs disapproval of official conductâ. 




At page 77B Lord Lowry, no doubt with the principle of proportionality in mind, continued: ââ¦ I would not expect a court to stay the proceedings of every trial which has been preceded by a venial irregularityâ. 






Lord Lowry at page 77F raises the question âWhy should the court not stay for abuse of process if the accused has been wrongfully arrested in the United Kingdomâ and answers his general question on wrongful arrest in the context of an extradition case as follows:




âA person wrongfully arrested here can seek release by applying for a writ of habeas corpus but, once released, can be lawfully arrested, charged and brought to trial. His earlier wrongful arrest is not essentially connected with his proposed trial and the proceedings against him will not be stayed as an abuse of processâ.




41. In the Mullen case the appellantâs presence within the jurisdiction was secured by unlawful means. It was held that it was open to the Court of Appeal to find a conviction unsafe because of abuse of process prior to the trial. Reference was made to the Bennett case and also to the comments of Lord Steyn in R v Latif [1996] 2 Cr. App. R. 92:-




âThe law is well settled. Weighing countervailing considerations of policy and justice, it is for the judge in the exercise of his discretion to decide whether there has been an abuse of process, which amounts to an affront to the public conscience and requires the criminal proceedings to be stayedâ.




On the facts of the Mullen case Rose L.J. concluded that there had been an abuse of process and therefore the prosecution and conviction of the appellant were unlawful. 




42. Mrs Jones usefully brought to the courtâs attention (albeit on the afternoon of the hearing) the case of R v Great Yarmouth Magistrates, ex.p. Thomas, Davis and Darlington [1992] Crim. L.R. 116. In that case the applicants were remanded in custody charged with the illegal importation of cannabis. A 70 day time limit had expired and the prosecutor sought an extension of time which was refused. The applicants were released. Fearful that they would disappear without trace, the prosecutor re-arrested them before they could leave the building, and later charged them with possession of cannabis with intent to supply. The defence alleged abuse of process. It was held, granting the applications and remitting the case to the magistrates, that the essence of the defence case was an allegation that the prosecutor had dishonestly contrived to manipulate the process so as to avoid the consequences of the English legislation which brought the applicants within the custody time limit regulations. That involved the contention that, in substance, the fresh charges were identical to the first charges. In this case there was an allegation that the prosecution was brought as a consequence of mala fides by the prosecution. The Divisional Court in England held that such an issue was an issue for the magistrates and was not a matter for the Divisional Court in the circumstances of the case before them. It could only be determined by the justices upon whatever evidence was put before them and, with echoes of the principle of proportionality, it was stated that âthe effect of that must then be put into perspective.â The justices must bear in mind that the burden of establishing dishonesty lay fairly and squarely on the defence, and it was a heavy burden. If they were satisfied that it was truly a consequence of dishonesty which brought about the further charge and there was, in substance, no difference between that and the first charge, plainly there had been an abuse of process.




43. Professor J C Smith QC in his commentary on this case states:




âCharges should be brought because they are charges upon which it is proper that the accused should be tried and, if found guilty, convictedâ.




Professor Smith refers to Rotherham J.J. ex. P Brough [1991] Crim. L.R. 522 and quotes the statement that to be an abuse there had to be âsome element of bad faith or sharp practice or oppressive or overreaching behaviour or, to use a colloquialism, some form of dirty trickâ. Where a process is used for some purpose other than that for which it is intended, the distinction between an error of judgment and an abuse of process may be rather fine.




44. In the case before this court there is no allegation of dishonesty, bad faith or dirty tricks on the part of the prosecution and indeed there is no evidence before the court of any dishonesty, bad faith or dirty tricks. The evidence before the court indicates that the prosecution made a genuine mistake in reviewing the wrong detainee. 




45. Mrs Jones, again leaving it to the afternoon of the hearing, also helpfully brought to the courtâs attention DPP v Chippendale [2004] EWHC 464 (the Chippendale case). Most of these cases on abuse of process are fact sensitive and the Chippendale case is no exception. Mrs Jones refers to paragraph 10 of the judgment of Nelson J. where there is reference to a concession made by the Crown at the magistrates court that if the arrest in that case was unlawful âthen it would follow that the proceedings were invalid and it would be right for the matter to be dismissed on the submission of no case to answer being madeâ. At paragraph 14 of the judgment it is stated that âwhere somebody is arrested unlawfully, charged improperly after that unlawful arrest bailed in accordance with the charge that has already been made against him and taken to the magistrates upon the basis of that charge, that is an unlawful basis and his appearance before the court is unlawful. Hence it cannot be said that he can be successfully prosecuted; indeed the whole proceedings are invalid. That is not a matter which is accepted by the Crown today, indeed it is submitted by the Crown that section 31(1) of the 1998 Act defines the offence. That is entirely separate from the power of arrest and that for the offence to be committed no warning is necessary. Furthermore, once the person is brought before the court, if that offence has been committed he can properly be dealt with before the court provided the ingredients of the offence have been made outâ. 






At paragraph 17 Nelson J. states:




âI, for my part, am satisfied that the Crownâs submissions are correct. It seems to me that the warning is required for arrest, but is not an essential ingredient of the offence itself. The justices may, if the offender is brought before the court, even though the charge itself may not have followed a correct arrest, hear that matter and convict were they to find that the offence itself had been committedâ.




May L.J. at paragraph 20 states:




âI also agree that a technical unlawfulness in the arrest in the present case did not and should not have disabled the magistrates from proceeding to hear the charge of the offence that was alleged against him under section 31(1).â




46. In my judgment the Chippendale case turns on its own facts and a close analysis of certain provisions of the Crime and Disorder Act 1998 and the Public Order Act 1986. The general comments in the case have however been of some assistance. 




47. In additional written submissions filed on the 6th January 2005 Mrs Watts maintains that there was no unlawful arrest. Mrs Watts indicates that a review of the authorities shows that in order for an arrest to be lawful there must be reasonable grounds to suspect (in this case) that an arrestable offence has been committed and at least a possibility that a charge will follow. Mrs Watts submits that there has been no abuse of the process of the court or indeed of executive power pre-charge in this case. The prosecution submit that where, as in this case, there is reliable, admissible evidence that an offence has been committed it is in the interests of justice that the case proceeds. For example of where a technical mistake has been made in good faith causing a failure to adopt the correct procedure and there was no stay of proceedings the prosecution point to the case of Dean and Bolden [1998] 2 Cr. App. R. 171. In that case the English Court of Appeal (applying the Bennett case) held that the failure to adopt the correct procedure was a technical mistake made in good faith so that there were no âvery compelling reasonsâ for exercising the courtâs discretion to grant a stay in the case before it. Mrs Watts also refers to the case of R v Rotherham MagistratesâCourt ex p. Brough [1991] C.O. D 77-171, 89 which I have considered. In that case it was held that the conduct of the prosecution in connection with certain procedural matters was incorrect and lacked judgment but there was no element of misconduct or bad faith. The court did not interfere with the conclusion of the justices at first instance that the prosecution had not abused the process of the court.




48. Mrs Jones did not put her case on the basis of bad faith. Mrs Jones submitted that the police knew they had got it wrong and took steps to deal with their error. Mrs Watts submitted that the police did not act in bad faith and have gained nothing from the procedural irregularity. 




49. In considering applications for stays the authorities make it plain that the court must weigh in the balance a number of factors including the right of the defendant to a fair trial, the gravity of the offence (although avoiding the temptation that the end justifies any means argument â indeed when the cards are stacked heavily against a defendant the court should take particular care to ensure that the defendantâs rights are protected), the public interest in ensuring that the innocent are acquitted and the public interest in the guilty being brought to justice. I remind myself that the Defendant presently enjoys the presumption of innocence but there can be no doubt that he faces extremely serious charges. The sentencing remarks of this Court in other cases and the judgments of the Staff of Government Division (see for example the Todd case 1993-95 MLR 330) have covered the evils of those involved in the drugs trade. As Lord Bingham stated in McIntosh v LordAdvocate [2001] 2 All ER 638 at 641 âthe unlawful consumption of drugs, particularly class A drugs, is a very grave, far-reaching and destructive social evilâ. I agree. There is no doubt that the Defendant is facing grave and serious charges of criminal conduct. 




50. The application of the 1st December 2004 makes no reference to a stay but I have nevertheless, in fairness to the Defendant, considered the position in relation to stays and the circumstances of this case. I have asked myself the question if there was an application for a stay before the court on the facts of this case would I grant such an application. The ultimate objective of the discretionary power to stop a prosecution is to ensure that there should be a fair trial according to the law which involves fairness both to the defendant and to the prosecution. Fairness of a trial is not all one sided. It requires that those who are undoubtedly guilty should be convicted as well as those about whose guilt there is a reasonable doubt should be acquitted. The issues in the case presently before me relate to an unlawful detention and an unlawful arrest and the consequences flowing from such matters. What the authorities show is that the courtâs influence in connection with matters outside proceedings in the court is, save in exceptional cases, somewhat limited. If however there is an abuse of the process by the prosecution the court does have ample powers in exceptional cases to prevent the prosecution from proceeding. I am not satisfied that the case before me is such an exceptional case and in any event no formal application for a stay has been made and on the facts before the court (taking the Defence case at its highest) it would be bound to fail. The Defence have not persuaded me that there has been a serious abuse of power by the police or the prosecuting authorities in this case which amounts to an affront to the public conscience or that there are other âvery compelling reasonsâ (to use the words of Lord Lowry in the Bennett case) which require these proceedings to be stayed. Indeed, on the contrary, in my judgment if these proceedings were stayed that would be an affront to the public conscience in the circumstances of this case. I have not been persuaded that it would be offensive to this courtâs sense of justice and propriety to be asked to try the Defendant in the circumstances of this case.




51. Mr Bell has yet to plead to the charges and presently enjoys the presumption of innocence. It is not for me to determine the innocence or guilt of the Defendant. If he pleads not guilty and is acquitted after a trial he will be innocent. If he pleads guilty or is found guilty after a trial he will be guilty. Those issues are yet to be determined. Mrs Jones is right to submit that this court should be concerned to maintain the integrity of the criminal justice system. This court is indeed concerned with the maintenance of the integrity of the criminal justice system. As regards the position generally, I remind myself that the criminal justice system does not involve a game where otherwise guilty defendants should be permitted to rely upon technical irregularities to, in colloquial terms, âget away with itâ. In more judicial terms I would refer to the comments of Lord Justice Auld in the case of R v Gleeson (the Court of Appeal Criminal Division Times Law Reports 30th October 2003) where Lord Justice Auld is credited with the comments âa criminal trial is not a game. Its object is to ensure that the guilty are convicted and the innocent acquitted.â Those comments also reflect the comments of Lord Scarman in the Sang case (1979) 69 Cr. App. R. 282 at page 307 where Lord Scarman indicated that the test of unfairness is not that of a game. A defendant should not be permitted to take unfair advantage of a technicality or procedural irregularity to escape trial any more than the prosecution should be permitted to take advantage of a technicality as the authorities on abuse of process make plain. If Mr Bell is innocent he should be acquitted. If he is guilty he should be convicted and dealt with accordingly. Justice must be done in respect of Mr Bell and in respect of the community in which he lives. The Court of Appeal also made it clear in the case of Vincent Munnery (1992) 94 Cr. App. R. 164 that in a criminal prosecution it was justice that mattered both to the public as represented by the prosecution as well as to the defence. I am not for one moment suggesting that the police should feel free to breach mandatory statutory or common law requirements and cause unlawful detentions or commit unlawful arrests. Nor am I suggesting that the unlawful interference with the liberty of the individual should be regarded as a mere technicality or a minor procedural matter. I am however suggesting that defendants against whom there is a prime facie case to answer should not be permitted to walk away from the consequences of their potentially serious criminal conduct by endeavouring to rely upon procedural irregularities committed by the police in good faith by inadvertence or neglect (rather than in bad faith or otherwise in circumstances where an abuse of process argument would be successful). In cases where the prosecution have acted in bad faith or otherwise abused the process then the court does have ample power to stop a prosecution. I entirely accept that the court can order a stay of proceedings in the absence of bad faith. This court in a proper case will not hesitate to intervene if the police or the prosecution authorities have seriously abused their powers. There is however no evidence of a serious abuse in the circumstances of the case presently before me which would justify an order being made in effect stopping these proceedings. 




52. I have not been persuaded that in the circumstances of this case the prosecution acted in bad faith or deliberately endeavoured to manipulate the process to disadvantage the Defendant or otherwise abused the process to an extent that would justify this court staying the existing criminal proceedings. In this case there appears to have been a genuine procedural irregularity committed in good faith through inadvertence or neglect. That procedural irregularity may enable the Defendant to commence a civil action for false imprisonment and to claim damages. I say no more about that. 








Conclusions




53. I have carefully considered the application, the submissions and the relevant law. Even taking the Defendantâs application at its highest and assuming all factual matters in his favour I have not been persuaded that a breach of the provisions of the Act (in particular section 43(3)(a)(i)) or an unlawful arrest in the circumstances of this particular case would justify me in stopping this prosecution and bringing an end to these present proceedings in the Court of General Gaol Delivery against the Defendant. There are no valid grounds for a stay or a dismissal of the prosecution or a declaration that the proceedings are in some way invalid. In these circumstances I dismiss the application of the Defendant and I do not grant an order preventing this prosecution from continuing.
party_one: THE ATTORNEY GENERAL ON BEHALF OF THE QUEEN

title: FATHER v MOTHER
text:
REASONS FOR DECISION




1. These reasons should be read in conjunction with my judgment of the 29th September making a contact order in favour of (the father) in respect of C, his daughter, now 4 years old.




2. I had adjourned further consideration of the father's application for parental responsibility to today, 25th January 2006, saying that I was sympathetic to the application, provided that the contact were successfully rekindled. Today I do indeed order that the father have that parental responsibility.




3. The mother has opposed the application. She says that it is too early, premature, to consider it; the relationship between herself and the father has not improved; if anything happened to her a grant of parental responsibility would put the father in a strong position to obtain residence of C and could lead to parting C from her siblings.




4. The last of these objections carries no weight at all. I cannot hypothesise as to the demise or incapacity of the mother. I have no reason to believe that she will not be around to remain C's main carer, if that remains appropriate, during C's minority. I must deal with matters as they are.




5. That it is presently too early; that the application is premature. Again I reject that. It is true that I had hoped that there would have been greater contact between C and her father in the four months since the contact order. That there has not been is attributable to the mother's obstructive behaviour and defiance (which led to her imprisonment for contempt). The mother is now co-operating and her evidence today opened "Generally supervised contact has gone fine." I am satisfied that contact has been successfully rekindled.




6. That the relationship between mother and father has not improved. It is far from clear that it ever will. It may if both are involved with C. In any event it is not in this case a ground to deprive C of the benefit of her father's being involved in decisions on her behalf.




7. C's welfare is the Court's paramount consideration.




8. C has her father's surname. This is a very material consideration to my mind and I adopt the words of Ward, L.J. in in re S (Parental Responsibility) [1995] 2FLR 648 at page 649E, "the implication from that is that the mother recognised at that time that it was right and proper that it be acknowledged in law as in fact that he was the father." Interestingly, in England, though not yet in the Isle of Man, that principle now has statutory force for since the 1st December 2003 by substitution in section 4(1) (a) in the Children Act 1989 of Parliament where a father of a non marital child is registered as father he acquires parental responsibility without more.




9. In in Re H (Illegitimate Children: Father: Paternal Rights) (No. 2) [1991] 1FLR214 at page 218F, Balcombe, L.J. set out a (non exhaustive) list of three considerations for the Court: the degree of commitment shown by father to child; the degree of attachment between them; the father's reasons for applying. The same Judge in Re G (A Minor) (Parental Responsibility Order) [1994] 1FLR 504 at page 508 C to D said that where those considerations are fulfilled, prima facie it is in the interests of the child to make a parental responsibility order. 




10. The father's commitment, subject to one blemish, cannot be doubted. Had he not been committed he would and could have walked away when C was about 8 months old. He did not. He sought and re-sought contact and parental responsibility. Since the order of the 29th September 2005 he has attended for contact as directed by the Court Welfare Officer, secure in the knowledge on several occasions that C would not be there and that the mother would defy the Order. The Court Welfare Officer has been impressed by the father's level of commitment and fully supports his present application. Likewise the degree of attachment between C and her father is not questioned; not even by the mother. That attachment is confirmed by the Manager at the Child Contact Centre. The father's motives in seeking parental responsibility: he was not challenged. He wants to be involved and to do what is right for C.




The blemish to which I referred; his failure to pay maintenance for a number of weeks. A matter of frustration, he said. Maintenance is a separate issue, but I do regard it as of some relevance. I accept that the father now appreciates that he was wrong to withhold payment and that he will take steps forthwith to put matters right.




11. I agree with the Court Welfare Officer's observation that C is entitled to have both parents making decisions for her. Her father's involvement can only operate to her benefit; the more so if in fact it leads to greater communication and co-operation between mother and father.




12. I do add this caveat, as I did in Court, and I base it on the words of Wilson, J., in Re P [1994] 1FLR 578 at pages 584G to 585D. I hope and believe that it is unnecessary for me to give the caveat, but from an abundance of caution I do so: "It is important to be quite clear that an order for parental responsibility to the father does not give him a right to interfere in matters within the day to day management of the child's life. â¦â¦ It is to be noted that on any view an order for parental responsibility gives the father no power to override the decision of the mother, who already has such responsibility; in the event of disagreement between them on a specific issue relating to the child, the Court will have to resolve it."
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN

title: ROMINA PROPERTIES LIMITED and WINKFORD FARM LIMITED and PARSONAGE FARM LIMITED
text:
IN THE MATTER of the Companies Acts 1931 â 1993
and
IN THE MATTER of ROMINA PROPERTIES LIMITED (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of Winkford Farm Limited (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of Parsonage Farm Limited (in voluntary liquidation)
and
IN THE MATTER of the Intended Action in the High Court of Justice of England and Wales

Petition




[1] By Petition dated 1st June 2004, Steven George Taylor (the Liquidator), in his capacity as liquidator of Romina Properties Limited (Romina), Winkford Farms Limited (Winkford) and Parsonage Farms Limited (Parsonage) (together referred to as the Companies), seeks that this Court sanction the terms of an Intended Compromise Agreement (Compromise Agreement) and/or authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the Compromise Agreement.




Representation




[2] At the hearing, Mr. P.B. Clucas appeared for the Liquidator, Mr. P.D. Morris for the Islamic Republic of Pakistan (Government of Pakistan), and Mr. A. White QC with Mr. J.T. Aycock for Mr. Asif Ali Zardari.




General Background




[3] On 2nd October 1995, Romina purchased Rockwood House, Witley, Surrey, England, with 355 acres or thereabouts for £2,225,000. I shall refer to the whole of the hereditaments, and lands so purchased as the âRockwood Estateâ.




[4] Following such purchase, Rockwood Estate was divided into three parcels with Rockwood House and land associated therewith being owned by Romina, Winkford Farm, and lands by Winkford, and Parsonage Farm, and lands by Parsonage.




[5] On 8th February 2002, Romina, Winkford, and Parsonage were each placed into membersâ voluntary liquidation with the Liquidator being appointed liquidator of each such company. In the course of the liquidations the Liquidator has received claims from various creditors, some of which have been paid in full or part, or accepted in full or part.




[6] By the Petition, the Liquidator pleads that the liquidations of Romina, Winkford, and Parsonage arose following assertions by the Government of Pakistan that Rockwood Estate was beneficially owned by Mrs. Benazir Bhutto and/or her husband, Mr. Zardari, and that the monies used to finance such purchase were provided through Bomer Finance Inc. by Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari. The Petition discloses the Liquidatorâs belief that the monies used to effect such purchase were provided from a bank account or accounts numbered 0/342035/019 and 0/342035/027 in the name of Bomer Finance Inc., a company incorporated on 25th April 1991 in the British Virgin Islands. The Liquidator also pleads that consequent upon assertions that such financing emanated from alleged illegal acts of corruption, the Government of Pakistan made various claims against the Rockwood Estate and/or the Companies, either as a creditor or by way of proprietary claim. The Liquidator further pleads that the Government of Pakistan has indicated that it will take action in the English courts, by which it will seek declarations that it is beneficially entitled to the Rockwood Estate, and all other assets of the Companies as are found to be derived directly, or indirectly from monies received by, or for the benefit of Mrs. Bhutto, and/or Mr. Zardari as secret commissions, and/or bribes; that Rockwood Estate, and such other assets are held upon constructive trusts for the Government of Pakistan; that the net proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate be given to the Government of Pakistan, and other appropriate relief. The Liquidator pleads that he believes that, if the Government of Pakistan succeeds in such intended action, there will be no assets to meet creditorsâ claims, and the Companies would be insolvent. Thus, he further pleads that on 4th November 2003, he entered into the Compromise Agreement with the Government of Pakistan, which agreement is expressed to be conditional upon approval by this Court. The Liquidator sets out his reasons for seeking the Courtâs sanction at paragraph 22 of the Petition.




[7] The Liquidator pleads that as well as its claim against Rockwood Estate, and the assets of the Companies, the Government of Pakistan has a claim for funding it has provided to the Liquidator in course of the liquidations under clause 5 of the Compromise Agreement.




[8] The Petition is supported by the Liquidatorâs affirmation (the Liquidatorâs first affirmation) sworn on 2nd June 2004 with exhibits thereto, in which affirmation he refers in some detail to the history of the matter, including certain proceedings in this, and other jurisdictions.




[9] In general terms, by his Answer, Mr. Zardari denies that the Rockwood Estate is beneficially owned by Mrs. Bhutto; that the Government of Pakistan would be successful in any action to obtain its desired declarations and relief; and any allegation of corruption. He maintains that each such allegation cannot be properly adjudicated upon in this jurisdiction, and pleads that such allegations are the subject of proceedings in Pakistan, and Switzerland. He pleads that the Liquidator has not seen any, or a substantial part of the material in his defence, and that the Liquidator cannot reach conclusions on matters of which he, or his advisers have no expertise. Mr. Zardari asserts that the Compromise Agreement is invalid under the laws of Pakistan; and denies that the Liquidator is entitled to the relief sought. Mr. Zardari seeks that the Petition is dismissed or stayed; that this Court orders that legitimate creditors be paid out of the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate; that the Liquidator provides an account of his fees, and expenses; and that the net proceeds of the liquidations be paid into court pending further order.




[10] In his Answer, Mr. Zardari, being at the material time incarcerated in Pakistan, refers to the first affidavit (Mr. Bakarâs first affidavit) sworn on 26th October 2004 of Abu Bakar, an advocate retained by Mr. Zardari, in which Mr. Bakar refers to various proceedings, challenges the basis upon which the liquidations were commenced, and challenges the Compromise Agreement. Mr. Bakar further challenges the Government of Pakistanâs allegations of corruption, the conduct of the Liquidator, and the funding of the liquidations. 




[11] By its Answer, the Government of Pakistan supports the Petition, and the relief sought.




[12] By his Reply, the Liquidator pleads that Mr. Zardari, having failed to identify any personal interest in the Companies, and/or the Rockwood Estate, and/or the proceeds of sale thereof, is not a person capable of being affected by this Courtâs decisions; that no creditor, or contributory opposes the Petition; and takes issue with matters pleaded in Mr. Zardariâs Answer.




[13] By its Answer, and Cross Petition, Tower Trustees Limited pleads that it is the trustee of the Winkford and Parsonage Farm Trust, which was created by deed on 6th September 1995; that the sole named beneficiary is the International Red Cross of Geneva; and that the trustee holds, through Bernard Michael Shimmin, the whole of the issued share capitals in Winkford, and Parsonage, which shares are the sole assets of the trust. The Answer records that, following execution of the Compromise Agreement, Tower Trustees Limited, and /or Mr Shimmin as shareholder in Winkford, and Parsonage were approached to consider sanctioning such agreement; that Tower Trustees Limited indicated that it would consider such request , but before doing so intended to give notice to certain interested, or potentially interested parties, including Mr. Zardari. The Answer pleads that, having had benefit of the pleadings with affidavits, and affirmations in support, Tower Trustees Limited proposes to adopt a neutral stance in these proceedings, to comply with any orders for discovery, to rely on this Courtâs decision; and to seek that its costs be borne as part of the payments under the Compromise Agreement, or by another party to these proceedings.




[14] By their respective Answers to the Tower Trustees Limitedâs Cross Petition, the Liquidator raises no objection to the payment of the requested costs, but the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari opposes the same




[15] On 31st May 2005, this Court ordered that Tower Trustees Limited is at liberty to adopt a neutral stance, and to take no active part in these proceedings. I record that Mr Shimmin, and the directors of Winkford, and Parsonage have also adopted a neutral stance. I further record that Legibus Nominees Limited, and Legibus Trust Limited, the shareholders in Romina, have been given notice of these proceedings, but have taken no part therein.




[16] On 16th July 2004, the Liquidator sold the Rockwood Estate, and received net proceeds in the sum of £4,206,781.85 after payment of legal, and estate agents fees.






Compromise Agreement and Side Letter




[17] I pause briefly to refer to the provisions of the Compromise Agreement, which recites that the Liquidator has received formal detailed notice of the Government of Pakistanâs claim to the Rockwood Estate by means of draft Particulars of Claim; and that from the Companiesâ perspectives the Liquidator is satisfied that the agreement represents a satisfactory compromise of such claim. The operative part of the Compromise Agreement makes provision for the sale of the Rockwood Estate, and for unconditional payment of the âNet Liquidation Proceedsâ, as defined in the schedule, to the Government of Pakistan. The operative part further provides that the Liquidator will seek the sanction of the court to the agreement, and to the Liquidatorâs authority to enter into the same, any such sanction to be given in terms to the reasonable satisfaction of the Government of Pakistan; that the Liquidator may seek funding from the Government of Pakistan, any such funding with all amounts then advanced by, or on behalf of the Government of Pakistan to be treated in priority, and to be secured; that, subject to prior termination by effluxion of time, or as otherwise provided, the Compromise Agreement shall continue in full force until such time as all outstanding obligations have been fully discharged and the winding up of the Companies completed, but upon prior termination, the Government of Pakistan shall be entitled to proceed with its claim. The schedule makes provision for the payment to the Government of Pakistan of the âNet Liquidation Proceedsâ, being the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate, and all other assets of the Companies, after repayment of all amounts paid by way of funding; deduction of certain fees, and expenses; the Liquidatorâs âreasonable remuneration in an amount not exceeding £250,000â; and payment of accepted proofs of debt from creditors, but not to exceed to Mr. and Mrs. Bancroft £50,000, to Grantbridge Limited £725,000, and to all other creditors in aggregate £100,000 without the prior written agreement of the Government of Pakistan.




[18] The Compromise Agreement was entered into by the Liquidator and the Government of Pakistan on 4th November 2003. By letter (the Side Letter) of even date therewith, the Liquidator undertook that, save for the completion of the sale of the Rockwood Estate, he would not nor would he permit any of the Companies without the prior consent of the Government of Pakistan to pay out or otherwise dispose of any of the assets of the Companies, including the proceeds of sale of the Rockwood Estate, whether contemplated in the Compromise Agreement or otherwise until the date of the grant of sanctioning of the court of the compromise or the fourteenth day following termination of the agreement, whichever is the earlier; that, subject to funding by the Government of Pakistan, if this Court refuses to sanction the Compromise Agreement, the Liquidator will vigorously appeal against such decision, if required to do so by the Government of Pakistan, or, if any person appealed against any sanction granted, he will vigorously oppose any such appeal or application. The Liquidator also undertook that neither he nor the Companies would have any direct or indirect dealings with Broadsheet LLC, International Asset Recovery Limited, or representatives of either such companies. Such undertakings were given by the Liquidator as liquidator on behalf of each of the Companies.




[19] Although under clause 2.4, the Compromise Agreement was due to expire by effluxion of time on 30th January 2004, by agreement the same has been presently extended so that it will expire on 28th February 2006 unless this Court sanctions the Compromise Agreement, or the Liquidatorâs authority to enter into the same or such sanctioning is waived by the Government of Pakistan, and the Companies.




Government of Pakistanâs Draft Particulars of Claim




[20] In the draft Particulars of Claim referred to in the Compromise Agreement, the Government of Pakistan refers, inter alia, to Mr. Zardari being the husband of Mrs. Bhutto, and having at various times served as a member of the National Assembly, and the Senate, and held federal ministerial positions. It alleges that Mr. Zardari was the trustee of assets and property of Pakistan as were in his possession, or control by virtue of his various offices, that he owed fiduciary duties to Pakistan, and in breach of his duties, he misappropriated and/or diverted monies from Pakistan and/or received secret commissions/bribes. 




[21] Dr. J. Schlegelmilch, a Swiss lawyer, is referred to in the draft Particulars as the âBhutto familyâs attorney in Europeâ, and is alleged to have facilitated a number of banking relationships for Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari with certain Swiss private banks, formed offshore companies, to have facilitated the purchase and subsequent refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate, and at all material times to have been the sole director of Bomer Finance Inc.




[22] The Government of Pakistan pleads that it relies on payments that were made into bank accounts, referred to therein, and for ease herein, as the âZardari Swiss bank accountsâ in the name of Bomer Finance Inc., Nassam Overseas Inc., and Benington Management Inc., each account being allegedly controlled by Mr. Zardari and/or Mrs. Bhutto. It further relies upon certain bank documentation to establish Mr. Zardariâs beneficial ownership of Bomer Finance Inc.âs Citibank account, and certain other accounts within the Zardari Swiss bank accounts. It alleges that such companies were incorporated, or acquired by Mr. Zardari, and/or Mrs. Bhutto âsimply for the purpose of receiving monies misappropriated and/or diverted from Pakistanâ.




[23] In support of its draft claim, the Government of Pakistan relies upon, and refers to alleged payments of secret commissions by Cotecna a Swiss inspection firm, which at certain times provided custom surveillance and inspection services in Pakistan, and by SociÃ©tÃ© GÃ©nÃ©rale de Surveillance SA (SGS), another Swiss inspection firm, and âparent of Cotecnaâ, refers to criminal investigative proceedings in Switzerland, and to findings, sentencing, confiscation, and other orders made on 30th July 2003 (âSwiss July Orderâ) by an investigating magistrate, Judge Devaud. 




[24] At paragraphs 35 to 90, the Government of Pakistan refers in some detail to ârelevant payments received into the Zardari Swiss bank accountsâ, to the background to the purchase of the Rockwood Estate, the promotion of the Companies, the completion of the purchase, the use of alleged secret commissions, and/or bribes for such purchase, and subsequent refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate, and to Mr. and Mrs. Bancroftâs claim to an interest therein.




[25] In the conclusion part of the draft Particulars, the Government of Pakistan pleads that the receipt of secret commissions and/or bribes by Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari were in breach of their respective duties to Pakistan and/or in breach of trust; further, in the alternative, that Mrs. Bhutto and Mr. Zardari were engaged in corrupt joint enterprises involving the receipt of secret commissions and/or bribes, and that in the premises both are equally and jointly liable. Further, that the secret commissions, or bribes received into the Zardariâs Swiss bank accounts for the benefit of Mrs. Bhutto and/or Mr. Zardari were at all material times held on constructive trust for Pakistan, that the monies and/or property received by the Companies were at all material times held on constructive trust for Pakistan. The Government of Pakistan claims a declaration that it is beneficially entitled to the Rockwood Estate, and to such other assets of the Companies as are found to have derived directly or indirectly from the alleged secret commissions and/ or bribes. 




[26] In the schedules to its draft Particulars, the Government of Pakistan sets out in some detail payments allegedly made in respect of the purchase, and refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate.




Hearing




[27] At the commencement of the hearing, leave was granted for Mr. Zardari to amend paragraph 5 of his Answer, by which amendment he acknowledged that he was the beneficial owner of the Rockwood Estate. To avoid misunderstanding, Mr. White subsequently confirmed that Mr. Zardari admitted that he was the beneficial owner of the Companies. Also, at the commencement of the hearing, leave was given to the Government of Pakistan to amend its Answer so that the same simply read that the Islamic Republic of Pakistan supports the Petition and the relief sought, and the references in the original pleading to the affidavit sworn on 23rd November 2004 (Mrs. Cloughâs affidavit) of Kathryn Louise Clough and to that sworn on 23rd November 2004 of Irfan Qadir were struck from the original Answer. I pause to record that whilst the Government of Pakistan continued to rely for support upon Mrs. Cloughâs affidavit, it had previously indicated that it did not rely upon Mr. Qadirâs affidavit. Following such amendments the Liquidator made consequential amendments to his Reply.








MrClucasâ opening address




[28] In his opening address, Mr. Clucas referred to the Liquidatorâs first affirmation, in which the Liquidator recorded that in, or about March, or April 2001, Mr. Paul Keating in his capacity as a director of Grantbridge Limited, a construction company, approached Maurice Raymond Dorrington, a partner in the Liquidatorâs firm, SPW Poppleton & Appleby, Insolvency Practitioners, of Finchley, London. Mr. Keating informed Mr. Dorrington that Grantbridge Limited had undertaken extensive refurbishment work at the Rockwood Estate during 1996 but had not been paid in full. He further informed him that he had met Dr. William Pepper of International Asset Recovery Limited who then represented the Government of Pakistan. The latter alleged that the Rockwood Estate had been acquired by Mr. Zardari using monies obtained from alleged bribes and secret commissions. Subsequently, Dr. Pepper informed Mr. Dorrington that International Asset Recovery Limited had been appointed by the Government of Pakistan to trace and recover assets which had been obtained by alleged corrupt activity on the part of the previous government of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, of which Mrs. Bhutto had been Prime Minister and in which Mr. Zardari had held ministerial office. It was further alleged that the Rockwood Estate formed part of the assets, which the Government of Pakistan sought to recover. As a result of such discussions, Dr. Pepper, Mr. Keating, and Mr. Dorrington met with Grantbridge Limitedâs English solicitors, Ingram Winter Green. It was agreed that the best way to progress matters was firstly for Grantbridge Limited, as a creditor of the Companies, to apply to restore Romina and Winkford to the Companies Register, such companies having been struck off for failure to comply with statutory filings; secondly, after such restoration, the contributories of each of the Companies would be invited to voluntarily wind up their affairs and appoint the Liquidator as liquidator; and thirdly, the Liquidator, in such capacity, could then sell the Rockwood Estate to realise monies for the benefit of creditors and âappropriate interested partiesâ. I shall refer to such strategy as the âStrategyâ. 




[29] Subsequently, Romina, and Winkford were duly restored to the Companies Register; the Companies resolved to be each wound up voluntarily; and the Liquidator was appointed liquidator of each company.




[30] Mr. Clucas confirmed that the Government of Pakistan had ceased to use International Asset Recovery Limited relevant to its pursuit of the Rockwood Estate and any other assets owned by the Companies. On 29th October 2003, the Liquidator received a letter from Kendall Freeman, solicitors of London, to which there was attached the draft Particulars of Claim, and in which they recorded that they acted for the Government of Pakistan and that from the outset of the liquidations, the Liquidator had been aware of the Government of Pakistanâs proprietary claim to the assets of the Companies. Kendall Freeman placed the Liquidator on formal notice of the Government of Pakistanâs case, and sought that the Liquidator confirm within âthe next three daysâ that the Government of Pakistanâs proprietary claim set out in the draft Particulars of Claim was finally accepted, failing which proceedings would issue without further notice. In a âwithout prejudiceâ letter of even date, Kendall Freeman referred to a discussion the previous day with Mr. Dorrington, and stated that they were preparing a conditional agreement between the Government of Pakistan and the Liquidator âalong the lines discussed with Mr. Dorringtonâ to compromise the Government of Pakistanâs claim, which draft they intended to submit to the Liquidator very quickly with a view to agreeing it in the course of the then next few days. On 4th November 2003, the Liquidator executed the Compromise Agreement. 




[31] I pause to refer to part of paragraph 35, and paragraphs 36, and 37 of the Liquidatorâs first affirmation, which part of paragraph 35 commences after the Liquidator records receipt of the two letters dated 29th October 2003, and which part, and paragraphs 36 and 37 read:-




â35. â¦ I accordingly arranged for Mr Dorrington to meet with the representatives of the ROP and the ROPâs said English solicitors at their offices in London on 31st October 2003 to discuss their claim.
