The compromise under discussion appeared to Mr Dorrington, and subsequently to me, to be in the best interest of the creditors as well as allowing the liquidations to be funded to conclusion but the ROP representatives were told that I would obviously first need to seek the advice of my English solicitors (IWG) to whom a copy of the draft Particulars of Claim and a draft Compromise Agreement was sent on 31st October 2003. Documents were also shown to my solicitors and Mr Dorrington at a meeting on 3rd November 2003 which appeared to verify the movement of monies as claimed in the draft Particulars of Claim. These documents are part of the documents exhibited as bundle âSGT2â. I am informed by Kendall Freeman that Bundle âSGT2â is a complete copy of the documents provided to me and/or my advisers by the ROPâs solicitors in support of the ROPâs draft Particulars of Claim. In the limited time allowed by the ROP to consider their claim, my solicitors and Mr Dorrington were only able to briefly review the documents made available in support of the draft Particulars of Claim. Notwithstanding this my solicitors agreed with the view reached by Mr Dorrington and on his advice by me that there was a significant risk that if ROP issued the intended action it may result in all the creditors and any other interested parties recovering nothing. It is our view that the documents made available by the ROP in support of the draft Particulars of Claim strongly suggested that Mr Zardari is the beneficial owner of the Companies and at all material times up to the liquidation of the Companies Mr Zardari and/or his agents controlled the affairs of the Estate. However the claim identified by the draft Particulars of Claim is a tracing claim and, if pursued by the ROP, either further evidence that fully traces the source of all the monies utilised to purchase the Estate may be required to be obtained by the ROP or the ROP will have to satisfy the Court that this can be inferred from the existing evidence. A key element of the claim that the ROP is beneficially entitled to the Estate is that the monies used to purchase the Estate are ultimately traceable or otherwise derived from secret commissions and/or bribes received by Mr Zardari and/or his wife Mrs. Bhutto when they held ministerial office in a former administration of the ROP. The documents reviewed by my agent and solicitors show that the monies used to purchase the Estate were received from two bank accounts in the name of a company called Bomer Finance Inc (âBomerâ) held at Citibank (Switzerland) SA (âCitibankâ) in Geneva, Switzerland (see SGT2/688-702). Ms Bhutto, Mr. Zardari and Dr Schlegelmilch have been convicted in July 2003 in a Swiss Court for money laundering offences involving the companies referred to in the draft Particulars of Claim, in particular Bomer. At the time that I was presented with the draft Particulars of Claim I was shown a copy of the translation into English of the Sentencing Order of the Swiss Court issued by Investigating Magistrate Devaud on the 30th July 2003 a copy of which is produced at SGT 2/1069-1101. I draw to the attention of the Court the findings of fact made by the Swiss Magistrate that including findings that:-




(i) Bomer is an offshore company having its seat in the British Virgin Islands;


(ii) Bomerâs representative is Dr Schlegelmilch 


(iii) Dr Schlegelmilch was introduced to the Bhutto family in the early 1980s and has been advising the Bhutto family for many years;


(iv) the beneficial owner of Bomer is Mr Zardari, husband of Mrs. Bhutto;


(v) in reality Mrs Bhutto shares with her husband the assets of Bomer over which she has power of disposition;


(vi) USD$8,190,085.00 in commissions ultimately paid by the ROP in relation to contracts granted by the ROP at the behest of Mrs Bhutto and Mr Zardari (but in the face of opposition from the Customs Service of Pakistan) to two Swiss companies were in part laundered to Bomer during the period from May 1995 to September 1997. Whilst I have not seen conclusive evidence that traces the money that was held in Bomerâs Citibank accounts and applied to the purchase or refurbishment of the Estate to illegal commissions or bribes, the findings of the Swiss Magistrate support the view that Bomer is beneficially owned by Mr Zardari and the Bhutto family and has been used by them to launder money at or about the time that monies were transferred from the Bomer Citibank accounts to fund the purchase of the Estate.




I have recently been informed by Kendall Freeman that Mr Zardari, Mrs Bhutto and Dr. Schlegelmilch have lodged an appeal against the Swiss Magistrates decision.




36. In all the circumstances I decided to enter into the terms of the Compromise Agreement with the ROP so as to allow the administration of the affairs of the Companies, and realisation of their assets and protection of the interests of creditors and any interested parties. However this action was expressly subject to the sanction of this Honourable Court.




37. Accordingly, the parties negotiated the final terms of the Compromise Agreement (see SGT1/282-287) which is conditional upon the sanction of this Honourable Court being obtained on the 4th November 2003. I was also asked to provide a side letter agreeing to preserve the assets of the Companies until Court sanction was obtained (page âSGT1/288-289) in order to give the ROP the appropriate comfort they required.â

Issues and Conclusions




[94] I refer to the List of Issues. Issue 1, reads:




â1. What is the correct legal test for the Court to apply:




(i) in relation to permitting the Liquidator to seek relief in the circumstances under section 240 Companies Act 1931;


(ii) in granting leave to the Liquidator to use the powers granted under section184(1)[e] Companies Act 1931;


and what are the powers of the Court on this application under section 240?â




[95] The Companies are each in membersâ voluntary liquidation. Under section 236(1)(a) Companies Act 1931 (the Act) in a members' voluntary winding up, a liquidator has authority with the sanction of an extraordinary resolution of the company to compromise claims by creditors, or persons claiming to be creditors, or persons having, or alleging themselves to have any claim, present, or future, certain, or contingent, ascertained, or sounding only in damages against the company, or whereby the company may be liable. None of the Companies have passed the requisite extraordinary resolution to sanction the Compromise Agreement: the Liquidator has petitioned this Court pursuant to section 240 of the Act seeking that this Court sanction the Compromise Agreement, and/or authorise the Liquidator to enter into, and implement the terms of the same.




[96] Section 240 of the Act provides:




âPower to apply to court to have questions determined or powers exercised




(1) The liquidator or any contributory or creditor may apply to the court to determine any question arising in the winding up of a company, or to exercise, as respects the enforcing of calls, or any other matter, all or any of the powers which the court might exercise if the company were being wound up by the court.




(2) The court, if satisfied that the determination of the question or the required exercise of power will be just and beneficial, may accede wholly or partially to the application on such terms and conditions as it thinks fit, or may make such other order on the application as it thinks justâ.




[97] Upon application by the liquidator, any contributory, or creditor in a membersâ voluntary winding up, the court has power under section 240(1) of the Act to exercise any power, which the court could exercise if the company was being wound up by the court. I refer to Palmers Company Law Fifteenth Edition, and that part at page 456, which reads:




âPowers of Liquidators.




â¦If [the liquidator] requires at any time the advice or protection of the Court, he can apply for it under section 252 [Companies Act 1929 of Parliament]; and he constantly does so â¦in such matters as borrowing, bringing or defending actions, the making of calls, the taking of misfeasance proceedings, compromises,adjusting the rights of contributories, adjudicating on disputed claims, settling the list of contributories, the payment of dividends, and many other like matters. Any contributory, or creditor, or the liquidator may also apply under the section to the Court.â




Section 252 Companies Act 1929 of Parliament had the same wording as section 240 of the Act.




[98] In this case the Liquidator is not seeking that this Court determines any question, but that it exercises the power given under section 184(1)(e) of the Act in the case of a winding up by the court, that is that this Court sanctions the Compromise Agreement, and/or authorises the Liquidator to enter into, and implement the terms of the same.




[99] What is the correct test for the Court to apply when presented with such an application?




[100] In a voluntary winding up, whether members', or creditors', upon application under section 240(1) by a liquidator, a contributory or creditor, section 240(2) determines the courtâs role. The court must first determine whether it is satisfied that it is just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power. If it is so satisfied, then the court has a discretion to determine whether it accedes in whole, or in part to the application, and what, if any, terms, and conditions ought to be imposed. If the court is not satisfied that it is just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power, then the court can make such other order on the application as it thinks just.




[101] In determining whether to exercise the requisite power, and if so, whether in whole, or partially, and whether subject to any terms, and/or conditions, the court may be assisted by factors recognised as relevant to the exercise of the relevant power in a winding up by the court: in this case by factors relevant to the exercise of the courtâs power under section 184(1)(e) of the Act. As to the exercise of such power, I refer to Re Greenhaven Motors Limited (in liquidation) [1999] 1 BCLC 635 (Court of Appeal) and in particular that part of the judgment of Chadwick LJ at 642a to 643h. However, although the court may be assisted by such factors, when determining an application under section 240, it cannot be constrained by the same. 




[102] Issue 2 reads:




â2. What is Mr. Zardariâs locus standi in the proceedings and/or in the liquidations and what weight, if any, should the Court give to his views.â






[103] An application under section 240 of the Act may be made by the Liquidator, a creditor, or any contributory. There is no evidence before me that Mr. Zardari is, or claims to be a creditor of the Companies, or any one of them. I refer to section 157 of the Act. There is no evidence that Mr. Zardari comes within the definition of a contributory, or a person, who would be deemed to be a contributory. I accept that under section 102 of the Act trusts are not to be entered on the register of members. I further accept that the courts have refused to join beneficiaries of trusts as parties to proceedings, which seek that companies be wound up, and in which companies the subject trusts are directly, or indirectly the shareholders, see judgment of His Honour Acting Deemster Teare, QC of 10th March 2005 in Re Colombo Investments Limited, Haynes Limited, Law Investments Limited, Mercury Consultants Limited and United Consultants Limited. 




[104] In this case, Mr. Zardari does not make application under section 240 of the Act. He is a party to these proceedings, and has been a party since the commencement thereof. Whilst Mr. Zardari may not have locus standi in the liquidations, he is party to these proceedings, and as such has locus standi in the same. The real question to be addressed is what weight should this Court give to the evidence, arguments and submissions by, or on behalf of Mr. Zardari.




[105] I accept, as argued by Mr. Clucas, and Mr. Morris, the denials maintained for some considerable time for, and on behalf of Mr. Zardari as to his beneficial ownership of the Companies, see for example Mrs Cloughâs affidavit at paragraph 35 with exhibits referred to therein. I further accept that during the liquidations, and other proceedings, there have been claims by Dr. Schlegelmilch, and others as to the beneficial ownership being vested in Mr. Oswais. I also accept that Mr. Zardariâs affidavit sworn on 17th December 2004 merely confirms that he has read Mr. Bakarâs first, and second affidavits, and agrees with their contents. I accept Mr. Morrisâ criticisms of the very limited participation of Mr. Zardari in the evidential enquiries; that Mr. Zardariâs pleadings are not open, and frank as to his true position relevant to the Companies; that discovery on his part has been minimal; and that it was due only to clarification by Mr. White during the hearing that the Liquidator, and the Government of Pakistan were informed in clear, and unequivocal terms that Mr. Zardari was not only the beneficial owner of the Rockwood Estate, but also of the Companies. I appreciate that as beneficial owner, Mr. Zardari could have requested the registered contributories of the Companies to fully participate in these proceedings and to oppose the relief sought; that Tower Trustees Limited, and Mr. Shimmin have taken a neutral stance, and that the contributories in Romina have been passive throughout these proceedings. However, in considering the weight to be given to Mr Zardariâs views, I must bear in mind that he has admitted, albeit at a late stage, but without limitation, that he is the beneficial owner of the Companies. 




[106] In his arguments, Mr. Clucas speculated as to the potential for another person to claim beneficial ownership of the Companies. On the evidence before me, absent, for this purpose only, Mr. Zardariâs admissions as to beneficial ownership, it appears that the beneficial ownership of the Companies vests in either Mr. Zardari, or Mrs. Bhutto with the greater probability being Mr Zardari. The implementation of the Compromise Agreement would lead to the contributories, and thus beneficial owner of the Companies receiving nothing from the liquidations. It may be considered that even if beneficial ownership does vest in an unrelated third party, Mr. Zardariâs opposition to the relief sought under the Petition is not detrimental to the interests of any such beneficial owner.




[107] In considering the weight to give to the evidence, arguments and submissions for, and on behalf of Mr. Zardari, I refer to those parts of the judgment of Chadwick LJ in Re Greenhaven MotorsLimited at page 642 paragraphs e to g and 643 paragraphs d and f, which parts read:-




ââ¦ The court may, and usually will, take into account the views of someone claiming to be a creditor or contributory, but it is not bound by those views. If the claim appears thin, or the claimant can be seen to have no real interest in the assets having regard to prior claims, his views may carry little weight. I would think it inappropriate for the court to embark, in the context of an application under s 167(1)(a) of the Act, on a detailed examination of the question whether a person wishing to be heard is indeed a creditor or a contributory. The circumstances in the present case demonstrate that such examination is likely to prove inconclusive. I think it is sufficient that the court should be satisfied that the claim is made bona fide and it is not plainly misconceived. If the claimant satisfies that test, then he should be heard. It remains a matter for the court what weight should be given to his wishes.


â¦


In deciding whether or not to sanction a proposed compromise the court must consider whether the interests of those, whether creditors or contributories, who have a real interest in the assets of a company in liquidation are likely to be best served â¦




In reaching that decision, the court may have to weigh the different interests of creditors and contributories and, perhaps, the different interests of preferential and non-preferential creditors. It will not give weight to the wishes of those who will be unaffected whichever way the decision goes;â.




[108] I also refer to that part of the judgment of Sir Andrew Morritt V-C in Re Barings Plc (in liquidation) (No. 7) (2002) 1 BCLC 401 at 412 paragraph 39, which reads:-




â [39] Accordingly, whichever way one approaches the problem the preference shareholders interest is theoretical only. They have no commercial or economic interest in the assets of PLC available for distribution in the liquidation.â




[109] In this case, Mr. Zardari undoubtedly has a real interest in the Companies and their underlying assets, his interest is not merely theoretical. Further, he is a person, who will be affected by the outcome of these proceedings. I am satisfied that his participation in these proceedings is bona fide, and not misconceived. In the circumstances, I consider that the evidence, arguments, and submissions on the part of Mr. Zardari should be given due weight.




[110] I refer to Issues 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8 which read:




â[3] Is there evidence upon which it was just and reasonable for the Liquidator to form a view that:-




(i) the funds to purchase the Rockwood Properties were paid by Bomer Finance Inc;




(ii) Bomer Finance Inc was beneficially owned by Mr Zardari and/or by his wife Benazir Bhutto;

[8] The costs of the Petition including the costs of the Noticed Party Tower Trustees Limited as Trustee of the Winkford and Parsonage Farm Trust more particularly sought in that Noticed Partyâs Cross-Petition dated 13th February 2005.â




[111] I refer to Issue 3, and in particular sub-paragraphs (i), (iii), and (iv). 




[112] I refer to the above paragraph [70], and Mr Whiteâs referral of the Liquidator in some detail to paragraphs 20 to 22 of Mr Bakarâs first affidavit. The Liquidator responded to such paragraphs at paragraph 5 of his Reply, the relevant part of which reads:-




ââ¦ save and except for the statements within Mr Bakarâs Affidavit at paragraphs 20 to 22. In respect of these latter statements your Petitioner avers that the evidence provided by the GOP (in particular the evidence in support of paragraphs 36 and 37 of the GOPâs draft Particulars of Claim) provide a sufficient basis for your Petitionerâs decisions to enter into the Compromise Agreement.â




[113] It is to be noted that the Liquidator refers in particular to paragraphs 36 and 37 of the draft Particulars of Claim. The introductory paragraph 35 and paragraphs 36, 37.1, 37.2, 37.3 and 37.4 read:-




â35. In this claim, Pakistan relies on the following payments received into the Zardari Swiss Bank Accounts.




36. The following relevant payments (used among other things for the purchase of the Rockwood Estate, as hereinafter pleaded) were received into the Bomer Citibank Account:

36.2 On or around 22 August 1995 a payment of $1.2 million was made by order of Mr Al-Assir, from Citibank New York.




36.3 On or around 1 September 1995 a payment of $1.8 million was made by order of Mr Al-Assir, from Citibank New York.




37. The aforementioned payments received from Mr Al-Assir (mentioned at paragraphs 36.2 and 36.3) constituted secret commissions or bribes. As to this:

In the premises, the said payments of $1 million and $325,000 (which were in fact paid into the Bomer UBS Account on or around 15 and 30 August 1994, respectively, and constituted the first payments received into the Bomer UBS Account and indeed the first payments to Bomer of which Pakistan is aware) represent secret commissions or bribes, and it is to be inferred that that (sic) subsequent payments from Mr Al-Assir to Bomer are similarly tainted. Indeed, Mr Schlegelmilch also stated (at the same oral examination) that Bomer had also been used to collect funds originating from other contracts with Mr Al-Assir, regarding other countries, which funds had been paid into the Bomer Citibank Account. 37.3 Further, in the Swiss Proceedings, one of the issues the investigating magistrate has raised and required evidence about relates to payments made by Mr Al-Assir to Bomer, with a view to establishing whether those payments were the product of corruption. As to paragraph 36.1, there is no evidence as to the transferee. The payments referred to in paragraph 37.2 do not relate to the payments received in paragraph 36.2 or 36.3. Further, there is no evidence whether in paragraph 37 or elsewhere which identifies Mr. Al-Assir or identifies any transaction with which he is concerned. The remainder of paragraph 37 contains no further evidence about the source of payments particularised at paragraph 36. Whilst I accept that other payments referred to in the draft Particulars of Claim might be linked to alleged corrupt practices, there is no evidence that the payments referred to in paragraph 36, upon which reliance is placed, derive from corrupt activity. Further, it is not sufficient in proceedings as those in this case to make inferences such as those made at paragraph 37.4.




[114] I also refer to that part of paragraph 35 of the Liquidatorâs first affirmation which reads:-




ââ¦. A key element of the claim that the ROP is beneficially entitled to the Estate is that the monies used to purchase the Estate are ultimately traceable or otherwise derived from secret commissions and/or bribes received by Mr Zardari and/or his wife Mrs Bhutto when they held ministerial office in a former administration of the ROP.â




In evidence, above paragraph [71], the Liquidator accepted that there was no evidence to link the monies used to purchase the Rockwood Estate with corruption. 




[115] I also refer to those parts at paragraph 35 of the Liquidatorâs first affirmation which read:-




â35. â¦ I draw to the attention of the Court the findings of fact made by the Swiss Magistrate that include findings that:-


â¦â¦â¦.. 


(vi) USD$8,190,085.00 in commissions ultimately paid by the ROP in relation to contracts granted by the ROP at the behest of Mrs Bhutto and Mr Zardari (but in the face of opposition from the Customs Service of Pakistan) to two Swiss companies were in part laundered to Bomer during the period from May 1995 to September 1997. 




Whilst I have not seen conclusive evidence that traces the money that was held in Bomerâs Citibank accounts and applied to the purchase or refurbishment of the Estate to illegal commissions or bribes, the findings of the Swiss Magistrate support the view that Bomer is beneficially owned by Mr Zardari and the Bhutto family and has been used by them to launder money at or about the time that monies were transferred from the Bomer Citibank accounts to fund the purchase of the Estate.â




The Liquidator accepted that the reference to the âtwo Swiss companiesâ was a reference to Cotecna and SGS; and that monies allegedly paid by such companies had nothing to do with the purchase of Rockwood Estate.The Liquidator further acknowledged that he had attached considerable significance to the Swiss July Order, including the findings therein. On the day that the Liquidator executed the Compromise Agreement, the Misdemeanours Court annulled the Swiss July Order. The Liquidator stated that he had not personally reconsidered, or re-assessed the Government of Pakistanâs claim upon becoming aware that the Swiss July Order had been annulled, but that Mr. Dorrington may have done. I record that there was no evidence that Mr Dorrington, or indeed anyone else had undertaken any such reconsideration, or re-assessment. 




[116] In his evidence, the Liquidator confirmed that exhibit SGT2 to the Liquidatorâs first affirmation contained all material available to him at the date of execution of the Compromise Agreement, and indicated that, although he had seen the draft Particulars of Claim, he had not personally reviewed the documents under exhibit SGT2. I refer to paragraph 35 of the Liquidatorâs first affirmation. With the exception of such paragraph, there was no evidence that the Liquidator met with Mr. Dorrington prior to his execution of the Compromise Agreement. However, even if he had, I would question the worth of any advice given by Mr Dorrington. It will be recalled that it was Mr Dorrington, who was first consulted by Grantbridge Limited, and the Government of Pakistan. Also, whilst it may be considered that the Government of Pakistanâs draft proof of debt prepared by Mr. Dorrington relied upon the Swiss July Order, the same contained inaccuracies, and indicates that Mr. Dorringtonâs independence must be at the least questionable, if not biased towards the Government of Pakistan. There was no evidence that the Liquidator met with, or received advice from Mr. Popat, before entering into the Compromise Agreement. Indeed, Mr Popat had not had the benefit of inspecting the documents allegedly in support of the Government of Pakistanâs claim, and as recorded by Mr. Popat, was faced with the uncompromising stance taken by the Government of Pakistan through its solicitors Kendall Freeman. The evidence adduced conclusively establishes that the Liquidator took no legal advice relevant to the complex proprietary claim of the Government of Pakistan before signing the Compromise Agreement. In the absence of any such advice, the Liquidatorâs decision must be flawed. In support, I refer to Faryab âv- Smith (2001) BPIR 246. Although that case involved bankruptcy proceedings, the principles applied were drawn from a corporate insolvency case, see paragraph 33 of the judgment, in which Robert Walker LJ referred to that part of the judgment of Nourse LJ in Re Edennote Limited (1996) 2 BCLC 389 in which the learned judge stated that a reasonable liquidator must be taken to be one who is properly advised. As in the case of Faryab, in a matter such as that of the Intended Action by the Government of Pakistan, which involves the evaluation of a complex claim, the Liquidator ought to have obtained independent legal advice before reaching his conclusion whether to enter into the Compromise Agreement. In this case the Liquidator obtained no such advice. Indeed, the evidence leads to the conclusion, which was accepted by Mr. Clucas, that the first time the Liquidator saw the Compromise Agreement was when he executed the same.




[117] In cross examination, the Liquidator confirmed that exhibit SGT2 to his first affirmation contained all material available to him at the date of execution of the Compromise Agreement, and that, although he had seen the draft Particulars of Claim, he had not personally reviewed the documents under exhibit SGT2. However, in re-examination the Liquidator referred to the IARL statement. Whilst I accept that the Liquidator had had benefit of the IARL statement, such report contains no evidence tracing alleged corruption through to the monies used in the purchase of the Rockwood Estate.




[118] I am mindful of the proceedings in the Bow Street Magistrates Court and evidence given by Mr. Howard, , including that relevant to his appreciation of the source of the purchase monies, the source of payment of his legal fees, references to Nassam Overseas Inc. and to Bennington Management Inc.




[119] Whilst I accept that the claim by the Government of Pakistan constitutes a significant risk to the assets that are the subject of the liquidations, having carefully considered the evidence, and in particular the matters referred to at paragraphs [112] to [117], I cannot conclude that there is evidence upon which it was just and reasonable for the Liquidator to form a view that the monies used by Bomer Finance Inc and others to finance the purchase, and refurbishment of the Rockwood Estate were obtained as a result of the illegal acts of corruption whilst Mr Zardari held government office in Pakistan. 




[120] I refer to Issue 4, namely in determining the Republic of Pakistanâs case and the evidence in support thereof and in pursuing this application has the Liquidator been unduly partial to the Republic of Pakistanâs position either as a result of funding arrangements between the Republic of Pakistan and the Liquidator and/or creditors of the liquidation or otherwise?




[121] I agree with Mr. White that the Liquidator is clearly partial, and has been unduly influenced at all material times by the Government of Pakistan and its representatives. 




[122] I refer to the Liquidatorâs report of 18th July 2003, see paragraph [40] above, and the Strategy devised by the Liquidatorâs firm for their then clients Grantbridge Limited, and the Government of Pakistan. The solicitors involved in devising the Strategy were Ingram Winter Green, which firm was then advising Grantbridge Limited. Despite the Liquidator seeking to distance himself from the Strategy, he confirmed that Grantbridge Limited, and the Government of Pakistan were clients of his firm, which developed the three part strategy. The Liquidator also confirmed that the object of the Strategy was to sell the Rockwood Estate, and realise the claims of the Government of Pakistan, Grantbridge Limited and other creditors. The Liquidator accepted that the first two stages of the Strategy had been financed by International Asset Recovery Limited. It is clear that the Petition is the culmination of the Strategy.




[123] Prior to entering into the Compromise Agreement, the Liquidator did not properly consider, if at all, the Government of Pakistanâs claim. He did not consider any documents allegedly in support thereof. He did not take any legal advice. Indeed, the first time he saw the same, was when he executed it.




[124] The Liquidator agreed with Mr White that subsequent to the execution of the Compromise Agreement, and Side Letter, he was totally reliant upon the Government of Pakistan for funding, and that unless the Government of Pakistan was satisfied with the progress of matters, funding would not be available. The Liquidator further agreed that since the sale of the Rockwood Estate, all payments to creditors, legal advisers, and others had required the Government of Pakistanâs authority under the terms of the Compromise Agreement. The evidence leads to the unavoidable conclusion that throughout the Liquidator has been beholden for funding to, and influenced by the Government of Pakistan such that steps taken in the liquidations by the Liquidator have been devoid of the necessary impartiality, and objectivity, which the court expects from a liquidator. In particular, by the Side Letter, the Liquidator unconditionally bound himself to undertake certain courses at the behest of the Government of Pakistan. 




[125] The evidence further reveals that the Liquidatorâs pleadings, and affirmations are clearly open to material challenge. I refer to the above paragraph [61], and the referral of the Liquidator to paragraphs 18, and 19 of the Petition. When Mr. White challenged the Liquidator relevant to such paragraphs, there was a long and pregnant silence with no response given by the Liquidator. The description in the Liquidatorâs second affirmation at paragraph 16(vii) of payment of a sum of £10,000 âinterestâ to Mr. Essex is clearly untrue. Further, such payment was paid out of liquidation funds pursuant to an agreement made initially by International Asset Recovery Limited, then by Mr Nawaz, and confirmed by Kendall Freeman.




[126] Whilst I accept that the Liquidator cannot be expected to remember particulars of each, and every claim, I find that his responses to challenges to the increase in the amounts claimed by Grantbridge Limited in the liquidations compared to those claimed in its proceedings against Mr. Pasha, to have been limited, general, and evasive. Further, in his responses concerning the âproblemsâ, which led Mr. Dorrington not to seek renewal of his insolvency practitionerâs licence, I found the Liquidatorâs responses again evasive. In response to other challenges by Mr White, the Liquidator maintained silence, or gave a âno commentâ answer.




[127] A review of the evidence, particularly that given by the Liquidator in cross-examination, leads me to conclude that he has not discharged his duties, and obligations in an independent, objective, and impartial manner. He has placed reliance, indeed perhaps over-reliance, upon Mr. Dorrington. 




[128] I find that, in determining the Government of Pakistanâs case, and the evidence in support thereof, and in pursuing this application the Liquidator has been unduly partial to the Government of Pakistanâs position. 




[129] I again refer to Re Greenhaven Motors Limited and that part of the judgment of Chadwick LJ in which he agreed with the approach identified by Lightman J in Re Edennote Limited (No. 2) (1997) 2 BCLC 89 at 92 in which the latter said:-




ââ Where a liquidator seeks the sanction of the court and takes the view that a compromise is in the best interest of the creditors, in any ordinary case, where (as in this case) there is no suggestion of lack of good faith by the liquidator or that he is partisan the court will attach considerable weight to the liquidatorâs views unless the evidence reveals substantial reasons why it should not do so, or that for some reason or other his view is flawed.ââ




[130] Whilst the decision whether to sanction the Compromise Agreement is a decision for the Court, and not a decision for the Liquidator, the Court will usually place considerable weight upon the liquidatorâs views unless there is a suggestion that he lacks good faith, or is partisan, or his view is flawed or for some other reason the court concludes that it should not do so. In this case, I agree with Mr. White that the Liquidatorâs decision is of little, if any, value. 




[131] I refer to the provisions of section 240(2) of the Act. 




[132] I readily accept that, other than challenging the Liquidator, and the Government of Pakistan, Mr. Zardari has not sought in these proceedings to identify the original source of the monies used to purchase and/or to refurbish the Rockwood Estate. 




[133] Mr. Morris referred to Mr. Zardariâs mere denials of corruption, and challenged Mr. Bakarâs assertions that Mr. Zardari was precluded from dealing with the specifics of the issues due to the ongoing investigations in Switzerland, or Pakistan on the grounds of sub judice. In response, Mr. White referred to paragraph 12 of the affidavit sworn on 9th February 2005 of Syed Ali Bin Adam Jafri, advocate of Karachi, Pakistan, the relevant part of which paragraph reads:-




â12. I say and opine that with reference to query at Sub-Para (c) of Para 2 above, in Pakistan the Government has instituted Reference No. 14/2001 wherein inter-alia allegation is that Mr. Asif Ali Zardari has acquired Rockwood Estate from money acquired through corruption and corrupt practices. The action which started in 1998, is still pending and it would be subjudice to give specific details at this stage in any proceeding outside Pakistani jurisdiction.â




[134] Given the history of, and ongoing proceedings in one form, or another by the Government of Pakistan against Mr. Zardari, and his period of incarceration. I can appreciate the cautious approach adopted by Mr. Zardari, and his advisers in these proceedings.




[135] It was accepted by Mr. Clucas that there are extant proceedings in Pakistan involving the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, which proceedings commenced in 1998, and the subject matter of which includes the acquisition of the Rockwood Estate. Mr. White strongly argued that the correct forum for the Government of Pakistan to seek to prove allegations of corruption against Mr. Zardari was before the courts of Pakistan. He supported such arguments by reference to the assertions that the alleged corrupt practices took place whilst Mr. Zardari held office in Pakistan; that the relevant laws are those of Pakistan; and to the availability of witnesses, and documentation in that jurisdiction. Whilst I can appreciate the force of such arguments, for my present purposes, I merely note the same.




[136] Mr. White identified certain matters, which he maintained ought to have concerned the Liquidator, and should concern this Court. He referred to the previous assertion by the Government of Pakistan that the Rockwood Estate was acquired by proceeds of drug trafficking, to the granting of a restraining order in this jurisdiction reliant upon allegations of such illicit activity, and to the discharge of such restraining order in 2002. In this case, Mr. White reminded this Court, the Government of Pakistan relies upon allegations not of drug trafficking but of corruption, he challenged what new evidence has been forthcoming since 2002. Mr. White again referred to two convictions of Mr. Zardari for corruption by courts in Pakistan, and that one such conviction had been overturned by the Supreme Court on the grounds of improper government interference, and the other overturned on the grounds of insufficient evidence.




[137] I appreciate that the claim by the Government of Pakistan is a proprietary claim, and presents a significant risk to the assets of the Companies. I further appreciate that, when presented with the draft Particulars of Claim, and threatened action, the Liquidator had no funds, with which to challenge the same. I accept that the Government of Pakistan has made a claim as a creditor in the liquidations, which claim has yet to be determined. 




[138] Whilst it may be easy to criticise the time restraints imposed upon the Liquidator by the Government of Pakistan for consideration of the draft Particulars of Claim, and the Compromise Agreement, I consider that, in circumstances such as this case, a prudent, objective, and independent liquidator would have not succumbed to such pressure, but would have insisted that he was given adequate time to properly consider the claim with the supporting documentation, to take appropriate advice, including most important, legal advice, and to fully consult with others, including the contributories.




[139] I am mindful of the interests of creditors. I am further mindful that, if I do not sanction the Compromise Agreement, and, if the Government of Pakistan is ultimately successful in the pursuit of its proprietary claim, the latter might seek to recoup monies paid to creditors by the Liquidator.




[140] I am aware that if I do not exercise the requested power, and the Government of Pakistan issue proceedings, then absent new funding, the Liquidator will have insufficient funds to oppose, or challenge such proceedings, and further that at present, whilst there may be what could be viewed as negative evidence, or challenges, there is little, if any, positive evidence to challenge such proprietary claim. I agree with Mr. Morris that in these proceedings, it is not for this Court to adjudicate upon the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, but to confine itself to the relief sought in the Petition. 




[141] Before sanctioning a compromise, the Court must be satisfied as to the facts on which it is based â see paragraph 584 Halsburyâs Volume 7(3) Fourth Edition. 




[142] Upon application under section 240, the Court must be satisfied that the required exercise of the power will be just, and beneficial before it accedes in whole, or part to the application. I bear in mind the above paragraphs [100] et seq, particularly paragraphs [119], [128], and [130]. I am mindful of the subject allegations of the intended action by the Government of Pakistan, and, if the same is successful, the potential significant consequences, particularly for the creditors. I am mindful of the proceedings between the Government of Pakistan, and Mr. Zardari, and particularly those extant in Pakistan. I bear in mind the lack of any real consideration by the Liquidator of the draft Particulars of Claim with the supporting documents, and of the Compromise Agreement before entering into the same. I have carefully considered the evidence. I am not satisfied that, in the circumstances of this case, it would be just, and beneficial to exercise the required power, and to sanction the Compromise Agreement or to authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the same. I decline to exercise the required power.




[143] Under section 240(2), if the Court is not satisfied that it will be just, and beneficial to exercise the requisite power, it may make such other orders on the application, in this case the Petition, as it thinks just. I look to Counsel to assist as to what orders, if any, I should make consequential upon my refusal to sanction the terms of the Compromise Agreement or to authorise the Liquidator to enter into and implement the terms of the same.




[144] With reference to costs, including costs of the Noticed Party Tower Trustees Limited sought in that partyâs Cross Petition dated 13th February 2005, I await applications, and arguments.
court: HIGH COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ISLE OF MAN
